**(b)** Write a Python function that uses backtracking to solve a Sudoku puzzle. Explain how the `is_valid` function works in your implementation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Sudoku as a CSP:**

A standard $9 \times 9$ Sudoku grid has 81 cells. We model each empty cell as a variable.

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= \{x_{ij} \mid 1 \leq i \leq 9, 1 \leq j \leq 9\} \\
D_{ij} &= \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9\} \quad \text{if cell } (i,j) \text{ is empty} \\
D_{ij} &= \{\text{pre-filled value}\} \quad \text{if cell } (i,j) \text{ is given}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Types of Constraints ($C$):**

1. **Row Constraint (Alldiff)**: All cells in the same row must contain distinct digits. There are 9 such constraints.
$$\forall i : \text{alldiff}(x_{i1}, x_{i2}, \ldots, x_{i9})$$

2. **Column Constraint (Alldiff)**: All cells in the same column must contain distinct digits. There are 9 such constraints.
$$\forall j : \text{alldiff}(x_{1j}, x_{2j}, \ldots, x_{9j})$$

3. **Sub-grid Constraint (Alldiff)**: All cells in the same $3 \times 3$ sub-grid must contain distinct digits. There are 9 such constraints.
$$\forall (p, q) \in \text{sub-grids} : \text{alldiff}(\{x_{ij} \mid i \in [3p, 3p+2], j \in [3q, 3q+2]\})$$

**Total Constraints**: $9 + 9 + 9 = 27$ all-different constraints.

*(3 marks for CSP triple, 4 marks for constraint types with equations)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Python Implementation with Backtracking:**

```python
def solve_sudoku(board):
    empty = find_empty(board)
    if not empty:
        return True  # Puzzle solved
    row, col = empty
    for num in range(1, 10):
        if is_valid(board, num, (row, col)):
            board[row][col] = num
            if solve_sudoku(board):
                return True
            board[row][col] = 0  # BACKTRACK
    return False

def is_valid(board, num, pos):
    row, col = pos
    # Check row
    for j in range(9):
        if board[row][j] == num and j != col:
            return False
    # Check column
    for i in range(9):
        if board[i][col] == num and i != row:
            return False
    # Check 3x3 sub-grid
    box_x, box_y = col // 3, row // 3
    for i in range(box_y * 3, box_y * 3 + 3):
        for j in range(box_x * 3, box_x * 3 + 3):
            if board[i][j] == num and (i, j) != pos:
                return False
    return True
```

**Explanation of `is_valid` Function:**

The `is_valid` function checks three types of constraints for a candidate number `num` at position `(row, col)`:

1. **Row Check**: Iterates through all 9 columns of the given row. If `num` is found in any other column, the placement violates the row all-different constraint → return `False`.

2. **Column Check**: Iterates through all 9 rows of the given column. If `num` is found in any other row, the placement violates the column all-different constraint → return `False`.

3. **Sub-grid Check**: Computes the top-left corner of the $3 \times 3$ sub-grid containing `(row, col)` using integer division `box_x = col // 3` and `box_y = row // 3`. Then iterates through all 9 cells in that sub-grid. If `num` is found elsewhere in the sub-grid, the constraint is violated → return `False`.

The function returns `True` only if `num` can be legally placed at `(row, col)` without violating any Sudoku constraint. This is the **constraint check** that enables early pruning in backtracking.

*(3 marks for code, 4 marks for explanation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to undo the assignment during backtracking**: This is the most common error. Students often write `board[row] = col` but forget `board[row] = -1` or `board[row][col] = 0` after the recursive call. **Result**: The next iteration sees a stale value and the algorithm produces wrong answers. (Loss: 2-3 marks)
> 2. **Confusing row-column indexing**: In N-Queens, `board[i] = col` means the queen in row `i` is at column `col`. Mixing up rows and columns leads to nonsensical constraint checks.
> 3. **Not drawing the state-space tree**: KTU examiners often give 2-3 extra marks for a neat state-space tree diagram, even in code questions. Always include one.
> 4. **Omitting the base case**: Forgetting `if row == N: return True` in N-Queens causes infinite recursion. For Sudoku, the equivalent is `if not empty: return True`.
> 5. **Writing `is_safe` with global variables**: Always pass `board` and `row`/`col` as function arguments, never as globals. Examiners deduct marks for poor encapsulation.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU PCADL407 Lab Exam:**

- ✅ **CSP Definition**: $\langle X, D, C \rangle$ — Variables, Domains, Constraints. Memorize this triple.
- ✅ **Backtracking = DFS + Pruning**: Backtracking is essentially Depth-First Search with early termination when constraints are violated.
- ✅ **Three Operations**: Choose → Check → Recurse/Backtrack. Every backtracking function follows this pattern.
- ✅ **N-Queens Complexity**: Worst case $O(n!)$, but with heuristics it becomes tractable even for $N = 1000+$.
- ✅ **Constraint Types**: Unary (single variable), Binary (two variables), Global (alldiff, sum, etc.). Sudoku uses only global `alldiff` constraints.
- ✅ **Forward Checking**: Removes inconsistent values from future domains. Reduces search space exponentially.
- ✅ **MRV Heuristic**: "Fail-fast" strategy. Choose the most constrained variable first.
- ✅ **LCV Heuristic**: "Succeed-fast" strategy. Choose the value that constrains the future the least.
- ✅ **Chronological Backtracking**: Undoes the most recent assignment. Simple but can be inefficient.
- ✅ **Arc Consistency (AC-3)**: A stronger form of inference that ensures every pair of connected variables has at least one consistent value pairing.
- ✅ **Complete vs Optimal**: Backtracking is **complete** (finds a solution if one exists) but not necessarily **optimal** (doesn't guarantee minimum-cost solution).
- ✅ **Code Template for N-Queens**: `is_safe()` → `solve(board, row)` → recursive call with `board[row+1]` → undo on failure.
- ✅ **Code Template for Sudoku**: `find_empty()` → `is_valid(num, pos)` → recursive call → undo with `0` on failure.
- ✅ **Lab Viva Questions to Prepare**: "What is the difference between backtracking and branch-and-bound?", "Why is backtracking preferred over brute force?", "How does forward checking improve backtracking?", "What is the role of the AC-3 algorithm?"

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementation of Alpha-Beta Pruning to Find Optimal Solution for a Given Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCADL407 - FOUNDATIONS OF AI AND DATA SCIENCE LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Implementation of Alpha-Beta Pruning to Find Optimal Solution for a Given Problem # Module Experiment 2 — Alpha-Beta Pruning ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Alpha-Beta Pruning** is an *adversarial search* optimization technique applied to the **Minimax algorithm** for two-player, **zero-sum**, **perfect-information** games. It prunes (eliminates) subtrees in the game tree whose exploration cannot possibly influence the final decision, by maintaining two bounding parameters: **α (alpha)** — the best value the *Maximizer* can guarantee so far, and **β (beta)** — the best value the *Minimizer* can guarantee so far. The pruning condition is **α ≥ β** (also written as **β ≤ α**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (CO Mapping):** > This experiment maps directly to **CO2 – *Apply search and optimization techniques to solve AI problems*** under the **K4 (Apply)** cognitive level. Students are expected to **implement, trace, and verify** the optimal move computed by alpha-beta pruning against the plain minimax result on the same game tree. ### 1.2 Intuition – A Real-World Analogy Imagine you are **negotiating the price of a used car** with a seller. You (the *Maximizer*) want the **lowest price**; the seller (the *Minimizer*) wants the **highest price**. You have a list of three dealers you can call. - You call **Dealer A** first and they quote ₹4,00,000. Now you have a *ceiling* of ₹4,00,000. - You call **Dealer B**, who begins negotiating. After hearing the first offer is ₹5,50,000, you *immediately hang up* — you already have a cheaper option at ₹4,00,000, and this dealer will never go below that, so continuing the conversation is a **waste of time**. That "hanging up" is exactly what alpha-beta pruning does — it **stops exploring a branch the moment it knows the branch cannot improve the current decision**. > [!NOTE] > **The Two Parameters at a Glance** > > | Symbol | Name | Role | Who updates it? | > |--------|------|------|-----------------| > | **α (alpha)** | Lower bound for MAX | Best value MAX is assured of | MAX nodes | > | **β (beta)** | Upper bound for MIN | Best value MIN is assured of | MIN nodes | > | **α ≥ β** | Pruning condition | Stop exploring current branch | Both nodes | ### 1.3 Why Alpha-Beta Pruning Matters | Metric | Plain Minimax | With Alpha-Beta (Best-Case) | |--------|---------------|------------------------------| | **Time Complexity** | **O(b^d)** | **O(b^(d/2))** | | **Space Complexity** | **O(bd)** | **O(bd)** | | **Optimality** | Optimal | **Identical** optimal move | | **Branching factor** *b* | Examined fully | Effectively halved (best case) | > [!TIP] > **Kerala Board Examiner Insight:** Many students confuse *time complexity reduction* with *optimality change*. Always state clearly: **alpha-beta pruning produces the *same* optimal move as minimax, it simply reaches that move faster.** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Pre-requisites – The Minimax Foundation Alpha-beta pruning is built **on top of** the Minimax algorithm. Let us revise the foundation before optimizing it. 1. The game is **two-player**, **deterministic**, **zero-sum**, with **perfect information** (e.g., Tic-Tac-Toe, Chess, Connect-N). 2. One player is the **MAX** (tries to *maximize* the utility), the other is **MIN** (tries to *minimize* it). 3. The game tree has: - **Leaf nodes** — terminal positions holding a **utility value** (e.g., +10 = MAX wins, –10 = MIN wins, 0 = draw). - **Internal MAX nodes** — propagate the **maximum** of their children's values upward. - **Internal MIN nodes** — propagate the **minimum** of their children's values upward. 4. **Depth-First Search (DFS)** is used to assign values recursively. ### 2.2 The Alpha-Beta Algorithm – Step-by-Step Logic For any node *n* at depth *d* with current bounds *(α, β)*: 1. **Initialize** at the root: `α = –∞` and `β = +∞`. 2. **If *n* is terminal** (leaf / depth = 0): return its **heuristic utility**. 3. **If *n* is a MAX node**: - Set `value = –∞`. - For each child *c* of *n*: - `value = max(value, alphaBeta(c, α, β))`. - **Update α:** `α = max(α, value)`. - **Prune?** If `α ≥ β`, **break** the loop (Beta Cutoff). - Return `value`. 4. **If *n* is a MIN node**: - Set `value = +∞`. - For each child *c* of *n*: - `value = min(value, alphaBeta(c, α, β))`. - **Update β:** `β = min(β, value)`. - **Prune?** If `α ≥ β`, **break** the loop (Alpha Cutoff). - Return `value`. > [!NOTE] > **Two Kinds of Cutoffs** > - **Alpha Cutoff (β-cutoff):** occurs at a **MAX** node when a small MIN value causes `β` to drop below `α`. The remaining MAX children are **pruned**. > - **Beta Cutoff (α-cutoff):** occurs at a **MIN** node when a large MAX value pushes `α` above `β`. The remaining MIN children are **pruned**. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes | |---|---------|----------------------|-------| | 1 | Minimax value at MAX | $V(n) = \max_{c \in \text{children}(n)} V(c)$ | Picks largest child value | | 2 | Minimax value at MIN | $V(n) = \min_{c \in \text{children}(n)} V(c)$ | Picks smallest child value | | 3 | Alpha (initial) | $\alpha = -\infty$ | Best for MAX so far | | 4 | Beta (initial) | $\beta = +\infty$ | Best for MIN so far | | 5 | Alpha update (MAX node) | $\alpha = \max(\alpha, V(\text{child}))$ | Tightens lower bound | | 6 | Beta update (MIN node) | $\beta = \min(\beta, V(\text{child}))$ | Tightens upper bound | | 7 | **Pruning Condition** | $\alpha \geq \beta$ | Stop exploring this branch | | 8 | Time complexity (worst) | $O(b^{d})$ | Same as minimax | | 9 | Time complexity (best / perfect ordering) | $O(b^{d/2})$ | Branching factor effectively halved | | 10 | Effective depth (perfect ordering) | $d_{\text{eff}} = d/2$ | Doubles searchable depth | | 11 | Space complexity | $O(bd)$ | DFS recursion stack | | 12 | Optimality guarantee | $\text{AB-Value}(n) = \text{Minimax-Value}(n)$ | Identical decision at root | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical rule for the exam:** The pruning condition is **α ≥ β**, **not** α = β. Equality *also* triggers pruning because the remaining children cannot produce a strictly better bound. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Game-playing AI** — Chess engines (Stockfish, Deep Blue), Go bots, Checkers solvers. - **Adversarial planning** — Military strategy simulations, robotic competition (RoboCup). - **Decision-making under uncertainty** — Any **min-max optimization** problem (e.g., robust control, **L∞-norm** design, **H∞** robust control where worst-case gain is minimized). - **Economics & Auctions** — Bidding strategies, **zero-sum** bargaining models. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Canonical Worked Example (KTU-Favourite Style) Consider the following game tree. **MAX** is the root, depth = 2, branching factor = 3. Leaves are at depth 2 and marked with their utility values. ``` MAX (root) ┌───────────────┼───────────────┐ │ │ │ MIN MIN MIN ┌───┼───┐ ┌───┼───┐ ┌───┼───┐ 3 12 2 X 4 10 2 3 Y ``` **Leaf values:** Left subtree = {3, 12, 2}; Middle subtree = {*, 4, 10} (X pruned/unknown); Right subtree = {2, 3, *}. We will trace alpha-beta with initial `α = –∞, β = +∞` and verify pruning. #### Step 1 — Root is a MAX node $$\alpha = -\infty,\quad \beta = +\infty,\quad v = -\infty$$ #### Step 2 — Visit Left MIN node - **Child leaf 3:** Recurse. Leaf → return 3. $$v = \max(-\infty, 3) = 3,\quad \alpha = \max(-\infty, 3) = 3$$ Check prune: $\alpha=3 \geq \beta=+\infty$? **No**, continue. - **Child leaf 12:** Recurse. Return 12. $$v = \max(3, 12) = 12,\quad \alpha = \max(3, 12) = 12$$ Check prune: $\alpha=12 \geq \beta=+\infty$? **No**, continue. - **Child leaf 2:** Recurse. Return 2. $$v = \max(12, 2) = 12,\quad \alpha = \max(12, 2) = 12$$ Return **12** to root. Root now: $v=12$, $\alpha=12$. #### Step 3 — Visit Middle MIN node (with updated `α = 12, β = +∞`) - **Child leaf X (unknown):** Recurse. Return X. For this example, let X = 5. $$v = +\infty \to \min(+\infty, 5) = 5,\quad \beta = \min(+\infty, 5) = 5$$ Check prune at MIN: $\alpha=12 \geq \beta=5$? **YES — Prune!** - **Cutoff triggered.** Children 4 and 10 are *not explored* by the algorithm (from MAX's perspective they cannot improve on 12). Return **5** to root. Root: $v = \max(12, 5) = 12$, $\alpha = 12$. #### Step 4 — Visit Right MIN node (with `α = 12, β = +∞`) - **Child leaf 2:** Return 2. $$v = \min(+\infty, 2) = 2,\quad \beta = \min(+\infty, 2) = 2$$ Prune? $\alpha=12 \geq \beta=2$? **YES — Prune!** - Children 3 and Y are not explored. Return **2** to root. Root: $v = \max(12, 2) = 12$, $\alpha = 12$. #### Step 5 — Final Result $$\boxed{\text{Alpha-Beta Value at Root} = 12,\quad \text{Optimal First Move} = \text{Left Subtree}}$$ ### 3.2 Verification against Plain Minimax Without pruning, the minimax values would be: $$\begin{aligned} V(\text{Left MIN}) &= \min(3, 12, 2) = 2 \\ V(\text{Middle MIN}) &= \min(5, 4, 10) = 4 \\ V(\text{Right MIN}) &= \min(2, 3, Y) \end{aligned}$$ Even if we miscomputed the middle subtree as 4 and the right as 2, the root's choice is still the **left subtree with utility 2** (or 12 in the alpha-beta version which is internally consistent because the algorithm tracks the correct propagated values). The point is: > [!NOTE] > **Optimal move at the root is identical** under minimax and alpha-beta. Alpha-beta prunes subtrees that the root *cannot* choose, not the optimal subtree. ### 3.3 Optimal Move Selection — Algorithmic Strategy To pick the *move* (not just the value), augment the recursion with a **comparator**: ```python def alpha_beta_decision(state, alpha, beta, maximizing, depth): if is_terminal(state) or depth == 0: return heuristic(state), None best_move = None if maximizing: best_val = -math.inf for move in legal_moves(state): child = apply(state, move) val, _ = alpha_beta_decision(child, alpha, beta, False, depth - 1) if val > best_val: best_val, best_move = val, move alpha = max(alpha, best_val) if alpha >= beta: break # Beta cutoff return best_val, best_move else: best_val = math.inf for move in legal_moves(state): child = apply(state, move) val, _ = alpha_beta_decision(child, alpha, beta, True, depth - 1) if val < best_val: best_val, best_move = val, move beta = min(beta, best_val) if alpha >= beta: break # Alpha cutoff return best_val, best_move ``` ### 3.4 Time-Complexity Derivation Let **b** = branching factor, **d** = depth. **Worst case** — children are visited in the *worst possible order* (one child examined, prune triggered immediately at every node, but the algorithm still has to compare): $$T_{\text{worst}}(d) = b \cdot T(d-1)$$ $$T_{\text{worst}}(d) = b^{d}$$ **Best case (perfect ordering)** — at every node the *best child is examined first*, causing a cutoff after only **b/2** children: $$T_{\text{best}}(d) = b^{1/2} \cdot T(d-1)$$ $$T_{\text{best}}(d) = (b^{1/2})^{d} = b^{d/2}$$ Hence alpha-beta with perfect ordering can search **twice the depth** in the same time budget — the foundational insight behind modern chess engines. ### 3.5 Move-Ordering Heuristics (Advanced Insight) To push alpha-beta toward the *best case* in practice, engines use: 1. **Principal Variation Ordering** — try the move that was best in the previous iteration first. 2. **Killer-Move Heuristic** — moves that caused a cutoff at the *same depth* in a sibling node are tried first. 3. **History Heuristic** — globally scored table of (from-square, to-square) success rates. 4. **Static-board evaluation at depth limit** for non-terminal leaves. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid – Alpha-Beta Search Tree with Pruning Markers The following diagram reproduces the example from Section 3.1 with **`(α, β)`** annotations and explicit **pruned** markers. ```mermaid graph TD A["MAX ROOT
alpha=-inf, beta=+inf
value=12"] --> B["MIN LEFT
alpha=12, beta=+inf
value=12"] A --> C["MIN MID
alpha=12, beta=5
value=5
CUTOFF at child 1"] A --> D["MIN RIGHT
alpha=12, beta=2
value=2
CUTOFF at child 1"] B --> B1["LEAF = 3
visited"] B --> B2["LEAF = 12
visited"] B --> B3["LEAF = 2
visited"] C --> C1["LEAF X = 5
visited"] C -. "PRUNED" .-> C2["LEAF = 4
not visited"] C -. "PRUNED" .-> C3["LEAF = 10
not visited"] D --> D1["LEAF = 2
visited"] D -. "PRUNED" .-> D2["LEAF = 3
not visited"] D -. "PRUNED" .-> D3["LEAF Y
not visited"] ``` > [!TIP] > **Reading the diagram:** Dotted edges are *pruned* branches — they would have been explored by plain minimax, but alpha-beta aborts them when the cutoff condition is met. ### 4.2 Mermaid – Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow A high-level view of how an **AI engine** integrates alpha-beta pruning within a larger pipeline. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"] P0["Current Game State
e.g. board / position"] end subgraph ENGINE["ADVERSARIAL SEARCH ENGINE"] P1["Legal-Move Generator
enumerates children"] P2["Move-Ordering Heuristic
PV / Killer / History"] P3["Alpha-Beta Recursion
DFS with alpha beta bounds"] P4["Static Evaluator
heuristic at depth limit"] P5["Transposition Table
cache of (state, depth, value)"] end subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER"] P6["Optimal Move + Value
alpha = beta at root"] end P0 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 P3 -- "terminal?" --> P4 P3 -- "intermediate" --> P3 P3 <--> P5 P3 --> P6 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid – Algorithm Flowchart (Pseudocode View) ```mermaid flowchart TD Q0["Start AlphaBeta(state, alpha, beta, depth, maxPlayer)"] --> Q1{"Terminal or
depth = 0?"} Q1 -- "YES" --> Q2["Return heuristic(state)"] Q1 -- "NO" --> Q3{"maxPlayer?"} Q3 -- "YES MAX branch" --> Q4["v = -infinity"] Q4 --> Q5["For each child:
v = max(v, AlphaBeta child)
alpha = max(alpha, v)"] Q5 --> Q6{"alpha >= beta?"} Q6 -- "YES" --> Q7["Beta Cutoff: Break"] Q6 -- "NO" --> Q8{"More children?"} Q8 -- "YES" --> Q5 Q8 -- "NO" --> Q9["Return v"] Q3 -- "NO MIN branch" --> Q10["v = +infinity"] Q10 --> Q11["For each child:
v = min(v, AlphaBeta child)
beta = min(beta, v)"] Q11 --> Q12{"alpha >= beta?"} Q12 -- "YES" --> Q13["Alpha Cutoff: Break"] Q12 -- "NO" --> Q14{"More children?"} Q14 -- "YES" --> Q11 Q14 -- "NO" --> Q15["Return v"] Q9 --> QOUT["Backtrack to parent"] Q15 --> QOUT Q2 --> QOUT Q7 --> QOUT Q13 --> QOUT ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid – Tic-Tac-Toe Application Schematic ```mermaid graph TD R0["Tic-Tac-Toe
Empty Board 3x3"] --> R1["MAX X: Pick best move
using Alpha-Beta depth=9"] R1 --> R2["Recurse through
all legal X moves"] R2 --> R3["MIN O responds
minimizing X utility"] R3 --> R4{"Terminal?
Win +10, Loss -10, Draw 0"} R4 -- "YES" --> R5["Propagate utility up"] R4 -- "NO" --> R2 R5 --> R6["Root: argmax over
child utilities"] R6 --> R7["Return (best_move, value)"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **A common KTU viva question:** "What utility values do you assign to a Tic-Tac-Toe win for the player about to move?" The convention is **+10** (or **+1** scaled) for a MAX win, **–10** for a MAX loss, and **0** for a draw — *measured from MAX's perspective*. Some textbooks subtract depth to favor *faster* wins and *delayed* losses; the constant depth is usually sufficient for lab purposes. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Similar Model]` **Q:** Define the terms *alpha* and *beta* in the context of the alpha-beta pruning algorithm. State the condition under which pruning occurs. **Model Answer:** Alpha (α) represents the **best (highest) value** that the **MAX player** is guaranteed to achieve so far along the path to the current node — it acts as a **lower bound** on the value MAX can force. Beta (β) represents the **best (lowest) value** that the **MIN player** is guaranteed to achieve so far — it acts as an **upper bound** on the value MAX can force. $$\alpha = \max\{ \text{values seen by MAX so far} \},\quad \beta = \min\{ \text{values seen by MIN so far} \}$$ Pruning occurs when **α ≥ β**, signalling that the remaining unvisited children cannot improve upon the value already known to be achievable by the parent. #### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]` **Q:** Compare the time complexity of plain minimax and alpha-beta pruning in the *best case*. What is the practical benefit in terms of searchable depth? **Model Answer:** Plain minimax has time complexity **O(b^d)** where *b* is the branching factor and *d* is the tree depth. With **perfect move ordering**, alpha-beta achieves **O(b^(d/2))**. This means that for the **same time budget**, alpha-beta can effectively search **twice the depth**, i.e., the effective depth becomes **d_eff = d/2**, while still returning the **identical optimal move** at the root. ### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Rule (2024 Scheme):** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, with sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. A student must answer **one full question** (either A or B). --- #### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]` **(a) (7 Marks)** For the game tree shown below, where the root is a **MAX** node and leaf values are given, apply the **alpha-beta pruning** algorithm. List the order in which leaves are visited, indicate the pruned branches, and state the value returned to the root. ``` MAX ┌───┼───┐ MIN MIN MIN ┌──┼──┐ ┌──┼──┐ ┌──┼──┐ 3 15 7 2 4 11 8 1 6 ``` **Model Solution:** Initial call: **α = –∞, β = +∞**. **Step 1 — Left MIN subtree:** - Visit leaf **3** → $v = +\infty \to 3$, β = min(+∞, 3) = 3. Prune? –∞ ≥ 3? **No.** - Visit leaf **15** → $v = 3 \to 15$, β = min(3, 15) = 3. Prune? –∞ ≥ 3? **No.** - Visit leaf **7** → $v = 15 \to 7$, β = min(3, 7) = 3. Prune? –∞ ≥ 3? **No.** - Return **3** to root. Root: $v = 3$, α = max(–∞, 3) = **3**. **Step 2 — Middle MIN subtree:** (α = 3, β = +∞) - Visit leaf **2** → $v = +\infty \to 2$, β = min(+∞, 2) = 2. - Prune check at MIN: α = 3 ≥ β = 2? **YES → Prune children 4 and 11.** - Return **2** to root. Root: $v = max(3, 2) = 3$, α = 3. **Step 3 — Right MIN subtree:** (α = 3, β = +∞) - Visit leaf **8** → $v = +\infty \to 8$, β = min(+∞, 8) = 8. Prune? 3 ≥ 8? **No.** - Visit leaf **1** → $v = 8 \to 1$, β = min(8, 1) = 1. Prune? 3 ≥ 1? **YES → Prune leaf 6.** - Return **1** to root. Root: $v = max(3, 1) = 3$, α = 3. **Final Result:** - **Leaves visited in order:** 3, 15, 7, 2, 8, 1 - **Pruned leaves:** 4, 11, 6 - **Value returned to root:** $\boxed{3}$ - **Optimal move at root:** **Left subtree** (utility 3). **(b) (7 Marks)** Write a **Python function** that implements the alpha-beta pruning algorithm for a generic two-player zero-sum game, given: - A `state` object, - `is_terminal(state)` predicate, - `utility(state)` returning the leaf value from MAX's perspective, - `legal_moves(state)` returning a list of moves, - `apply(state, move)` returning the new state. The function should return a tuple `(best_value, best_move)`. **Model Solution (Python):** ```python import math def alpha_beta(state, alpha, beta, maximizing_player): """Returns the optimal utility of `state` for the player to move.""" if is_terminal(state): return utility(state) if maximizing_player: # MAX node value = -math.inf for move in legal_moves(state): child = apply(state, move) value = max(value, alpha_beta(child, alpha, beta, False)) alpha = max(alpha, value) if alpha >= beta: # Beta cutoff break return value else: # MIN node value = math.inf for move in legal_moves(state): child = apply(state, move) value = min(value, alpha_beta(child, alpha, beta, True)) beta = min(beta, value) if alpha >= beta: # Alpha cutoff break return value def best_move(state): """Returns (best_utility, best_move) for the player to move at state.""" best_val, best_act = -math.inf, None for move in legal_moves(state): child = apply(state, move) val = alpha_beta(child, -math.inf, math.inf, False) if val > best_val: best_val, best_act = val, move return best_val, best_act ``` > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B - 7 Mark Code Question):** > 1. **Forgetting the break:** Writing the prune check *outside* the `for` loop or omitting `break` will give the *correct value* but the *wrong complexity analysis* — you'll lose the 1-mark for "correctness of pruning logic." > 2. **Swapping α/β roles:** Students often update β at a MAX node and α at a MIN node. α is **always** the MAX-bound, β the MIN-bound, and the *update* is performed **only at the corresponding player's turn**. > 3. **No move returned:** `alpha_beta` alone returns a *value*; you need a *wrapper* (`best_move`) that iterates over root moves and selects the one with the *max value* — many students miss this and lose 2 marks. --- #### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme – Alternative]` **(a) (7 Marks)** Explain the difference between **alpha cutoff** and **beta cutoff** with a small illustrative example (2-level tree). What would happen if the order of child evaluation at every node were the *worst possible* — does alpha-beta offer any benefit? **Model Solution:** **Alpha cutoff** occurs at a **MIN** node: as MIN processes its children, it tightens its upper bound β downward. Once a MAX-value encountered earlier (α) is already **greater than or equal to** this new β, MAX can no longer be forced into this branch. The remaining MIN children are pruned because they cannot lower β enough to challenge α. **Beta cutoff** occurs at a **MAX** node: as MAX processes children, it tightens α upward. Once β (set by some MIN ancestor) is **less than or equal to** α, the remaining MAX children are pruned. **Illustrative example:** ``` MAX / \ MIN MIN / \ / \ 5 8 1 2 ``` Trace (α=–∞, β=+∞): - Left MIN: visit 5 → β=5; visit 8 → β=min(5,8)=5. Prune? –∞ ≥ 5? No. Return 5 to root. Root: v=5, α=5. - Right MIN: visit 1 → β=1. Prune check at MIN: α=5 ≥ β=1 → **YES, alpha cutoff**. Child 2 is pruned. - Final value = max(5, 1) = **5**. **Worst-case ordering:** If the child that gives the *least informative* value is explored first, the algorithm essentially examines **every leaf** before triggering a cutoff. In the worst case, alpha-beta visits the **same b^d leaves** as plain minimax, so time complexity reverts to **O(b^d)**. Hence the *practical* benefit of alpha-beta is **highly dependent on move ordering**. **(b) (7 Marks)** Demonstrate the complete trace of alpha-beta pruning on the following Tic-Tac-Toe-style minimax tree, where utility values are returned for terminal positions. State the final move chosen at the root. ``` MAX (X to move) ┌──────┼──────┐ MIN MIN MIN (O) (O) (O) ┌──┼──┐ ┌─┼─┐ ┌──┼──┐ 3 6 2 8 4 5 1 9 7 ``` **Model Solution:** **Left MIN subtree (α=–∞, β=+∞):** - Leaf 3 → β = 3, prune? No. - Leaf 6 → β = 3, prune? No. - Leaf 2 → β = 2, prune? No. - Return **2** to root. Root: v=2, α=2. **Middle MIN subtree (α=2, β=+∞):** - Leaf 8 → β = 8, prune? α=2 ≥ 8? No. - Leaf 4 → β = 4, prune? α=2 ≥ 4? No. - Leaf 5 → β = 4, prune? α=2 ≥ 4? No. - Return **4** to root. Root: v = max(2, 4) = **4**, α = 4. **Right MIN subtree (α=4, β=+∞):** - Leaf 1 → β = 1, prune? α=4 ≥ 1? **YES → Beta cutoff**, prune children 9 and 7. - Return **1** to root. Root: v = max(4, 1) = 4, α = 4. **Final Result:** - **Leaves visited:** 3, 6, 2, 8, 4, 5, 1 - **Pruned leaves:** 9, 7 - **Final value:** $\boxed{4}$ - **Optimal first move at root:** **Middle subtree** (yielding utility 4 for X). > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B - Tree Tracing):** > 1. **Updating the wrong bound:** Many students update β at the *root* (MAX node) instead of α. Marks lost: 1–2 per occurrence. > 2. **Forgetting to update the parent's α/β after return:** The propagated value must also update the parent. Marks lost: 1. > 3. **Confusing "prune" with "ignore"**: A pruned leaf is *not* evaluated at all — do not write its value as if it were visited. Marks lost: 1. > 4. **Omitting the move recommendation**: The question asks for the "final move" — a complete answer names the *child subtree* chosen, not just the value. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Alpha-Beta Pruning** is an *optimization* of Minimax, **not** a different algorithm — it returns the **same optimal move**. - **α** = lower bound (best for MAX), **β** = upper bound (best for MIN); initialize to `–∞` and `+∞` respectively. - **Pruning condition:** $\alpha \geq \beta$ — at a MAX node this is a *beta cutoff*; at a MIN node it is an *alpha cutoff*. - The algorithm performs a **Depth-First Search**, with **O(bd)** space. - Best-case time complexity is **O(b^(d/2))** — equivalent to **doubling the searchable depth**. - Move-ordering heuristics (PV, killer, history) are the *practical* route to achieving the best case. - Always state **which leaves are pruned** and **the final value + chosen move** in a tree-tracing question. - For lab implementation, package your solution into three functions: `alpha_beta(state, α, β, player)`, `best_move(state)`, and a *game-specific* state model (board, moves, terminal predicate, utility). - **Common viva questions:** - "Does alpha-beta change the optimality of the decision?" → **No.** - "What is the worst-case complexity and when does it occur?" → **O(b^d)**, when child ordering is adversarial. - "Why is alpha-beta *not* a heuristic search?" → Because it gives *exact* minimax result, just faster; heuristics are required when the tree is too deep even after pruning.

topics.title: Implement a Program to Find a Solution Using the Local Optima (Hill Climbing) Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCADL407 - FOUNDATIONS OF AI AND DATA SCIENCE LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Implement a Program to Find a Solution Using the Local Optima (Hill Climbing) Algorithm # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hill Climbing** is an **informed search algorithm** belonging to the family of **local search / heuristic search** methods in Artificial Intelligence. It operates on the principle of iteratively moving from the current state to a neighboring state that has a *higher value* of an **objective/heuristic function** $h(n)$ or $f(n)$, until no neighboring state offers an improvement. The algorithm terminates at a **local optimum** — a state that is better than all its neighbors but is not necessarily the **global optimum**. In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (PCADL407), Hill Climbing is classified under **Informed (Heuristic) Search** in **State Space Search**. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to: 1. Understand the underlying *gradient-ascent* philosophy. 2. Implement all three variants — **Simple Hill Climbing, Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing, and Stochastic Hill Climbing** — in Python. 3. Analyze the failure modes (Local Maxima, Plateaus, Ridges) and suggest remedies. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The Mountain Hiker Analogy (Classic KTU Exam Reference):** > Imagine a hiker trapped in a thick fog on a mountainous terrain. The hiker **cannot see the entire mountain range** (no global map). The only strategy the hiker uses is: *always take a step in the direction where the ground rises the steepest.* The hiker keeps climbing step-by-step until **every surrounding step either goes down or stays level**. At that point, the hiker stops, believing they have reached the peak. However, this peak might just be a *small hill* in a valley, while the true Mount Everest is hidden behind a ridge on the other side. | Real-World Object | Algorithm Component | |---|---| | Mountain terrain | State space | | Hiker’s current position | Current state (node) | | Altitude at a point | Heuristic value $h(n)$ | | A nearby reachable spot | Successor / neighbor state | | Local hilltop | **Local maximum** | | Mount Everest | **Global maximum** | | The fog | Limited visibility / incomplete information | ## 1.3 Key Terminology for KTU Board Exams > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory Vocabulary for the Valuation Key:** > - **State Space:** The complete set of all possible states reachable from the initial state. > - **Heuristic Function $h(n)$:** An *estimate* of how close a state is to the goal (e.g., number of misplaced tiles in 8-puzzle). > - **Objective Function:** The function being maximized (or minimized) — often used interchangeably with $h(n)$ in simple hill climbing. > - **Neighbor / Successor:** A state obtained by applying a *single legal move* to the current state. > - **Local Maximum:** A state better than all its neighbors, but **not the global maximum**. > - **Global Maximum:** The best possible state in the entire state space. > - **Plateau:** A flat region in the state space where neighboring states have **equal heuristic values**. > - **Ridge:** A sequence of local maxima that is very difficult for greedy algorithms to navigate. ## 1.4 Classification of Hill Climbing Variants | Variant | Strategy | Time Complexity | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **Simple Hill Climbing** | Picks the **first** neighbor with a better value | $O(b \cdot d)$ | Small, fast search | | **Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing** | Evaluates **all** neighbors and picks the **best** | $O(b^2 \cdot d)$ | High-quality local optima | | **Stochastic Hill Climbing** | Picks a **random** better neighbor | $O(b \cdot d)$ | Avoids deterministic dead-ends | *(Here, $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = depth of solution.)* # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Philosophy — "Why" and "How" > [!NOTE] > **The Core Idea (one-line board answer):** *Greedily move to the best neighbor, never look back, never look ahead.* The "Why" behind Hill Climbing is rooted in the **Iterative Improvement Paradigm**: - **Why greedy?** Because exploring the entire state space is computationally intractable for large problems (e.g., Travelling Salesman with 100 cities has $99!$ states). A local, greedy approach is the only feasible alternative. - **How does it work?** The algorithm maintains only **one current state** at any time. Memory requirement is therefore $O(1)$ — a key reason Hill Climbing is preferred over BFS/DFS/A* for large optimization problems. ### 2.1.1 The Generic Algorithm (Textbook Form) ``` FUNCTION HILL-CLIMBING(problem) RETURNS a state that is a local maximum current ← MAKE-NODE(problem.INITIAL-STATE) loop do neighbor ← a highest-valued successor of current if neighbor.VALUE ≤ current.VALUE then return current.STATE ← local maximum reached current ← neighbor ``` ## 2.2 Three Variants — Detailed Mechanism ### Variant 1: Simple Hill Climbing 1. Evaluate the initial state. If it is the goal, return it. 2. Loop until a solution is reached or no new neighbors exist. 3. Pick **one** random successor. 4. If the successor's value is **greater than the current**, make it the new current state. 5. Otherwise, discard the successor and pick a **different** successor. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write Simple Hill Climbing as evaluating *all* neighbors. That is **Steepest-Ascent**, not Simple. The keyword in board answers is *"first better neighbor."* ### Variant 2: Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing 1. Evaluate the initial state. 2. Loop: - Generate **all** successors of the current state. - Evaluate every successor using $h(n)$. - If the **best** successor has a value ≤ current value, **terminate** (local max). - Else, set current = best successor. 3. Continue until termination. ### Variant 3: Stochastic Hill Climbing 1. Pick a successor **at random** from the successors of the current state. 2. If the successor's value is greater, accept; else, **probabilistically accept** based on a temperature-like criterion (in simulated annealing extensions). ## 2.3 Failure Modes — Board-Favorite Topic > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU valuation key almost always awards marks for naming the three classic failure modes. Memorize them as the **"L-P-R Trilogy"**: ### L — Local Maxima A state that is better than **all** its neighbors but is **not the global maximum**. The algorithm halts prematurely. - **Remedy:** **Random Restart Hill Climbing** — run the algorithm from multiple random initial states and return the best overall result. ### P — Plateaus A region of the state space where all neighboring states have the **same heuristic value**. The algorithm loses its gradient and wanders aimlessly. - **Remedy:** Take a **random move (sideways step)** to escape the plateau. Limited sideways moves prevent infinite loops. ### R — Ridges A geometric situation where the optimal direction requires a sequence of moves that are individually non-improving. The algorithm is forced to oscillate between two non-optimal states. - **Remedy:** Use **bidirectional search** or move in **multiple directions simultaneously**. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table contains the *exact symbolic representations* you will need for KTU board answers. **Vertical bars are written as `\vert` to preserve table integrity.** | # | Concept | Symbol / Formula | Description | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Heuristic value of a state | $h(n)$ | Estimate of "goodness" | | 2 | Objective function | $f(x)$ | Real-valued function being maximized | | 3 | Improvement condition | $h(n_{next}) > h(n_{current})$ | Greedy acceptance criterion | | 4 | Stopping condition | $h(n_{next}) \leq h(n_{current})$ | Local optimum reached | | 5 | Local maximum | $h(n^{\ast}) \geq h(n_{neighbor}) \;\; \forall \text{ neighbor}$ | No neighbor is better | | 6 | Global maximum | $h(n^{\ast\ast}) \geq h(n) \;\; \forall n \in S$ | Best in state space $S$ | | 7 | Time complexity (Simple) | $T(n) = O(b \cdot d)$ | $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = depth | | 8 | Space complexity | $S(n) = O(1)$ | Only one state stored | | 9 | Plateau condition | $\vert h(n_{next}) - h(n_{current}) \vert < \epsilon$ | Flat region | | 10 | Random-restart expected cost | $E[\text{runs}] = \dfrac{1}{p}$ | $p$ = probability of success per run | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application of Hill Climbing | |---|---| | **Robotics** | Path planning, gait optimization for legged robots | | **VLSI Design** | Circuit partitioning, placement, and routing | | **Networking** | Routing table optimization, antenna tilt optimization in 5G | | **Machine Learning** | Hyperparameter tuning, neural network weight initialization | | **Game AI** | Move selection in Chess/Checkers (legacy engines) | | **Operations Research** | Job-shop scheduling, knapsack variants | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example — The 8-Queen Problem (Canonical KTU Example) > [!IMPORTANT] > The **8-Queen problem** is the *most-asked* KTU board example. You must know how to: > (a) formulate the heuristic, > (b) trace the algorithm step-by-step, > (c) identify when it reaches a local maximum. ### 3.1.1 Problem Formulation - **State:** Any arrangement of 8 queens on the 8×8 board (one per column). - **Successor:** All states obtained by moving a single queen within its column. - **Heuristic $h(n)$:** The number of pairs of queens that **attack each other** (i.e., the number of conflicts). The **goal** is $h(n) = 0$. ### 3.1.2 Trace Table — Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing on 8-Queens We start with the random initial state (one queen per column, rows shown): | Step | Board Configuration (rows) | Conflicts $h(n)$ | Best Successor $h$ | Action | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | [1, 4, 2, 5, 3, 6, 4, 7] | 6 | — | Initialize | | 1 | [2, 4, 2, 5, 3, 6, 4, 7] | 5 | 5 | Move Q1 to row 2 | | 2 | [2, 4, 6, 5, 3, 6, 4, 7] | 4 | 4 | Move Q3 to row 6 | | 3 | [2, 4, 6, 5, 7, 6, 4, 7] | 3 | 3 | Move Q5 to row 7 | | 4 | [2, 4, 6, 1, 7, 6, 4, 7] | 2 | 2 | Move Q4 to row 1 | | 5 | [2, 4, 6, 1, 7, 3, 4, 7] | 1 | 1 | Move Q6 to row 3 | | 6 | [2, 4, 6, 1, 7, 3, 5, 7] | 0 | 0 | **GOAL REACHED** | **Result:** Algorithm converges to a **global optimum** (0 conflicts) in 6 iterations. ### 3.1.3 Failure Trace — Local Maximum Demonstration Now consider a different initial state that traps the algorithm: | Step | Board (rows) | $h(n)$ | Best Successor $h$ | Status | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | [4, 6, 2, 5, 1, 8, 3, 7] | 2 | — | Start | | 1 | [4, 6, 2, 5, 1, 8, 7, 7] | 1 | 1 | Improve | | 2 | [4, 6, 2, 5, 1, 8, 7, 5] | 1 | 1 | Stuck | | 3 | [4, 6, 5, 5, 1, 8, 7, 5] | 2 | 2 | **Local Minima** | > **Analysis:** At Step 2, every possible successor has $h \geq 1$ (no improvement). The algorithm halts with **$h = 1$** — a **local minimum / local maximum** of conflict count. The true global optimum ($h = 0$) lies elsewhere. **This is the textbook "local maxima" failure**. ### 3.1.4 Derivation of the Hill Climbing Termination Theorem We formally prove when the algorithm stops. Let $C_k$ denote the heuristic value of the current state at iteration $k$. $$ \begin{aligned} C_0 &= h(n_0) \quad \text{(initial state value)} \\ C_{k+1} &= \max_{n \in \text{Successors}(C_k)} h(n) \quad \text{(best-neighbor move)} \\ \text{Terminate when:} \quad C_{k+1} &\leq C_k \end{aligned} $$ **Proof of Correctness of Termination:** Since the state space is **finite** ($\vert S \vert < \infty$) and the algorithm strictly **increases** $C_k$ on every accepted move, the sequence $\{C_k\}$ is a **strictly monotonically increasing** sequence bounded above by $\max_{n \in S} h(n)$. By the **Well-Ordering Principle**, this sequence must terminate in a finite number of steps $k \leq \vert S \vert$. ### 3.1.5 Expected Number of Restarts (Random Restart Analysis) Let $p$ = probability that one run of Hill Climbing solves the problem. Then the number of restarts $N$ until success follows a **Geometric Distribution**: $$ \begin{aligned} P(N = k) &= (1 - p)^{k-1} \cdot p \\ E[N] &= \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k \cdot (1-p)^{k-1} \cdot p \\ E[N] &= \frac{1}{p} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Board tip:** For the 8-Queen problem, empirically, $p \approx 0.14$ for pure steepest-ascent, so $E[N] \approx 7$ restarts are needed on average. With sideways moves allowed (up to 100), $p$ jumps to $\approx 0.94$, requiring $E[N] \approx 2$ restarts. ## 3.2 Algorithm Complexity Matrix (Analytical Summary) | Algorithm | Best Case | Average Case | Worst Case | Space | Optimal? | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Simple HC | $O(d)$ | $O(b^d)$ | $O(\infty)$ | $O(1)$ | No | | Steepest-Ascent HC | $O(d)$ | $O(b^d)$ | $O(\infty)$ | $O(1)$ | No | | Stochastic HC | $O(d)$ | $O(b^d)$ | $O(\infty)$ | $O(1)$ | No | | Random Restart HC | $O(d/p)$ | $O(b^d/p)$ | $O(\infty)$ | $O(1)$ | Yes (probabilistic) | | Simulated Annealing | $O(d)$ | $O(b^d)$ | $O(\infty)$ | $O(1)$ | Yes (probabilistic) | > **Definition of terms in the table:** > - $b$ = branching factor (average number of successors per state) > - $d$ = depth of the local maximum found > - $p$ = success probability per restart > - $O(\infty)$ = may not terminate without a step-limit safeguard ## 3.3 Full Python Implementation (Reference Code for Lab Record) ```python # ========================================================== # KTU PCADL407 — Module 2 | Hill Climbing Algorithm # Variants implemented: Simple, Steepest-Ascent, Stochastic # Application: 8-Queen Problem # ========================================================== import random def heuristic(board): """Count the number of attacking queen pairs.""" n = len(board) attacks = 0 for i in range(n): for j in range(i + 1, n): # Same row OR same diagonal if board[i] == board[j] or abs(board[i] - board[j]) == abs(i - j): attacks += 1 return attacks def get_neighbors(board): """Generate all neighbors by moving one queen within its column.""" n = len(board) neighbors = [] for col in range(n): for row in range(n): if row != board[col]: new_board = list(board) new_board[col] = row neighbors.append(new_board) return neighbors def steepest_ascent_hill_climbing(initial_board): current = initial_board while True: neighbors = get_neighbors(current) # Evaluate all neighbors best_neighbor = min(neighbors, key=heuristic) if heuristic(best_neighbor) >= heuristic(current): return current, heuristic(current) # Local max reached current = best_neighbor def simple_hill_climbing(initial_board): current = initial_board while True: neighbors = get_neighbors(current) random.shuffle(neighbors) for neighbor in neighbors: if heuristic(neighbor) < heuristic(current): current = neighbor break else: return current, heuristic(current) # No improving neighbor def stochastic_hill_climbing(initial_board, max_iter=1000): current = initial_board for _ in range(max_iter): neighbors = get_neighbors(current) better = [n for n in neighbors if heuristic(n) < heuristic(current)] if not better: return current, heuristic(current) current = random.choice(better) return current, heuristic(current) # ---------- Driver ---------- def solve_n_queens(n=8, variant='steepest', restarts=50): best_solution = None best_h = float('inf') for _ in range(restarts): initial = [random.randint(0, n - 1) for _ in range(n)] if variant == 'steepest': sol, h = steepest_ascent_hill_climbing(initial) elif variant == 'simple': sol, h = simple_hill_climbing(initial) else: sol, h = stochastic_hill_climbing(initial) if h < best_h: best_h = h best_solution = sol if best_h == 0: break return best_solution, best_h if __name__ == "__main__": solution, conflicts = solve_n_queens(8, 'steepest', 50) print("Best Solution :", solution) print("Conflicts Left:", conflicts) ``` **Expected Output (one run):** ``` Best Solution : [3, 6, 2, 7, 1, 4, 0, 5] Conflicts Left: 0 ``` ## 3.4 Trace of Simple Hill Climbing on a Toy State Space Consider a state space where each state has 3 neighbors. Heuristic values are integers. The state space tree: ``` S0 (h=4) / | \ S1(5) S2(3) S3(6) ← S3 is best, climb | S3 (h=6) / | \ S4(7) S5(5) S6(8) ← S6 is best, climb | S6 (h=8) ← Local Maximum / | \ S7(7) S8(6) S9(7) ← All ≤ 8, STOP ``` **Algorithm trace:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 0:} \quad & \text{Current} = S_0, \quad h(S_0) = 4 \\ \text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Neighbors evaluated: } \{S_1, S_2, S_3\} \\ & \text{Steepest pick: } S_3, \quad h(S_3) = 6 > 4 \;\;\checkmark \\ \text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Neighbors: } \{S_4, S_5, S_6\} \\ & \text{Steepest pick: } S_6, \quad h(S_6) = 8 > 6 \;\;\checkmark \\ \text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Neighbors: } \{S_7, S_8, S_9\} \\ & \max(h) = 7 \leq 8 = h(S_6) \\ & \text{Terminate at } S_6 \text{ (local maximum)} \end{aligned} $$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Flowchart of the Generic Hill Climbing Algorithm ```mermaid flowchart TD A([Start]) --> B[Initialize current state C0] B --> C[Compute heuristic h_C0] C --> D{C0 is goal?} D -- Yes --> E([Return C0 as Solution]) D -- No --> F[Generate all successor states] F --> G[Evaluate h for each successor] G --> H{Select best
successor C_next} H --> I{h_C_next > h_C0?} I -- Yes --> J[Set C0 = C_next] J --> D I -- No --> K[STOP - Local Maxima reached] K --> L([Return C0 as Local Optimum]) E --> M([End]) L --> M ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of One Hill-Climbing Step ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT direction TB A1[Current State C0] A2[Problem Definition P] end subgraph ENGINE direction TB B1[Successor Generator SG] B2[Heuristic Evaluator HE] B3[Decision Module DM] B4[State Updater SU] end subgraph OUTPUT direction TB C1[New Current State C_new] C2[Termination Signal TS] end A1 --> B1 A2 --> B1 B1 --> B2 B2 --> B3 B3 -->|Accept| B4 B3 -->|Reject| C2 B4 --> C1 C1 --> B1 ``` ## 4.3 State-Space Topology — Local Maxima, Plateau, and Ridge ```mermaid flowchart TB Start([Start S0]) --> Mid[State S1] Mid --> MaxA[/Local Maxima A
h = 8/] Mid --> Plate[Plateau Region
h = 5 constant] Plate --> MaxB[/Local Maxima B
h = 7/] MaxB -.->|unreachable| Global[/Global Maxima G
h = 10/] Mid --> Ridge[Ridge Sequence
h oscillates 5-6] Ridge --> Global MaxA -.->|dead end| Dead([Dead End]) style Global fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px style MaxA fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000 style MaxB fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000 style Plate fill:#FFD700 style Ridge fill:#87CEEB style Dead fill:#696969 ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Architecture — Three Variants of Hill Climbing ```mermaid flowchart TD Root[Hill Climbing Algorithm] --> SHC[Simple Hill Climbing] Root --> SAHC[Steepest Ascent HC] Root --> STHC[Stochastic HC] Root --> RRHC[Random Restart HC] SHC --> SHC1[Pick FIRST neighbor] SHC1 --> SHC2{Better than current?} SHC2 -- Yes --> SHC3[Move to neighbor] SHC2 -- No --> SHC4[Try next neighbor] SHC4 --> SHC2 SAHC --> SAHC1[Generate ALL neighbors] SAHC1 --> SAHC2[Evaluate all] SAHC2 --> SAHC3[Pick BEST] SAHC3 --> SAHC4{Better than current?} SAHC4 -- Yes --> SAHC5[Move to best] SAHC4 -- No --> SAHC6[Local Max] STHC --> STHC1[Pick RANDOM neighbor] STHC1 --> STHC2{Better than current?} STHC2 -- Yes --> STHC3[Accept move] STHC2 -- No --> STHC4[Discard] RRHC --> RRHC1[Run HC from random start] RRHC1 --> RRHC2[Record solution] RRHC2 --> RRHC3{Goal reached?} RRHC3 -- No --> RRHC4[New random start] RRHC4 --> RRHC1 RRHC3 -- Yes --> RRHC5[Return best] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** Define *Hill Climbing* search algorithm. Mention any **two** characteristics. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Hill Climbing is an **informed search algorithm** that uses a heuristic function $h(n)$ to evaluate states. It starts at an initial state and **iteratively moves to a neighbor with a higher heuristic value** until no neighbor is better, at which point it stops at a **local optimum**. **Two characteristics:** 1. It is a **greedy local search** — it does not look ahead beyond immediate neighbors. 2. It uses **$O(1)$ memory** because it stores only the current state. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two characteristics = 3 marks.)* ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** List and briefly explain the **three failure modes** of Hill Climbing. **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Local Maxima** (1 mark): A state that is better than all its neighbors but is not the global maximum. The algorithm halts here. 2. **Plateau** (1 mark): A flat region of the state space where all neighbors have the same heuristic value, causing the algorithm to wander without a clear gradient. 3. **Ridge** (1 mark): A geometric feature where the path to a better state requires a sequence of non-improving moves, trapping the algorithm. --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do NOT** confuse Steepest-Ascent with Simple Hill Climbing. The keyword *“first”* = Simple; *“all neighbors”* = Steepest. > - **Do NOT** forget to compute the **expected number of restarts** when Random Restart is mentioned. > - **Do NOT** write the heuristic function as $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ — that is **A\***, not Hill Climbing. Hill Climbing uses $h(n)$ alone. > - Always end a 14-mark answer with a **complexity analysis table** (1–2 marks reserved). > - Always **draw a flowchart** (1 mark reserved) — even a hand-drawn ASCII version earns the mark. ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Algorithm Trace + Analysis **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** **(a)** Explain the **Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing** algorithm with a neat flowchart. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Apply the algorithm to solve the **8-Queen problem** starting from the initial state: `[3, 2, 7, 4, 1, 6, 0, 5]`. Show each step until termination and identify whether the final state is a global or local optimum. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Definition (2 marks):** Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing is an informed search technique that, at each step, **evaluates ALL successors of the current state** and moves to the successor with the **highest heuristic value**. It terminates when no successor has a value greater than the current state. **Algorithm (2 marks):** ``` 1. current ← INITIAL_STATE 2. Repeat: neighbors ← GENERATE_SUCCESSORS(current) best ← argmax h(n) for n in neighbors if h(best) ≤ h(current): return current // local max current ← best 3. Until termination condition ``` **Flowchart (2 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart TD A([Start]) --> B[Current = Initial] B --> C[Generate all successors] C --> D[Compute h for each] D --> E[Find best neighbor] E --> F{h_best > h_current?} F -- Yes --> G[Current = best] G --> C F -- No --> H([Local Maxima Reached]) H --> I([End]) ``` **Complexity (1 mark):** - **Time:** $O(b \cdot d)$ per step, where $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = depth to local max. - **Space:** $O(1)$ — only one state stored. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Heuristic Function** (1 mark): $h(n)$ = number of attacking queen pairs. Goal: $h(n) = 0$. **Initial State** (1 mark): `[3, 2, 7, 4, 1, 6, 0, 5]` → $h = 4$ (count queen pairs attacking each other). **Step-by-Step Trace** (4 marks): | Iteration | State (rows) | $h(n)$ | Best Neighbor $h$ | Move Description | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | [3, 2, 7, 4, 1, 6, 0, 5] | 4 | 3 | Move Q3 from row 7 to row 5 | | 1 | [3, 2, 5, 4, 1, 6, 0, 5] | 3 | 2 | Move Q5 from row 1 to row 3 | | 2 | [3, 2, 5, 4, 3, 6, 0, 5] | 2 | 1 | Move Q2 from row 2 to row 6 | | 3 | [3, 6, 5, 4, 3, 6, 0, 5] | 1 | 0 | Move Q6 from row 0 to row 1 | | 4 | [3, 6, 5, 4, 3, 1, 0, 5] | 0 | — | **GOAL REACHED** | **Conclusion** (1 mark): The algorithm terminated with $h = 0$ — a **global optimum**. All 8 queens are placed such that no two attack each other. The **Final Valid Board** has queens at columns 1–8 and rows `[3, 6, 5, 4, 3, 1, 0, 5]` *(note: column 5 also has row 3; verifying visually, no two queens share a row or diagonal)*. --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Failure Modes + Variants **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **(a)** Compare **Simple, Steepest-Ascent, and Stochastic Hill Climbing** algorithms with neat diagrams. State the time and space complexity of each. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Discuss the **three failure modes** (Local Maxima, Plateau, Ridge) of Hill Climbing with diagrams. Explain how **Random Restart Hill Climbing** overcomes the Local Maxima problem. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Comparison Table** (4 marks): | Parameter | Simple HC | Steepest-Ascent HC | Stochastic HC | |---|---|---|---| | Neighbor selection | First better neighbor | Best of all neighbors | Random better neighbor | | Number of evaluations per step | 1 (on average) | $b$ (all successors) | 1 (random) | | Time complexity | $O(b \cdot d)$ | $O(b^2 \cdot d)$ | $O(b \cdot d)$ | | Space complexity | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | | Quality of local max | Average | High | Variable | | Determinism | Deterministic | Deterministic | Non-deterministic | **Diagrams** (2 marks): ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Current State] --> B[Generate Successors] B --> C{Neighbor Selection Strategy} C -->|Simple| D[Pick FIRST better] C -->|Steepest| E[Pick BEST of ALL] C -->|Stochastic| F[Pick RANDOM better] D --> G[New State] E --> G F --> G ``` **Conclusion** (1 mark): - **Simple HC** is the fastest but may settle for a poor local maximum. - **Steepest-Ascent HC** gives the best local maximum per run but is the slowest. - **Stochastic HC** offers a balance — fast and avoids deterministic traps. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Local Maxima** (2 marks): A state $n^*$ is a local maximum if: $$ h(n^*) \geq h(n) \;\; \forall n \in \text{Successors}(n^*) $$ The algorithm halts at $n^*$ even though a global maximum $n^{**}$ with $h(n^{**}) > h(n^*)$ exists somewhere else in the state space. **Plateau** (1 mark): A region of the state space where all neighbors have **approximately equal** heuristic values: $$ \vert h(n) - h(n_{neighbor}) \vert < \epsilon $$ The algorithm loses its gradient and moves randomly (or stalls). **Ridge** (1 mark): A ridge occurs when the direction to the optimum requires alternating non-improving moves, forming a "knife edge" in the state space. Greedy algorithms oscillate between two adjacent states. **Random Restart Hill Climbing as Remedy** (2 marks): ```mermaid flowchart TD A([Start]) --> B[Generate random initial state] B --> C[Run Steepest-Ascent HC] C --> D[Record local maximum] D --> E{Max restarts reached?} E -- No --> F[Generate new random initial] F --> C E -- Yes --> G[Return BEST local maximum] G --> H([End]) ``` **Mathematical Justification** (1 mark): Let $p$ be the probability of success in a single run. The expected number of restarts to find the global optimum is: $$ E[N] = \frac{1}{p} $$ For 8-Queens, with $p \approx 0.14$ (pure steepest-ascent) and $p \approx 0.94$ (with sideways moves), Random Restart HC is provably **complete with probability 1** as the number of restarts $\to \infty$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **⚡ RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Last-Minute KTU Board Prep** ### ✅ Must-Know Definitions - [x] **Hill Climbing** = greedy local search using heuristic $h(n)$. - [x] **Local Maximum** = better than all neighbors, but not globally best. - [x] **Heuristic $h(n)$** = estimated "goodness" of state $n$. - [x] **Successor / Neighbor** = state reachable by *one* legal move. - [x] **State Space** = set of all reachable states from initial state. ### ✅ Must-Know Formulas - [x] Stopping condition: $h(n_{next}) \leq h(n_{current})$ - [x] Time complexity: $O(b \cdot d)$ (Simple & Stochastic), $O(b^2 \cdot d)$ (Steepest) - [x] Space complexity: $O(1)$ - [x] Random restart expectation: $E[N] = \dfrac{1}{p}$ ### ✅ Must-Know Differences - [x] **Simple HC** = first better neighbor - [x] **Steepest-Ascent HC** = best of **all** neighbors - [x] **Stochastic HC** = random better neighbor - [x] **HC vs A\*** — HC uses only $h(n)$; A* uses $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ ### ✅ Must-Know Failure Modes ("L-P-R Trilogy") - [x] **L** = Local Maxima → Remedy: **Random Restart** - [x] **P** = Plateau → Remedy: **Sideways Move** - [x] **R** = Ridge → Remedy: **Bidirectional / Multi-direction Move** ### ✅ Must-Know Application - [x] **8-Queen problem** is the canonical KTU example. Heuristic = number of attacking pairs. ### ✅ Must-Know Code Snippets (for the Lab Record) - [x] `heuristic(board)` function for 8-Queens. - [x] `get_neighbors(board)` function. - [x] Three variants: `simple_hill_climbing`, `steepest_ascent_hill_climbing`, `stochastic_hill_climbing`. - [x] `solve_n_queens()` driver with **Random Restart** loop. > [!NOTE] > **Final Exam Tip:** When asked *"Implement a Program to Find a Solution Using the Local Optima (Hill Climbing) Algorithm"*, structure your answer as: > 1. **Algorithm explanation** (2 marks) > 2. **Heuristic design** (2 marks) > 3. **Code** (4–5 marks) > 4. **Sample I/O trace** (2 marks) > 5. **Failure mode discussion + remedy** (2–3 marks) > 6. **Complexity table** (1 mark)

topics.title: Implement Propositional Logic Inferences for AI Tasks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADL407 - FOUNDATIONS OF AI AND DATA SCIENCE LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implement Propositional Logic Inferences for AI Tasks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Propositional Logic (PL)** is a branch of mathematical logic that deals with propositions (statements that are either **TRUE** or **FALSE**, never both, never neither) and the logical relationships between them using connectives. In Artificial Intelligence, propositional logic is used as a foundational **knowledge representation and reasoning** formalism, where a *Knowledge Base (KB)* of logical statements is built and new facts are *inferred* using valid inference rules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme PCADL407 Lab syllabus, Module 2 mandates the student to *implement* propositional logic inference engines. The "inference" is the mechanical derivation of new logical consequences (sentences) from a set of existing premises using sound rules of inference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Vocabulary You Must Know for the Exam:**
> - **Proposition (Atomic Sentence):** A declarative statement with a definite truth value (e.g., *"It is raining"*).
> - **Complex Sentence:** Propositions joined by logical connectives.
> - **Knowledge Base (KB):** A set of sentences (propositions) known to be true in the world.
> - **Inference:** The process of deriving a new sentence $\alpha$ from KB, written as $KB \models \alpha$.
> - **Sound Rule:** A rule whose conclusions are true in every world where the premises are true.
> - **Complete Rule:** A rule that can derive any sentence that is entailed by the premises.

### 1.2 Intuition: The "Detective Analogy"

Imagine you are a detective at a crime scene. You have a notebook full of facts (your **Knowledge Base**): *The butler was in the library*, *The library window is broken*, *It rained last night*. You want to deduce a new fact: *The butler may have escaped through the window*.

You are not *guessing* — you are applying logical rules:
- If someone is the only person in a room with a broken window, AND the window leads outside, THEN that person could have escaped.
- This "IF... THEN..." chaining is exactly what propositional logic inference does in AI.

**AI Mapping:** In an AI system (like a medical diagnosis expert system or a smart robot):
- The *facts in KB* = sensor readings, user inputs, hard-coded rules.
- The *inference engine* = the "detective" that applies rules like **Modus Ponens** to derive new knowledge.
- The *conclusion* = an action or decision (e.g., *"The patient has Flu"*, *"Robot should turn left"*).

### 1.3 Standard Logical Connectives (Foundational Metrics)

| Symbol | Name | Read As | Python Equivalent |
|:------:|:-----|:--------|:------------------|
| $\neg P$ | Negation | NOT P | `not P` |
| $P \wedge Q$ | Conjunction | P AND Q | `P and Q` |
| $P \vee Q$ | Disjunction | P OR Q | `P or Q` |
| $P \rightarrow Q$ | Implication | IF P THEN Q | `(not P) or Q` |
| $P \leftrightarrow Q$ | Biconditional | P IFF Q | `(P and Q) or (not P and not Q)` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask for the **truth table** of an implication. Memorize: $P \rightarrow Q$ is **FALSE only** when $P$ is True and $Q$ is False. In all other three cases, it is **TRUE**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Propositional Logic Inferences Matter in AI

Propositional logic is the *bedrock* of symbolic AI. Even modern neural systems rely on logical principles for:
- **Rule-based expert systems** (MYCIN, DENDRAL).
- **Automated theorem provers** (used in hardware verification by Intel/AMD).
- **SAT solvers** (used in software verification, planning, scheduling).
- **Database query systems** (relational algebra is essentially propositional logic).

### 2.2 The "How" of Inference — The Reasoning Engine Architecture

A propositional inference engine typically operates in these structured stages:

1. **Input Phase:** Accept facts (premises) and rules (implications) from the user.
2. **Knowledge Base Construction:** Store them in a structured format (list of sentences, dict, or a graph).
3. **Rule Application:** Repeatedly apply a chosen *inference rule* (Modus Ponens, Modus Tollens, etc.) to the KB.
4. **Termination Check:** Stop when either the goal is proved, no new facts can be derived, or a time/memory bound is reached.
5. **Output:** Return the inferred proposition or indicate failure.

### 2.3 Core Inference Rules — The "Why" Behind Each Rule

| Rule | Logical Form | Plain English | Real-World Use in AI |
|:-----|:-------------|:--------------|:---------------------|
| **Modus Ponens (MP)** | $P \rightarrow Q, \; P \models Q$ | If P implies Q, and P is true, then Q must be true. | Medical diagnosis: *"If patient has fever AND cough, then flu"* + *"Patient has fever and cough"* $\Rightarrow$ *"Patient has flu"*. |
| **Modus Tollens (MT)** | $P \rightarrow Q, \; \neg Q \models \neg P$ | If P implies Q, and Q is false, then P is false. | Security: *"If logged in from new IP → alert"* + *"No alert"* $\Rightarrow$ *"Login from known IP"*. |
| **Hypothetical Syllogism (HS)** | $P \rightarrow Q, \; Q \rightarrow R \models P \rightarrow R$ | Transitivity of implication. | Routing: *"A→B, B→C, therefore A→C"*. |
| **Disjunctive Syllogism (DS)** | $P \vee Q, \; \neg P \models Q$ | One of two options ruled out leaves the other. | Planners: *"Take bus or train, bus unavailable, so take train"*. |
| **Resolution (RES)** | $P \vee Q, \; \neg Q \vee R \models P \vee R$ | Cancel complementary literals. | SAT solvers, automated theorem proving. |
| **Conjunction / Simplification** | $P \wedge Q \models P$ | From a conjunction, take any conjunct. | Sensor fusion: extracting individual readings. |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical Form | Implementation Note |
|:--------|:------------------|:--------------------|
| **Entailment** | $KB \models \alpha$ | KB logically entails $\alpha$ (true in all models of KB) |
| **Validity** | $\models \alpha$ | $\alpha$ is true in all possible worlds (a tautology) |
| **Satisfiability** | $KB \not\models \bot$ | KB has at least one model |
| **Contradiction** | $KB \models \bot$ | KB has NO model (unsatisfiable) |
| **De Morgan's Law 1** | $\neg(P \wedge Q) \equiv \neg P \vee \neg Q$ | Crucial for converting to CNF |
| **De Morgan's Law 2** | $\neg(P \vee Q) \equiv \neg P \wedge \neg Q$ | Crucial for converting to CNF |
| **Implication Elimination** | $P \rightarrow Q \equiv \neg P \vee Q$ | Required to put rules into CNF |
| **Biconditional Elimination** | $P \leftrightarrow Q \equiv (P \rightarrow Q) \wedge (Q \rightarrow P)$ | Used before resolution |
| **Double Negation** | $\neg(\neg P) \equiv P$ | Simplification |
| **Resolution Closure** | $KB \models \alpha \iff (KB \wedge \neg\alpha) \text{ is unsatisfiable}$ | Proof by contradiction technique |
| **Truth Value of Implication** | $P \rightarrow Q = \neg P \vee Q$ | Use to compute truth tables in Python |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical pipes `|x|` are used inside the table above** — KTU engine safety rule. Use `\vert` only when needed in inline math.

### 2.5 Truth Table — The Universal Tool

For $n$ propositions, there are exactly $2^n$ rows in the truth table. KTU frequently asks truth tables for expressions with 2 or 3 variables.

For a 2-variable expression $P \rightarrow Q$:

| $P$ | $Q$ | $P \rightarrow Q$ |
|:---:|:---:|:-----------------:|
| T | T | T |
| T | F | **F** |
| F | T | T |
| F | F | T |

For Modus Ponens, the compound proposition $((P \rightarrow Q) \wedge P) \rightarrow Q$ is a **tautology** (always true) — this is why MP is *sound*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Walk-Through: Proving $KB \models Q$ Using Modus Ponens

**Given Knowledge Base:**
- $\text{P1}: P \rightarrow Q$
- $\text{P2}: P$

**Step 1 — Identify the rule to apply.**
The KB contains an implication $P \rightarrow Q$ and the antecedent $P$. This matches the **Modus Ponens** template exactly.

**Step 2 — Match variables.**
In the MP template $A \rightarrow B, A \models B$, set $A = P$ and $B = Q$. This is a perfect match with the KB.

**Step 3 — Apply the rule.**
We conclude that $Q$ is entailed. Formally:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{KB} &= \{(P \rightarrow Q), \; P\} \\
\text{By MP: } & (P \rightarrow Q) \wedge P \models Q \\
\text{Therefore: } & KB \models Q
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification via truth table.**
The compound formula $((P \rightarrow Q) \wedge P) \rightarrow Q$ evaluates to **TRUE** for all combinations of $P$ and $Q$, confirming the rule is sound.

---

### 3.2 Algorithm: Truth Table Entailment Checker

This is one of the most common programs in the KTU lab exam.

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. Read the number of propositional symbols $n$.
2. Enumerate all $2^n$ truth assignments (models).
3. For each model, check if the KB is true.
4. If at least one model makes KB true, also check if the query $\alpha$ is true in that model.
5. If KB $\Rightarrow \alpha$ (KB implies $\alpha$), then return `ENTAILED`.

**Python Implementation (Lab-Ready):**

```python
def entails(KB_formula, query, symbols):
    """
    Checks if KB entails the query using the truth-table enumeration method.
    KB_formula: a Python lambda taking a dict of symbol->bool
    query:      a Python lambda taking a dict of symbol->bool
    symbols:    list of propositional symbol names, e.g. ['P', 'Q']
    """
    from itertools import product

    n = len(symbols)
    # Iterate over all 2^n truth assignments
    for values in product([True, False], repeat=n):
        # Build the assignment dictionary
        model = dict(zip(symbols, values))

        # Check if KB is satisfied in this model
        if KB_formula(model):
            # If KB is true, the query MUST be true for entailment
            if not query(model):
                return False   # Found a counter-model -> NOT entailed
    return True  # All KB-satisfying models also satisfy query
```

**How the algorithm maps to the math:**

$$
KB \models \alpha \iff \forall m, \; (m \models KB) \rightarrow (m \models \alpha)
$$

Equivalently, $KB \models \alpha$ is **TRUE** iff there is no model $m$ in which KB is TRUE but $\alpha$ is FALSE.

---

### 3.3 Algorithm: Resolution-Based Inference (Proof by Contradiction)

The resolution method is a **complete** inference procedure for propositional logic. The idea: to prove $KB \models \alpha$, show that $KB \wedge \neg\alpha$ is unsatisfiable (i.e., produces an empty clause $\square$).

**Step-by-step pipeline:**

1. Convert all KB sentences into **Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)** — a conjunction of disjunctions of literals.
2. Negate the query $\alpha$ and add it to the CNF clause set.
3. Repeatedly apply the **Resolution Rule** on pairs of clauses containing complementary literals.
4. If the empty clause is derived, return `ENTAILED` (proof found). Else, return `NOT PROVEN`.

**CNF Conversion Steps (write this down for the exam!):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step A: Eliminate} \rightarrow & : \quad (P \rightarrow Q) \equiv (\neg P \vee Q) \\
\text{Step B: Move} \neg \text{inwards (De Morgan):} & \quad \neg(P \vee Q) \equiv (\neg P \wedge \neg Q) \\
\text{Step C: Distribute} \vee \text{over} \wedge : & \quad (P \wedge Q) \vee R \equiv (P \vee R) \wedge (Q \vee R) \\
\text{Step D: Flatten into a set of clauses.} & \quad \text{Each clause is a set of literals.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Python Implementation (CNF + Resolution):**

```python
def to_cnf(sentence):
    """
    A simple recursive CNF converter for propositional sentences.
    Sentence is a nested tuple, e.g. ('or', ('not', 'P'), 'Q') for ~P v Q.
    Supported connectives: 'not', 'and', 'or', 'implies', 'iff'.
    """
    # Implication elimination: P -> Q  =>  ~P v Q
    if sentence[0] == 'implies':
        P, Q = sentence[1], sentence[2]
        sentence = ('or', ('not', P), Q)

    # Biconditional: P <-> Q  =>  (P -> Q) ^ (Q -> P)
    if sentence[0] == 'iff':
        P, Q = sentence[1], sentence[2]
        sentence = ('and', ('implies', P, Q), ('implies', Q, P))

    # De Morgan + Double negation
    if sentence[0] == 'not':
        A = sentence[1]
        if A[0] == 'not':           # ~~P  =>  P
            return to_cnf(A[1])
        if A[0] == 'and':           # ~(P ^ Q) => ~P v ~Q
            return to_cnf(('or', ('not', A[1]), ('not', A[2])))
        if A[0] == 'or':            # ~(P v Q) => ~P ^ ~Q
            return to_cnf(('and', ('not', A[1]), ('not', A[2])))

    # Distribution: (P ^ Q) v R  =>  (P v R) ^ (Q v R)
    if sentence[0] == 'or':
        P, Q = sentence[1], sentence[2]
        if P[0] == 'and':
            return to_cnf(('and', to_cnf(('or', P[1], Q)), to_cnf(('or', P[2], Q))))
        if Q[0] == 'and':
            return to_cnf(('and', to_cnf(('or', P, Q[1])), to_cnf(('or', P, Q[2]))))

    # AND: recurse on both sides
    if sentence[0] == 'and':
        return ('and', to_cnf(sentence[1]), to_cnf(sentence[2]))

    return sentence  # atomic literal


def resolve(clause_i, clause_j):
    """
    Resolution rule on two clauses (represented as frozensets of literals).
    Returns a new clause or None if resolution not applicable.
    A literal is ('not', 'P') or 'P'.
    """
    for lit in clause_i:
        complement = ('not', lit) if isinstance(lit, str) else lit[1]
        if complement in clause_j:
            new_clause = (clause_i - {lit}) | (clause_j - {complement})
            return frozenset(new_clause)
    return None


def resolution_entails(kb_cnf, query):
    """
    kb_cnf : set of frozenset clauses
    query  : a sentence in tuple form (will be negated and added)
    Returns True if KB entails query.
    """
    from copy import deepcopy

    # Negate the query
    negated_query = ('not', to_cnf(query))

    clauses = set(kb_cnf)
    # Add negated-query clauses into KB
    if negated_query[0] == 'and':
        clauses.add(frozenset(negated_query[1][1:]))   # simplified for demo
    else:
        clauses.add(frozenset([negated_query]))

    new = set()
    while True:
        pairs = [(ci, cj) for ci in clauses for cj in clauses if ci != cj]
        for (ci, cj) in pairs:
            resolvent = resolve(ci, cj)
            if resolvent is not None:
                if not resolvent:           # empty clause => contradiction
                    return True
                new.add(resolvent)
        if new.issubset(clauses):           # no new clauses => cannot prove
            return False
        clauses = clauses.union(new)
```

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Forward Chaining vs Backward Chaining

| Property | Forward Chaining | Backward Chaining |
|:---------|:-----------------|:------------------|
| **Direction** | Data-driven (premises $\rightarrow$ conclusion) | Goal-driven (conclusion $\rightarrow$ premises) |
| **Starts From** | Known facts | Query / hypothesis |
| **Stops When** | No new facts can be derived OR goal found | Goal is proved OR cannot be proved |
| **Best For** | Monitoring, planning, real-time systems | Diagnostic, query-answering systems |
| **Implementation in PL** | Iterate Modus Ponens over KB until fixed point | Recursively check if query is in KB or implied |
| **Complexity** | Linear in KB size (for Horn clauses) | Linear in KB size (for Horn clauses) |

**Forward Chaining Algorithm Outline:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Input: } KB \text{ (set of definite clauses)}, \text{query } q \\
&\textbf{Output: } \text{True if } KB \models q \\
&1. \text{count}[c] = \text{number of premises in clause } c \\
&2. \text{inferred}[s] = \text{False for all symbols } s \\
&3. \text{queue} \leftarrow \text{all facts (clauses with no premises)} \\
&4. \textbf{while } \text{queue not empty:} \\
&5. \quad p \leftarrow \text{pop}(queue) \\
&6. \quad \textbf{if } p == q: \text{ return True} \\
&7. \quad \textbf{if not } \text{inferred}[p]: \\
&8. \quad\quad \text{inferred}[p] = \text{True} \\
&9. \quad\quad \textbf{for each clause } c \text{ where } p \text{ is a premise:} \\
&10. \quad\quad\quad \text{count}[c] \text{ -= } 1 \\
&11. \quad\quad\quad \textbf{if } \text{count}[c] == 0: \text{ push}(c.conclusion, queue) \\
&12. \text{return False}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Propositional Inference Engine — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Inputs Facts and Rules] --> B[Tokenizer and Parser]
    B --> C[Knowledge Base Builder]
    C --> D[Inferred Facts Store]
    D --> E[Inference Engine Core]
    E --> F[Modus Ponens Module]
    E --> G[Modus Tollens Module]
    E --> H[Resolution Module]
    E --> I[Forward Chaining Module]
    E --> J[Backward Chaining Module]
    F --> K[New Fact Generated]
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> D
    K --> L{Goal Reached?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Return ENTAILED]
    L -- No --> N[Add Fact to KB and Continue]
    N --> E
    L -- Exhausted --> O[Return NOT ENTAILED]
```

### 4.2 Forward Chaining — Data-Driven Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Fact: P] --> MP1
    F2[Fact: P implies Q] --> MP1
    MP1[Apply Modus Ponens] --> F3[Inferred: Q]
    F3 --> MP2
    F4[Fact: Q implies R] --> MP2
    MP2[Apply Modus Ponens] --> F5[Inferred: R]
    F5 --> Goal{Is R the Query?}
    Goal -- Yes --> Done[ENTAILED: KB proves R]
    Goal -- No --> Loop[Add R to KB and Repeat]
```

### 4.3 Resolution Proof — Contradiction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: KB and query alpha] --> S2[Convert KB to CNF]
    S2 --> S3[Negate query and add as clause]
    S3 --> S4[Initialize clause set C]
    S4 --> S5[Pick two clauses with complementary literals]
    S5 --> S6[Apply Resolution Rule]
    S6 --> S7{Empty clause derived?}
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Return ENTAILED: proof complete]
    S7 -- No --> S9[Add resolvent to C]
    S9 --> S10{New clauses generated?}
    S10 -- Yes --> S5
    S10 -- No --> S11[Return NOT ENTAILED]
```
