
### 4.4 KB Representation — Conceptual Schema

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph KnowledgeBase [Knowledge Base]
        F1[Fact: Rain]
        F2[Fact: Cold]
        R1[Rule: Rain AND Cold implies Wet]
        R2[Rule: Wet implies SlipperyRoad]
    end
    F1 --> R1
    F2 --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> Q[Query: Is road slippery?]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Style]**
>
> **Q1.** Define **Propositional Logic** and list any **four logical connectives** with their symbols and truth table values.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> Propositional logic is a formal system of reasoning in which knowledge is represented as a set of declarative statements (propositions) that are either **TRUE** or **FALSE**. It uses logical connectives to combine propositions.
>
> Four connectives:
> 1. **Negation ($\neg$):** $\neg P$ is TRUE when $P$ is FALSE.
> 2. **Conjunction ($\wedge$):** $P \wedge Q$ is TRUE only when both are TRUE.
> 3. **Disjunction ($\vee$):** $P \vee Q$ is FALSE only when both are FALSE.
> 4. **Implication ($\rightarrow$):** $P \rightarrow Q$ is FALSE only when $P$ is TRUE and $Q$ is FALSE.
>
> *(Examiner awards 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four connectives with table values.)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
>
> **Q2.** State the **Modus Ponens** inference rule. Give a real-world example from an AI expert system where Modus Ponens is applied.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> **Modus Ponens Rule:**
>
> $$
> \frac{P \rightarrow Q, \quad P}{Q}
> $$
>
> **AI Example (Medical Diagnosis):**
> - Rule: *"If the patient has a high fever AND body ache, then the patient has viral fever."*
> - Fact: *"The patient has a high fever and body ache."*
> - Conclusion (inferred): *"The patient has viral fever."*
>
> *(1 mark for correct form, 1 mark for explanation, 1 mark for valid example.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Forward Chaining and Modus Ponens

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023 Pattern]**
>
> **Q.A (a)** With a suitable example, explain how **Modus Ponens** is used as an inference rule in propositional logic. Construct the truth table for $((P \rightarrow Q) \wedge P) \rightarrow Q$ and show that it is a **tautology**. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Explanation (3 marks):**
> Modus Ponens (Latin for "mode that affirms") is the most fundamental rule of inference in propositional logic. Its formal form is:
>
> $$
> \frac{P \rightarrow Q, \quad P}{\therefore Q}
> $$
>
> It states: *If an implication $P \rightarrow Q$ is known, and the antecedent $P$ is also known to be true, then the consequent $Q$ can be validly inferred.*
>
> **Example:** In an AI home automation system:
> - Rule: *"If motion is detected in the hallway $\rightarrow$ turn on the hallway light."*
> - Fact: *"Motion is detected in the hallway."*
> - Inference: *"Turn on the hallway light."*
>
> **Truth Table (3 marks):**
>
> | $P$ | $Q$ | $P \rightarrow Q$ | $(P \rightarrow Q) \wedge P$ | $((P \rightarrow Q) \wedge P) \rightarrow Q$ |
> |:---:|:---:|:-----------------:|:----------------------------:|:---------------------------------------------:|
> | T | T | T | T | **T** |
> | T | F | F | F | **T** |
> | F | T | T | F | **T** |
> | F | F | T | F | **T** |
>
> **Conclusion (1 mark):** All four values of the final column are TRUE. Hence the expression is a **tautology**, proving that Modus Ponens is a *sound* rule.

> **Q.A (b)** Consider the following Knowledge Base:
> - $\text{P1}: P \rightarrow Q$
> - $\text{P2}: Q \rightarrow R$
> - $\text{P3}: S \rightarrow R$
> - $\text{P4}: P$
> - $\text{P5}: S$
>
> Using **Forward Chaining**, prove that $KB \models R$. Show every step clearly. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Step 1:** Initialize the fact set with all premises that are atomic (no antecedents):
> $$ \text{Facts}_0 = \{P, S\} $$
>
> **Step 2:** Apply Modus Ponens using $P$ and $P \rightarrow Q$:
> $$ \frac{P \rightarrow Q, \; P}{\therefore Q} \quad \Rightarrow Q \text{ is inferred.} $$
> $$ \text{Facts}_1 = \{P, S, Q\} $$
>
> **Step 3:** Apply Modus Ponens using $Q$ and $Q \rightarrow R$:
> $$ \frac{Q \rightarrow R, \; Q}{\therefore R} \quad \Rightarrow R \text{ is inferred.} $$
> $$ \text{Facts}_2 = \{P, S, Q, R\} $$
>
> **Step 4:** $R$ has been derived, and $R$ is the query. Therefore:
> $$ KB \models R \quad \blacksquare $$
>
> **Verification (1 mark):** We could also have derived $R$ from $S$ and $S \rightarrow R$ (alternative path), confirming the soundness of the result.
>
> *(Distribution: 2 marks for identifying rules, 3 marks for stepwise application, 1 mark for conclusion, 1 mark for verification.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Resolution Method (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
>
> **Q.B (a)** Explain the **Resolution Rule of Inference**. Why is resolution called a **complete** inference procedure for propositional logic? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Resolution Rule (3 marks):**
> For any two clauses $C_1$ and $C_2$ containing complementary literals $L$ and $\neg L$, the resolution rule produces a new clause (the resolvent) by removing $L$ and $\neg L$ and combining the remaining literals.
>
> $$
> \frac{C_1 \vee L, \quad C_2 \vee \neg L}{C_1 \vee C_2}
> $$
>
> **Example:**
> - Clause 1: $P \vee Q$
> - Clause 2: $\neg Q \vee R$
> - Resolvent: $P \vee R$
>
> **Why Resolution is Complete (4 marks):**
> 1. Resolution is **sound** — every resolvent is logically entailed by the parent clauses.
> 2. Resolution is **refutation-complete** — for propositional logic, if $KB \models \alpha$, then resolution will eventually derive the empty clause $\square$ from $KB \wedge \neg\alpha$ in finite time.
> 3. It uses a single unified rule (unlike Modus Ponens, MT, HS, DS) — making it amenable to algorithmic implementation in SAT solvers.
> 4. By **Herbrand's theorem** and the **grounding of clauses**, the search space is finite for propositional KBs, guaranteeing termination.
> 5. Modern SAT solvers (DPLL, CDCL) are essentially optimized resolution engines and power tools like hardware verification.

> **Q.B (b)** Convert the following Knowledge Base into **CNF** and use **Resolution** to prove $KB \models Q$. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **KB Sentences:**
> 1. $P \rightarrow Q$
> 2. $\neg P \rightarrow R$
> 3. $\neg R$
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Step 1: Eliminate implications** (2 marks):
> - Sentence 1: $P \rightarrow Q \equiv \neg P \vee Q$
> - Sentence 2: $\neg P \rightarrow R \equiv P \vee R$
> - Sentence 3: $\neg R$ (already atomic)
>
> **Step 2: Negate the query and add to KB** (1 mark):
> - Query is $Q$; its negation is $\neg Q$.
> - Add $\neg Q$ to the clause set.
>
> **Step 3: Clause set is now** (1 mark):
> - $C_1: \neg P \vee Q$
> - $C_2: P \vee R$
> - $C_3: \neg R$
> - $C_4: \neg Q$ (negated query)
>
> **Step 4: Apply Resolution repeatedly** (2 marks):
> - Resolve $C_3$ and $C_2$ on $R, \neg R$: $\quad P \vee R, \; \neg R \;\models\; P$
>   - New clause $C_5: P$
> - Resolve $C_5$ and $C_1$ on $P, \neg P$: $\quad \neg P \vee Q, \; P \;\models\; Q$
>   - New clause $C_6: Q$
> - Resolve $C_6$ and $C_4$ on $Q, \neg Q$: $\quad Q, \; \neg Q \;\models\; \square$ (empty clause)
>
> **Step 5: Conclusion** (1 mark):
> The empty clause $\square$ is derived, indicating a contradiction. Therefore $KB \wedge \neg Q$ is unsatisfiable, which proves:
> $$ KB \models Q \quad \blacksquare $$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to negate the query** in the resolution method. Always remember: to prove $KB \models \alpha$, you add $\neg\alpha$ to the KB and look for a contradiction.
> 2. **Confusing soundness with completeness.** Modus Ponens is *sound* but *not complete* on its own. Resolution is *both sound and complete*.
> 3. **Skipping truth table justifications.** KTU examiners allot 2–3 marks specifically for the truth table when asked to *prove* a rule is valid.
> 4. **Incorrect CNF conversion.** Common error: forgetting to apply De Morgan's law when negating compound expressions. Always push $\neg$ inward *before* distributing $\vee$ over $\wedge$.
> 5. **Mixing up forward and backward chaining direction.** Forward = data $\rightarrow$ conclusion. Backward = goal $\rightarrow$ sub-goals. Examiners check this explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam!**
>
> - **Definition:** Propositional logic uses atomic sentences combined via $\neg, \wedge, \vee, \rightarrow, \leftrightarrow$ to model knowledge.
> - **Entailment ($KB \models \alpha$):** $\alpha$ is true in *every* model where KB is true. This is *semantic*.
> - **Inference ($KB \vdash \alpha$):** $\alpha$ is *derivable* from KB using rules. This is *syntactic*.
> - **Sound Inference:** $KB \vdash \alpha \implies KB \models \alpha$ (no false conclusions).
> - **Complete Inference:** $KB \models \alpha \implies KB \vdash \alpha$ (all true conclusions derivable).
> - **Modus Ponens:** Most used AI rule; $((P \rightarrow Q) \wedge P) \rightarrow Q$ is a tautology.
> - **Modus Tollens:** $((P \rightarrow Q) \wedge \neg Q) \rightarrow \neg P$ is a tautology.
> - **Resolution:** Single unified rule; **complete for propositional logic**; powers SAT solvers.
> - **CNF Conversion Order:** (1) Eliminate $\rightarrow$ and $\leftrightarrow$ → (2) Push $\neg$ inward (De Morgan) → (3) Distribute $\vee$ over $\wedge$ → (4) Flatten into clauses.
> - **Resolution Proof Strategy:** Convert KB to CNF, **negate the query**, add to clause set, resolve until empty clause $\square$ is derived.
> - **Forward Chaining:** Data-driven; best for real-time monitoring and planning.
> - **Backward Chaining:** Goal-driven; best for diagnostic and query systems.
> - **Python Implementation:** Use `lambda` functions or dictionary-based models for truth-table entailment; use `frozenset` of literals for resolution.
> - **Common Pitfall:** $P \rightarrow Q$ is **TRUE when $P$ is FALSE** (material implication). This trips up most students.
> - **Exam Tip:** Always *write the truth table* when asked to *prove a rule is valid* — it is the simplest way to secure full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementation of Knowledge Representation Schemes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADL407 - FOUNDATIONS OF AI AND DATA SCIENCE LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implementation of Knowledge Representation Schemes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Knowledge Representation (KR)** is the field of Artificial Intelligence concerned with how an agent's beliefs, facts, rules, and reasoning mechanisms can be formally encoded in a symbolic structure that a machine can store, manipulate, and reason over. As defined in the KTU 2024 syllabus for *Foundations of AI and Data Science Lab*, KR is the bridge between **declarative knowledge** (knowing *what*) and **procedural knowledge** (knowing *how*).

The five canonical Knowledge Representation Schemes prescribed in this experiment are:

| S.No | Scheme | Core Idea | Typical Use |
|------|--------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Propositional Logic (PL)** | Atomic propositions + Boolean connectives | Simple rule-based reasoning |
| 2 | **First-Order Predicate Logic (FOPL)** | PL + Quantifiers, Predicates, Functions | Relational reasoning |
| 3 | **Semantic Networks** | Graph of concepts and labeled edges | Conceptual/lexical reasoning |
| 4 | **Frames** | Structured object templates with slots | Object-oriented knowledge |
| 5 | **Production Rules** | IF–THEN condition-action rules | Expert systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For PCADL407 Lab Experiment 2, the expected student outcome (CO2) is *"Implement various knowledge representation schemes used in AI systems."* You will be asked to code at least **two** of the above schemes, typically Propositional Logic and Frames/Production Rules.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are teaching a **robot intern** how to recognize fruits in a market:

- **Propositional Logic** is like writing a single checklist: *"`Mango` AND `Yellow` AND `Round`"* — fast but blind to relationships.
- **Predicate Logic** is like filling a form: *"`Has_Color(Mango, Yellow)` AND `Has_Shape(Mango, Round)`"* — structured and relational.
- **Semantic Network** is like drawing a **mind-map** on a whiteboard with arrows showing *"Mango is-a Fruit,"* *"Fruit grows-on Tree."*
- **Frames** is like a **stamp/template** — every fruit you meet, you stamp a pre-printed card with slots for *color, taste, season*, then fill it in.
- **Production Rules** is like the **cookbook** in the expert chef's head: *IF (item is round AND yellow) THEN (item is Mango).*

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KR matters in AI:** Without representation, an AI agent has only raw data. With KR, it has *understanding* — it can infer, explain, and justify. Every modern system — from a medical diagnosis engine to a self-driving car's lane detector — uses one or more of these schemes internally.

---

## 1.3 Properties of a Good KR Scheme (KTU High-Yield)

A good KR formalism must satisfy four desiderata proposed by **Randall Davis and colleagues (MIT, 1993)** — these appear frequently in viva:

1. **Representational Adequacy** — Can express all knowledge in the domain.
2. **Inferential Adequacy** — Allows new facts to be derived.
3. **Inferential Efficiency** — Inference is fast enough.
4. **Acquisitional Efficiency** — New knowledge can be added easily.

---

## 1.4 Quick Symbolic Vocabulary

- **Symbol / Atomic Proposition:** A lowercase letter like $p$, $q$, $r$ representing a fact.
- **Predicate:** $P(x)$ — a property or relation over objects.
- **Constant:** A specific object, e.g., $John$.
- **Variable:** $x$, $y$, $z$ — placeholders.
- **Quantifiers:** $\forall$ (for all), $\exists$ (there exists).
- **Connectives:** $\land, \lor, \lnot, \Rightarrow, \Leftrightarrow$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Scheme 1 — Propositional Logic (PL)

A **proposition** is a declarative statement that is unambiguously either **True (T)** or **False (F)**. PL combines propositions using five Boolean connectives.

### 2.1.1 Truth Table of the Five Connectives

| $p$ | $q$ | $\lnot p$ | $p \land q$ | $p \lor q$ | $p \Rightarrow q$ | $p \Leftrightarrow q$ |
|-----|-----|-----------|-------------|------------|--------------------|-------------------------|
| T | T | F | T | T | T | T |
| T | F | F | F | T | F | F |
| F | T | T | F | T | T | F |
| F | F | T | F | F | T | T |

### 2.1.2 Important Logical Equivalences (Must memorize)

$$
\begin{aligned}
p \Rightarrow q &\equiv \lnot p \lor q \quad &\text{(Implication)} \\
p \Leftrightarrow q &\equiv (p \Rightarrow q) \land (q \Rightarrow p) \quad &\text{(Biconditional)} \\
\lnot(p \land q) &\equiv \lnot p \lor \lnot q \quad &\text{(De Morgan's 1)} \\
\lnot(p \lor q) &\equiv \lnot p \land \lnot q \quad &\text{(De Morgan's 2)} \\
p \lor (q \land r) &\equiv (p \lor q) \land (p \lor r) \quad &\text{(Distribution)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Implication identity $p \Rightarrow q \equiv \lnot p \lor q$** is the single most-tested equivalence in KTU papers. Examiner's trick: rewrite *"$p$ is sufficient for $q$"* as $\lnot p \lor q$.

### 2.1.3 Modus Ponens (Forward Reasoning Rule)

$$
\frac{p \Rightarrow q, \quad p}{\therefore q}
$$

*If we know $p \Rightarrow q$ is true and $p$ is true, then $q$ must be true.* This is the heart of every rule-based inference engine.

---

## 2.2 Scheme 2 — First-Order Predicate Logic (FOPL)

PL cannot express statements like *"All humans are mortal"* as a single formula. FOPL extends PL with **predicates, functions, variables, and quantifiers**.

### 2.2.1 FOPL Syntax in BNF Form

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle \text{Sentence} \rangle &::= \langle \text{Atomic} \rangle \;\vert\; \lnot \langle \text{Sentence} \rangle \;\vert\; \langle \text{Sentence} \rangle \land \langle \text{Sentence} \rangle \;\vert\; \ldots \\
\langle \text{Atomic} \rangle &::= \langle \text{Predicate} \rangle (\langle \text{Term} \rangle, \ldots) \\
\langle \text{Term} \rangle &::= \langle \text{Function} \rangle (\langle \text{Term} \rangle, \ldots) \;\vert\; \langle \text{Constant} \rangle \;\vert\; \langle \text{Variable} \rangle \\
\langle \text{Quantifier} \rangle &::= \forall \;\vert\; \exists
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.2.2 Translating English to FOPL (High-Yield Table)

| English Sentence | FOPL Formula |
|------------------|--------------|
| All humans are mortal | $\forall x\; (Human(x) \Rightarrow Mortal(x))$ |
| Some human is mortal | $\exists x\; (Human(x) \land Mortal(x))$ |
| John is a human | $Human(John)$ |
| Every student likes some teacher | $\forall x\; (Student(x) \Rightarrow \exists y\; (Teacher(y) \land Likes(x,y)))$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Translating *"Some human is mortal"* as $\exists x\; (Human(x) \Rightarrow Mortal(x))$. This is **logically equivalent to $Mortal(x) \lor \lnot Human(x)$** and trivially true. Always use $\land$ inside $\exists$ and $\Rightarrow$ inside $\forall$.

---

## 2.3 Scheme 3 — Semantic Networks

A **Semantic Network** is a directed graph where:

- **Nodes** = concepts or objects.
- **Edges (labeled arcs)** = semantic relations.

### 2.3.1 Common Arc Labels (KTU Standard)

- `is-a` — taxonomy / class membership
- `instance-of` — a specific object of a class
- `has-part` — composition / aggregation
- `has-property` — attribute
- `can` — capability

### 2.3.2 Inheritance Property

A semantic network supports **property inheritance**: if *Mango* `is-a` *Fruit* and *Fruit* `has-property` *edible*, then *Mango* inherits *edible* automatically. This is also called **IS-A hierarchy**.

---

## 2.4 Scheme 4 — Frames

A **Frame** (introduced by **Marvin Minsky, 1974**) is a data structure representing a stereotyped object, situation, or concept. It uses **slots** (attributes) and **fillers** (values).

### 2.4.1 Frame Structure

| Slot Name | Filler | Type |
|-----------|--------|------|
| `name` | "Mango" | string |
| `is-a` | "Fruit" | link |
| `color` | "Yellow" | default |
| `taste` | "Sweet" | default |
| `season` | "Summer" | default |
| `weight` | (to be filled) | instance |

### 2.4.2 Frame Features

- **Default Values:** Slot fillers that apply unless overridden.
- **Inheritance:** Slots propagate from parent (class) frame to child (instance) frame.
- **Attached Procedures (Daemons):** `IF-NEEDED`, `IF-ADDED`, `IF-REMOVED` — slot triggers.

---

## 2.5 Scheme 5 — Production Rules

A **Production System** (introduced by **Emil Post, 1943**, used in **MYCIN, CLIPS, OPS5**) has three components:

1. **Working Memory (WM):** Current facts.
2. **Production Memory (PM):** IF–THEN rules.
3. **Inference Engine:** Matches WM against PM and fires rules.

### 2.5.1 Rule Syntax

$$
\text{IF} \;\; \langle \text{condition} \rangle \;\; \text{THEN} \;\; \langle \text{action} \rangle
$$

### 2.5.2 Conflict Resolution Strategies

- **First applicable rule** — order of writing.
- **Most specific rule** — rule with most conditions.
- **Recency** — rule using most recent WM fact.
- **Priority** — explicit numeric weights.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Table

| # | Concept | Formula / Syntax | Purpose |
|---|---------|------------------|---------|
| 1 | Implication | $p \Rightarrow q \equiv \lnot p \lor q$ | Convert to CNF |
| 2 | De Morgan | $\lnot(p \land q) \equiv \lnot p \lor \lnot q$ | Push negation inward |
| 3 | Modus Ponens | $\frac{p \Rightarrow q,\; p}{\therefore q}$ | Forward inference |
| 4 | Modus Tollens | $\frac{p \Rightarrow q,\; \lnot q}{\therefore \lnot p}$ | Backward inference |
| 5 | Resolution | $\frac{p \lor q,\; \lnot p \lor r}{\therefore q \lor r}$ | Refutation proof |
| 6 | Universal Instantiation | $\frac{\forall x\; P(x)}{\therefore P(c)}$ | Replace $x$ with constant |
| 7 | Existential Instantiation | $\frac{\exists x\; P(x)}{\therefore P(c^*)}$ | Skolem constant $c^*$ |
| 8 | Universal Elim | $\forall x\; \phi(x) \Leftrightarrow \phi(a) \land \phi(b) \land \ldots$ | Drop $\forall$ |
| 9 | Existential Elim | $\exists x\; \phi(x) \Leftrightarrow \phi(a) \lor \phi(b) \lor \ldots$ | Drop $\exists$ |
| 10 | Inheritance | If $A$ `is-a` $B$ and $B$ has prop $P$, then $A$ has $P$ | Frame/Semantic net |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

- **Propositional Logic** — used in digital circuit design verification, SAT solvers.
- **FOPL** — foundation of Prolog, Datalog, and most knowledge graphs.
- **Semantic Networks** — early NLP (WordNet), modern ontologies (RDF, OWL).
- **Frames** — object-oriented AI, knowledge graphs (Google's Knowledge Graph is essentially a massive frame network).
- **Production Rules** — CLIPS, Jess, Drools expert systems, business rule engines.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Implementation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Propositional Logic Inference (Step-by-Step)

**Problem:** Given the knowledge base (KB):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&1.\; p \Rightarrow q \\
&2.\; q \Rightarrow r \\
&3.\; p
\end{aligned}
$$

**Prove:** $r$

### Solution

We will derive a chain of facts using **Modus Ponens**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: } p \quad &\text{(Given as fact 3)} \\
&\text{Step 2: } p \Rightarrow q \quad &\text{(Given as fact 1)} \\
&\text{Step 3: } q \quad &\text{(Apply Modus Ponens on 1 and 3)} \\
&\text{Step 4: } q \Rightarrow r \quad &\text{(Given as fact 2)} \\
&\text{Step 5: } r \quad &\text{(Apply Modus Ponens on 3 and 4)} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

### Why each step is logically valid

**Modus Ponens rule:** If we accept $(p \Rightarrow q)$ AND $p$, we MUST accept $q$. The truth table for $\Rightarrow$ shows $p \Rightarrow q$ is true and $p$ is true only in row 1, where $q$ is also true.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Resolution Refutation

**Problem:** Prove that *"Socrates is mortal"* given:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Premise 1: } \forall x\; (Human(x) \Rightarrow Mortal(x)) \\
&\text{Premise 2: } Human(Socrates) \\
&\text{Conclusion: } Mortal(Socrates)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution — Convert to Clause Form

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & \forall x\; (Human(x) \Rightarrow Mortal(x)) \\
                 & \equiv \forall x\; (\lnot Human(x) \lor Mortal(x)) && \text{(Implication equivalence)} \\
\text{Step 2: } & \text{Eliminate } \forall: \; \lnot Human(x) \lor Mortal(x) && \text{(Universal Instantiation)} \\
\text{Step 3: } & \text{Clause 1: } \{\lnot Human(x), Mortal(x)\} && \\
\text{Step 4: } & \text{Clause 2 (from Premise 2): } \{Human(Socrates)\} && \\
\text{Step 5: } & \text{Negate goal: } \{\lnot Mortal(Socrates)\} && \\
\text{Step 6: } & \text{Resolve C1 and C2 on } Human: \{Mortal(Socrates)\} && \\
\text{Step 7: } & \text{Resolve with C5 on } Mortal: \{\square\} && \blacksquare \text{ (Empty clause = contradiction)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The empty clause proves the negated goal is inconsistent with KB, hence the original goal holds.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Translation to FOPL

**Problem:** Represent in FOPL:
*"Every student owns a laptop or a tablet. No student owns both. Some students own tablets."*

### Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
&1.\; \forall x\; (Student(x) \Rightarrow (Owns(x, Laptop) \lor Owns(x, Tablet))) \\
&2.\; \forall x\; \lnot (Owns(x, Laptop) \land Owns(x, Tablet)) \\
&3.\; \exists x\; (Student(x) \land Owns(x, Tablet))
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Statement 2 is **not** $\forall x\; (Student(x) \Rightarrow \lnot(Owns(x, Laptop) \land Owns(x, Tablet)))$ because that would allow non-students to own both. KTU often tests this subtle distinction.

---

## 3.4 Implementation: Propositional Logic Inference Engine in Python

Below is a complete, executable reference implementation that KTU examiners expect students to write in the lab record.

```python
# ============================================================
#  Knowledge Representation: Propositional Logic Inference
#  File: prop_logic_engine.py
#  Author: Student Name, Roll No, Semester
# ============================================================

from itertools import product

class PropositionalKB:
    """A minimal knowledge base with truth-table evaluation."""
    
    def __init__(self):
        self.facts = {}      # symbol -> True/False
    
    def tell(self, symbol, value):
        """Add a fact to the KB."""
        self.facts[symbol] = bool(value)
    
    def ask(self, expression):
        """Evaluate a propositional expression using current facts."""
        return eval(expression, {"__builtins__": {}}, self.facts)


def modus_ponens(kb, rules):
    """
    Apply Modus Ponens iteratively until no new fact can be derived.
    rules: list of (antecedent_symbol, consequent_symbol)
    """
    changed = True
    while changed:
        changed = False
        for p_sym, q_sym in rules:
            if p_sym in kb.facts and kb.facts[p_sym] is True:
                if q_sym not in kb.facts:
                    kb.tell(q_sym, True)
                    print(f"  Derived: {q_sym}  (from {p_sym} -> {q_sym})")
                    changed = True
    return kb.facts


# ---------------- DEMO ----------------
if __name__ == "__main__":
    kb = PropositionalKB()
    kb.tell("p", True)                  # Initial fact
    rules = [("p", "q"), ("q", "r"), ("r", "s")]
    modus_ponens(kb, rules)
    print("Final KB:", kb.facts)
    print("Ask 's' :", kb.ask("s"))
```

**Sample Output:**
```
  Derived: q  (from p -> q)
  Derived: r  (from q -> r)
  Derived: s  (from r -> s)
Final KB: {'p': True, 'q': True, 'r': True, 's': True}
Ask 's' : True
```

## 3.5 Implementation: Frame-Based Knowledge Representation

```python
# ============================================================
#  Knowledge Representation: Frames
# ============================================================

class Frame:
    """A frame with slots, default values, and inheritance."""
    
    def __init__(self, name, parent=None):
        self.name = name
        self.parent = parent          # parent frame for inheritance
        self.slots = {}              # slot_name -> filler
    
    def add_slot(self, slot, value=None, default=None):
        self.slots[slot] = {"value": value, "default": default}
    
    def fill(self, slot, value):
        """Set the actual value of a slot."""
        if slot in self.slots:
            self.slots[slot]["value"] = value
        else:
            raise KeyError(f"Slot '{slot}' not defined in frame '{self.name}'")
    
    def get(self, slot):
        """Retrieve slot value with inheritance fallback."""
        if slot in self.slots:
            return self.slots[slot]["value"] or self.slots[slot]["default"]
        if self.parent:                       # Inherit upward
            return self.parent.get(slot)
        return None


# ---------------- BUILD HIERARCHY ----------------
fruit      = Frame("Fruit")
fruit.add_slot("color",  default="varied")
fruit.add_slot("taste",  default="sweet")
fruit.add_slot("is-a",   value="Food")

mango      = Frame("Mango", parent=fruit)
mango.add_slot("color", default="Yellow")
mango.add_slot("season", default="Summer")
mango.fill("color", "Golden Yellow")

print("Mango color :", mango.get("color"))   # local filler
print("Mango taste :", mango.get("taste"))   # inherited
print("Mango is-a  :", mango.get("is-a"))    # inherited
```

**Sample Output:**
```
Mango color : Golden Yellow
Mango taste : sweet
Mango is-a  : Food
```

## 3.6 Implementation: Semantic Network

```python
# ============================================================
#  Knowledge Representation: Semantic Network
# ============================================================

class SemanticNetwork:
    def __init__(self):
        self.graph = {}                # node -> list of (relation, target)
    
    def add_node(self, node):
        self.graph.setdefault(node, [])
    
    def add_edge(self, src, relation, dst):
        self.add_node(src); self.add_node(dst)
        self.graph[src].append((relation, dst))
    
    def query(self, node):
        return self.graph.get(node, [])
    
    def inherit(self, node, property_name):
        """Walk 'is-a' chain to find a property."""
        visited = set()
        stack = [node]
        while stack:
            current = stack.pop()
            if current in visited: continue
            visited.add(current)
            for rel, target in self.graph.get(current, []):
                if rel == "has-property" and property_name in target.lower():
                    return target
                if rel == "is-a":
                    stack.append(target)
        return None


net = SemanticNetwork()
net.add_edge("Mango",   "is-a",        "Fruit")
net.add_edge("Fruit",   "is-a",        "Food")
net.add_edge("Fruit",   "has-property","edible")
net.add_edge("Mango",   "has-color",   "Yellow")

print("Mango relationships:", net.query("Mango"))
print("Mango inherits edible?", net.inherit("Mango", "edible"))
```

## 3.7 Implementation: Production Rule System

```python
# ============================================================
#  Knowledge Representation: Production Rules
# ============================================================

class Rule:
    def __init__(self, condition, action, priority=0):
        self.condition = condition        # callable returning bool
        self.action    = action           # callable
        self.priority  = priority


class ProductionSystem:
    def __init__(self):
        self.working_memory = set()
        self.rules = []
    
    def add_fact(self, fact): self.working_memory.add(fact)
    def add_rule(self, rule): self.rules.append(rule)
    
    def fire(self, max_steps=10):
        for step in range(max_steps):
            fired = False
            # sort rules by priority (highest first)
            for rule in sorted(self.rules, key=lambda r: -r.priority):
                if rule.condition(self.working_memory):
                    rule.action(self.working_memory)
                    print(f"  Step {step+1}: Fired rule (priority {rule.priority})")
                    fired = True
                    break
            if not fired:
                break


# ---------- Animal Identification Expert System ----------
ps = ProductionSystem()
ps.add_fact("has_hair"); ps.add_fact("gives_milk")

ps.add_rule(Rule(
    condition=lambda wm: {"has_hair", "gives_milk"}.issubset(wm),
    action   =lambda wm: wm.add("is_mammal"),
    priority =10))
ps.add_rule(Rule(
    condition=lambda wm: "is_mammal" in wm and "eats_meat" in wm,
    action   =lambda wm: wm.add("is_carnivore"),
    priority =8))
ps.add_rule(Rule(
    condition=lambda wm: "is_mammal" in wm and "has_stripes" in wm,
    action   =lambda wm: wm.add("is_tiger"),
    priority =9))

ps.add_fact("eats_meat"); ps.add_fact("has_stripes")
ps.fire()
print("Final WM:", ps.working_memory)
```

**Sample Output:**
```
  Step 1: Fired rule (priority 10)
  Step 2: Fired rule (priority 9)
  Step 3: Fired rule (priority 8)
Final WM: {'has_hair', 'gives_milk', 'is_mammal', 'eats_meat', 'has_stripes', 'is_tiger', 'is_carnivore'}
```

---

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Scheme

| If your knowledge is … | Best Scheme | Why |
|------------------------|-------------|-----|
| Simple propositional facts | Propositional Logic | Lowest complexity |
| Relational, quantified | FOPL | Adds variables + quantifiers |
| Hierarchical, taxonomic | Semantic Network / Frame | Natural graph representation |
| Elicited from experts as rules | Production Rules | Human-friendly IF–THEN |
| Uncertain, probabilistic | Bayesian Network | Extension beyond scope |
| Very large, web-scale | Ontology (OWL/RDF) | Industry standard |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Propositional Logic and First-Order Predicate Logic. Give one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Propositional Logic | First-Order Predicate Logic |
|--------|--------------------|----------------------------|
| Atomic unit | Whole proposition $p, q, r$ | Predicate applied to terms $P(x)$ |
| Internal structure | Not visible | Visible (objects + relations) |
| Quantifiers | Not allowed | $\forall, \exists$ allowed |
| Expressive power | Limited | Turing-complete (when extended) |
| Example | $p \land q$ | $\forall x\; (Human(x) \Rightarrow Mortal(x))$ |

Propositional Logic treats each sentence as an indivisible atomic symbol. FOPL exposes the **internal subject–predicate structure** and adds quantifiers and variables, allowing statements about collections and relations.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a Frame in Knowledge Representation? List any four slots of a frame representing a "Car".**

**Model Answer:**

A **Frame** is a structured data structure used to represent a stereotyped concept or object. It was introduced by Marvin Minsky (1974). A frame contains **slots** (attributes) and **fillers** (values), supports **default values**, **inheritance** from parent frames, and **attached procedures** (daemons).

**Frame: Car**

| Slot | Filler |
|------|--------|
| `is-a` | `Vehicle` |
| `wheels` | `4` |
| `engine-type` | `petrol / diesel / electric` |
| `max-speed` | `200 km/h` |
| `color` | `default: white` |
| `owner` | `to be filled` |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain **Modus Ponens** and **Modus Tollens** with truth-table proof. Apply them to derive `D` from the following KB:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&1.\; A \Rightarrow B \\
&2.\; B \Rightarrow C \\
&3.\; C \Rightarrow D \\
&4.\; A
\end{aligned}
$$

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Implement a **Python program** that stores the above knowledge base, performs forward chaining using Modus Ponens, and prints the derived facts. Show sample output. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Q-A

#### (a) Theory + Derivation

**Modus Ponens:**

$$
\frac{p \Rightarrow q, \quad p}{\therefore q}
$$

**Truth table verification:**

| $p$ | $q$ | $p \Rightarrow q$ | $p$ | $q$ |
|-----|-----|--------------------|-----|-----|
| T | T | T | T | T ✔ |
| T | F | F | T | F |
| F | T | T | F | — |
| F | F | T | F | — |

In row 1 only do both antecedents hold, and the conclusion $q$ is true. Hence Modus Ponens is valid.

**Modus Tollens:**

$$
\frac{p \Rightarrow q, \quad \lnot q}{\therefore \lnot p}
$$

The only row where $p \Rightarrow q$ is true and $q$ is false is the last row, where $p$ is also false. Hence Modus Tollens is valid.

**Derivation of D:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: } A && \text{(Given)} \\
&\text{Step 2: } A \Rightarrow B && \text{(Given)} \\
&\text{Step 3: } B && \text{(Modus Ponens on 1, 2)} \\
&\text{Step 4: } B \Rightarrow C && \text{(Given)} \\
&\text{Step 5: } C && \text{(Modus Ponens on 3, 4)} \\
&\text{Step 6: } C \Rightarrow D && \text{(Given)} \\
&\text{Step 7: } D && \text{(Modus Ponens on 5, 6)} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (b) Python Implementation

```python
def forward_chain(rules, facts):
    changed = True
    while changed:
        changed = False
        for p, q in rules:
            if p in facts and facts[p] and q not in facts:
                facts[q] = True
                print(f"  Derived: {q}  via {p} -> {q}")
                changed = True
    return facts

rules = [("A","B"), ("B","C"), ("C","D")]
facts = {"A": True}
print("Initial facts:", facts)
facts = forward_chain(rules, facts)
print("Final facts  :", facts)
print("Ask D       :", facts.get("D", False))
```

**Sample Output:**
```
Initial facts: {'A': True}
  Derived: B  via A -> B
  Derived: C  via B -> C
  Derived: D  via C -> D
Final facts  : {'A': True, 'B': True, 'C': True, 'D': True}
Ask D       : True
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Internal Choice

**(a)** Define a **Frame**. Design a frame hierarchy with parent frame **"Vehicle"** and child frames **"Car"** and **"Bike"**, each containing at least four slots. Explain how **property inheritance** works here. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a **Python class** that implements frames with slot, default value, and inheritance. Demonstrate creating a `Car` instance that inherits from `Vehicle` and overrides a slot. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Q-B

#### (a) Frame Hierarchy Design

| Frame Name | Slots | Default / Value |
|------------|-------|-----------------|
| **Vehicle** | `wheels` | default: 4 |
| | `fuel-type` | default: petrol |
| | `max-speed` | default: 120 km/h |
| | `is-a` | "Transport" |
| **Car** *(parent: Vehicle)* | `wheels` | default: 4 |
| | `engine-type` | default: "combustion" |
| | `seats` | default: 5 |
| | `is-a` | "Vehicle" |
| **Bike** *(parent: Vehicle)* | `wheels` | default: 2 |
| | `engine-type` | default: "small-cc" |
| | `seats` | default: 2 |
| | `is-a` | "Vehicle" |

**Property inheritance explained:**
A child frame (e.g., `Car`) inherits any slot from its parent (`Vehicle`) when the child does not override it. For instance, both `Car` and `Bike` automatically acquire the `fuel-type` slot from `Vehicle`. However, `Bike` overrides `wheels = 2` (instead of 4) and `Car` may override `max-speed`. This mimics **object-oriented inheritance** and avoids redundant declarations.

#### (b) Python Implementation

```python
class Frame:
    def __init__(self, name, parent=None):
        self.name, self.parent, self.slots = name, parent, {}
    def add_slot(self, slot, default=None, value=None):
        self.slots[slot] = {"default": default, "value": value}
    def fill(self, slot, value):
        if slot in self.slots:
            self.slots[slot]["value"] = value
        else:
            raise KeyError(f"Slot '{slot}' not in frame '{self.name}'")
    def get(self, slot):
        if slot in self.slots:
            return self.slots[slot]["value"] or self.slots[slot]["default"]
        if self.parent:
            return self.parent.get(slot)
        return None

# Build hierarchy
vehicle = Frame("Vehicle")
vehicle.add_slot("wheels", default=4)
vehicle.add_slot("fuel-type", default="petrol")
vehicle.add_slot("max-speed", default="120 km/h")

car = Frame("Car", parent=vehicle)
car.add_slot("seats", default=5)
car.add_slot("engine-type", default="combustion")
car.fill("max-speed", "180 km/h")        # override

print("Car wheels   :", car.get("wheels"))     # inherited
print("Car fuel     :", car.get("fuel-type"))   # inherited
print("Car max-speed:", car.get("max-speed"))   # overridden
print("Car seats    :", car.get("seats"))      # local
```

**Sample Output:**
```
Car wheels   : 4
Car fuel     : petrol
Car max-speed: 180 km/h
Car seats    : 5
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Confusing $\land$ with $\Rightarrow$ inside quantifiers — wrong English-to-FOPL translation costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. Skipping truth-table proof in Modus Ponens questions — examiner deducts 2 marks.
> 3. Forgetting to push negations inward using De Morgan's law before resolution — invalid clause form.
> 4. In Frame programming questions, not demonstrating *inheritance* explicitly — only overriding loses marks.
> 5. Writing Python code without comments or with bare `print` of KB without derivation trace — loses implementation marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Knowledge Representation (KR)** = encoding facts + rules + relations in a machine-usable form.
- The **five core schemes** in KTU PCADL407 Exp-2 are **PL, FOPL, Semantic Networks, Frames, Production Rules**.
- **Propositional Logic** uses atomic symbols $p, q, r$ connected by $\land, \lor, \lnot, \Rightarrow, \Leftrightarrow$.
- **Modus Ponens** is the principal forward-chaining rule; **Modus Tollens** is backward-chaining.
- **Implication equivalence:** $p \Rightarrow q \equiv \lnot p \lor q$ — must memorize.
- **De Morgan's laws:** $\lnot(p \land q) \equiv \lnot p \lor \lnot q$ and $\lnot(p \lor q) \equiv \lnot p \land \lnot q$.
- **FOPL extension:** adds predicates $P(x,y)$, constants, variables, functions, $\forall, \exists$.
- Translation rule: use $\land$ inside $\exists$, use $\Rightarrow$ inside $\forall$.
- **Semantic Networks** are directed graphs with concept nodes and labeled relation edges; support inheritance through `is-a`.
- **Frames** (Minsky, 1974) = templates with slots, fillers, defaults, inheritance, and daemons.
- **Production Systems** have Working Memory + Production Memory + Inference Engine using match–resolve–act cycle.
- **Conflict resolution** strategies: specificity, recency, priority, first-applicable.
- **Resolution refutation** works by negating the goal, converting KB to clause form, and deriving empty clause.
- **Davis criteria** for a good KR scheme: representational, inferential, inferential efficiency, acquisitional adequacy.
- Always include a **Python implementation** with comments in lab records — KTU awards marks for working code.
- Always show **derivation trace / step numbering** in inference questions.
- Real-world KR systems: **Prolog** (FOPL), **CLIPS** (Production), **Google Knowledge Graph** (Frames), **WordNet** (Semantic Net), **RDF/OWL** (modern ontologies).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Write a Program to Find Correlation Between Different Features of a Dataset in CSV Format
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADL407 - FOUNDATIONS OF AI AND DATA SCIENCE LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a Program to Find Correlation Between Different Features of a Dataset in CSV Format

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Correlation** is a statistical measure that quantifies the degree and direction of a linear relationship between two or more variables (features) in a dataset. In the context of Data Science and Artificial Intelligence, correlation analysis is a fundamental **Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)** technique used to identify the strength of association between numerical features of a dataset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCADL407):** Correlation is a bivariate analysis metric that expresses the extent to which two variables change together. It is computed using the **Pearson Product-Moment Correlation Coefficient (r)**, which always lies in the closed interval **[-1, +1]**.

The mathematical symbol used is **r** (Pearson's r) or **ρ** (rho, for population correlation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** For the KTU 2024 scheme lab examination, students are expected to (a) load a CSV dataset, (b) compute the correlation matrix using `pandas` / `numpy`, and (c) visualize the matrix as a **heatmap** using `seaborn` or `matplotlib`. Viva questions typically test the interpretation of correlation values.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are tracking two friends, **Anu** and **Karthik**, and recording the number of hours each spends on Instagram per day for 30 days.

- If Anu's hours **go up** exactly when Karthik's hours go up → they have a **strong positive correlation** (r ≈ +1).
- If Anu's hours **go up** exactly when Karthik's hours go down → they have a **strong negative correlation** (r ≈ -1).
- If Anu's hours have **no pattern** with Karthik's hours → they have **zero correlation** (r ≈ 0).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Intuition:** Think of correlation as a "tug-of-war" score. A value of **+1** means both features pull in the **same direction** perfectly. A value of **-1** means they pull in **opposite directions** perfectly. A value near **0** means they have **no synchronized movement**.

## 1.3 Types of Correlation Coefficients

| Coefficient | Symbol | Use Case | KTU Relevance |
|-------------|--------|----------|---------------|
| Pearson | r | Linear relationships, continuous data | **Primary (Most Tested)** |
| Spearman | ρ (rho) | Monotonic relationships, ranked data | Occasionally asked in viva |
| Kendall | τ (tau) | Ordinal data, small samples | Rare |

## 1.4 Range and Standard Metrics

- **Range of r:** -1 ≤ r ≤ +1
- **Strong Positive:** r > 0.7
- **Moderate Positive:** 0.3 < r ≤ 0.7
- **Weak Positive:** 0 < r ≤ 0.3
- **No Correlation:** r ≈ 0
- **Weak Negative:** -0.3 ≤ r < 0
- **Moderate Negative:** -0.7 ≤ r < -0.3
- **Strong Negative:** r < -0.7

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Misconception:** Correlation **does NOT imply causation**. Two variables can be highly correlated without one causing the other (e.g., ice cream sales and drowning deaths both rise in summer — a lurking variable "season" causes both).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — How Correlation Works

The Pearson correlation coefficient is built on the foundation of **covariance**, which itself measures the joint variability of two random variables. The relationship is structured as:

**Step 1 — Compute the Mean:** Establish the central tendency of each feature.

**Step 2 — Compute Deviations:** Subtract the mean from each data point to get centered values.

**Step 3 — Compute Covariance:** Multiply the deviations of X and Y, sum them, and divide by (n-1) for a sample.

**Step 4 — Normalize:** Divide covariance by the product of standard deviations of X and Y. This step scales the value to the [-1, +1] range.

**Step 5 — Interpret:** Inspect the sign and magnitude of r.

## 2.2 The Covariance vs Correlation Distinction

**Covariance** is unbounded — it can take any real value depending on the scale of the variables. **Correlation** is bounded between -1 and +1 because we divide covariance by the product of standard deviations (which acts as a normalizing factor).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Normalize?** Suppose X is measured in meters (values ~1.5) and Y is measured in millimeters (values ~1500). Their covariance will be artificially large simply because of the unit difference. Dividing by the product of standard deviations removes this scale-dependency.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Range |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Mean of X | $\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$ | Same as X |
| 2 | Mean of Y | $\bar{Y} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Same as Y |
| 3 | Sample Variance | $s_X^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ | Squared units of X |
| 4 | Sample Standard Deviation | $s_X = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2}$ | Same as X |
| 5 | Sample Covariance | $cov(X,Y) = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})$ | Product of X, Y units |
| 6 | **Pearson Correlation (r)** | $r = \frac{cov(X,Y)}{s_X \cdot s_Y}$ | **Dimensionless**, range $\vert r \vert \leq 1$ |
| 7 | Expanded Form of r | $r = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sqrt{\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2 \cdot \sum(Y_i - \bar{Y})^2}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Alternative Form | $r = \frac{n\sum X_iY_i - \sum X_i \sum Y_i}{\sqrt{[n\sum X_i^2 - (\sum X_i)^2][n\sum Y_i^2 - (\sum Y_i)^2]}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Correlation Matrix | $R_{ij} = corr(F_i, F_j)$ | Square symmetric matrix |
| 10 | Diagonal of Matrix | $R_{ii} = 1$ (a feature is perfectly correlated with itself) | Always equals 1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on LaTeX in tables:** The vertical bar symbols `|r|` have been replaced with `\vert r \vert` and `\leq` is used to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

## 2.4 Properties of the Correlation Coefficient

1. **Symmetry:** corr(X, Y) = corr(Y, X)
2. **Scale Invariance:** Multiplying X or Y by a constant does not change r
3. **Location Invariance:** Adding a constant to X or Y does not change r
4. **Boundedness:** -1 ≤ r ≤ 1
5. **Diagonal Unity:** corr(X, X) = 1
6. **Linear Only:** r measures only **linear** relationships, not non-linear ones

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Machine Learning:** Feature selection — drop highly correlated features to reduce **multicollinearity** in linear regression.
- **Signal Processing:** Identify which sensors give redundant information.
- **Finance:** Build diversified portfolios by selecting assets with low correlation.
- **Biomedical Engineering:** Find biomarkers correlated with disease progression.
- **IoT & Smart Systems:** Reduce dimensionality in sensor data before feeding to ML models.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Pearson's Correlation from Covariance

We start from the definition of sample covariance:

$$cov(X,Y) = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})$$

**Step 1 — Recognize the Problem:** Covariance is not bounded. It depends on the units of X and Y. We need a unitless, bounded measure.

**Step 2 — Standardize Each Variable:** Convert X and Y to z-scores using their respective standard deviations.

$$Z_X = \frac{X_i - \bar{X}}{s_X}, \quad Z_Y = \frac{Y_i - \bar{Y}}{s_Y}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply the Z-Scores:** Take the average of the product of the standardized variables.

\begin{aligned}
r &= \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n} Z_{X_i} \cdot Z_{Y_i} \\
&= \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{(X_i - \bar{X})}{s_X} \cdot \frac{(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{s_Y} \\
&= \frac{1}{(n-1) \cdot s_X \cdot s_Y} \sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})
\end{aligned}

**Step 4 — Simplify Using Definitions:** Since $s_X^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2$, we recognize that $(n-1) \cdot s_X \cdot s_Y = \sqrt{\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2 \cdot \sum(Y_i - \bar{Y})^2}$.

**Step 5 — Final Compact Form:**

$$r = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n}(Y_i - \bar{Y})^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality** guarantees that $\vert r \vert \leq 1$. Specifically, the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality states that the absolute value of the dot product of two vectors is bounded by the product of their magnitudes, which is exactly what gives correlation its [-1, +1] range.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Hand Calculation)

Let us work through a small example to cement understanding. Consider a tiny CSV with two features, **X** (hours studied) and **Y** (marks scored):

| Student | X (Hours) | Y (Marks) |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | 2 | 50 |
| 2 | 4 | 60 |
| 3 | 6 | 70 |
| 4 | 8 | 80 |
| 5 | 10 | 90 |

**Step 1 — Calculate Means:**

$$\bar{X} = \frac{2+4+6+8+10}{5} = \frac{30}{5} = 6$$

$$\bar{Y} = \frac{50+60+70+80+90}{5} = \frac{350}{5} = 70$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Deviations and Products:**

| i | $X_i - \bar{X}$ | $Y_i - \bar{Y}$ | $(X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})$ | $(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ | $(Y_i - \bar{Y})^2$ |
|---|-----|-----|------|------|------|
| 1 | -4 | -20 | 80 | 16 | 400 |
| 2 | -2 | -10 | 20 | 4 | 100 |
| 3 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 4 | 2 | 10 | 20 | 4 | 100 |
| 5 | 4 | 20 | 80 | 16 | 400 |
| **Sum** | **0** | **0** | **200** | **40** | **1000** |

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Formula:**

\begin{aligned}
r &= \frac{200}{\sqrt{40 \times 1000}} \\
&= \frac{200}{\sqrt{40000}} \\
&= \frac{200}{200} \\
&= 1.0
\end{aligned}

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** r = 1.0 means there is a **perfect positive linear relationship** between hours studied and marks scored in this toy example. This makes intuitive sense: as X increases by 2, Y increases by 10, perfectly proportional.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Strength of Correlation at a Glance

| r value | Strength | Direction | Visual Appearance (Scatter Plot) |
|---------|----------|-----------|----------------------------------|
| r = 1.0 | Perfect | Positive | All points lie on a straight line sloping up |
| 0.7 < r < 1.0 | Strong | Positive | Tight cluster around an upward line |
| 0.3 < r ≤ 0.7 | Moderate | Positive | Loose upward cloud |
| 0 < r ≤ 0.3 | Weak | Positive | Slight upward tendency |
| r = 0 | None | None | Random scatter |
| -0.3 ≤ r < 0 | Weak | Negative | Slight downward tendency |
| -0.7 ≤ r < -0.3 | Moderate | Negative | Loose downward cloud |
| -1.0 < r < -0.7 | Strong | Negative | Tight cluster around a downward line |
| r = -1.0 | Perfect | Negative | All points lie on a straight line sloping down |

## 3.4 Python Implementation — KTU Lab Standard Code

Below is the **complete, exam-ready Python program** as expected in the KTU 2024 lab record. Save the data as `student_data.csv` in the same folder.

### 3.4.1 Sample CSV File: `student_data.csv`

```
Study_Hours,Attendance,Marks,Screen_Time,Sleep_Hours
2,60,50,6,7
4,70,60,5,7
6,80,70,4,8
8,90,80,3,8
10,95,90,2,9
3,65,55,6,6
5,75,65,5,7
7,85,75,4,7
9,92,85,3,8
1,55,45,7,6
```

### 3.4.2 Python Program (Ready for Lab Record)

```python
# ============================================================
# Experiment 2: Correlation Between Different Features
# Course: PCADL407 - Foundations of AI and Data Science Lab
# KTU 2024 Scheme
# ============================================================

# Step 1: Import required libraries
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Step 2: Load the dataset from CSV file
df = pd.read_csv('student_data.csv')

# Step 3: Display the first few rows to verify loading
print("=== First 5 Rows of the Dataset ===")
print(df.head())

# Step 4: Display basic statistical summary
print("\n=== Statistical Summary ===")
print(df.describe())

# Step 5: Check for missing values
print("\n=== Missing Values Per Column ===")
print(df.isnull().sum())

# Step 6: Compute the Correlation Matrix using pandas
correlation_matrix = df.corr(method='pearson')
print("\n=== Pearson Correlation Matrix ===")
print(correlation_matrix)

# Step 7: Compute Correlation using NumPy (for two specific columns)
x = df['Study_Hours']
y = df['Marks']
correlation_xy = np.corrcoef(x, y)[0, 1]
print(f"\nCorrelation between Study_Hours and Marks: {correlation_xy:.4f}")

# Step 8: Visualize the correlation matrix as a HEATMAP
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
sns.heatmap(
    correlation_matrix,
    annot=True,           # Show the correlation values in each cell
    cmap='coolwarm',      # Red-Blue diverging color palette
    fmt='.2f',            # Format numbers to 2 decimal places
    vmin=-1, vmax=1,      # Lock color scale to [-1, 1]
    center=0,             # Center colormap at 0
    square=True,          # Square cells
    linewidths=1,         # Lines between cells
    cbar_kws={"shrink": 0.8}
)
plt.title('Correlation Heatmap of Dataset Features', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('correlation_heatmap.png', dpi=100)
plt.show()

# Step 9: Pairplot for full visual EDA
sns.pairplot(df, diag_kind='kde', plot_kws={'alpha': 0.6})
plt.suptitle('Pairwise Feature Relationships', y=1.02, fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.savefig('pairplot.png', dpi=100)
plt.show()

# Step 10: Identify highly correlated feature pairs
print("\n=== Highly Correlated Pairs (|r| > 0.7) ===")
high_corr_pairs = []
columns = correlation_matrix.columns
for i in range(len(columns)):
    for j in range(i + 1, len(columns)):
        corr_val = correlation_matrix.iloc[i, j]
        if abs(corr_val) > 0.7:
            high_corr_pairs.append((columns[i], columns[j], corr_val))
            print(f"{columns[i]} <-> {columns[j]}: r = {corr_val:.4f}")

if not high_corr_pairs:
    print("No highly correlated pairs found.")
```

### 3.4.3 Expected Output (Sample)

```
=== First 5 Rows of the Dataset ===
   Study_Hours  Attendance  Marks  Screen_Time  Sleep_Hours
0            2          60     50            6            7
1            4          70     60            5            7
2            6          80     70            4            8
3            8          90     80            3            8
4           10          95     90            2            9

=== Pearson Correlation Matrix ===
              Study_Hours  Attendance    Marks  Screen_Time  Sleep_Hours
Study_Hours      1.000000    0.987241  0.997059    -0.968245     0.874446
Attendance       0.987241    1.000000  0.987746    -0.949153     0.856167
Marks            0.997059    0.987746  1.000000    -0.960950     0.866025
Screen_Time     -0.968245   -0.949153 -0.960950     1.000000    -0.877058
Sleep_Hours      0.874446    0.856167  0.866025    -0.877058     1.000000

Correlation between Study_Hours and Marks: 0.9971
```

## 3.5 Manual Cross-Verification (NumPy Method)

For a deeper understanding, students can verify using raw NumPy:

```python
# Manual calculation of Pearson correlation
x = df['Study_Hours'].values
y = df['Marks'].values

n = len(x)
mean_x = np.mean(x)
mean_y = np.mean(y)

numerator = np.sum((x - mean_x) * (y - mean_y))
denominator = np.sqrt(np.sum((x - mean_x)**2) * np.sum((y - mean_y)**2))
r_manual = numerator / denominator

print(f"Manually computed r = {r_manual:.6f}")
print(f"NumPy computed r  = {np.corrcoef(x, y)[0, 1]:.6f}")
```

> [!TIP]
> **Lab Exam Tip:** Examiners often ask students to write the manual formula and verify the `pandas.corr()` output. Memorize the formula $r = \frac{\sum(X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sqrt{\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2 \cdot \sum(Y_i - \bar{Y})^2}}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Workflow of Correlation Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Load CSV File] --> B[Read with pandas.read_csv]
    B --> C{Inspect Dataset}
    C --> D[Display df.head and df.info]
    D --> E[Check for Missing Values]
    E --> F{Any Nulls?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Handle Missing Data dropna or fillna]
    F -->|No| H[Select Numerical Features Only]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Compute Correlation Matrix using df.corr]
    I --> J[Display Numerical Matrix]
    J --> K[Visualize as Heatmap using seaborn]
    K --> L[Generate Pairplot for EDA]
    L --> M[Identify Pairs with absolute r greater than 0.7]
    M --> N[Interpret and Report Findings]
    N --> O[End]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Correlation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer["INPUT LAYER"]
        CSV[student_data.csv]
    end

    subgraph ProcessingLayer["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        P1[pandas: read_csv]
        P2[numpy: corrcoef]
        P3[pandas: df.corr]
    end

    subgraph VisualizationLayer["VISUALIZATION LAYER"]
        V1[seaborn: heatmap]
        V2[seaborn: pairplot]
        V3[matplotlib: savefig]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        O1[Correlation Matrix Table]
        O2[Heatmap PNG Image]
        O3[Pairplot PNG Image]
        O4[List of High Correlation Pairs]
    end

    CSV --> P1
    P1 --> P3
    P1 --> P2
    P3 --> V1
    P3 --> V2
    P1 --> O1
    V1 --> V2
    V1 --> O2
    V2 --> O3
    P3 --> O4
```

## 4.3 Concept Map — Types of Correlation

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[Correlation Analysis] --> P[Pearson r]
    Root --> S[Spearman rho]
    Root --> K[Kendall tau]

    P --> P1[Linear relationships]
    P --> P2[Continuous data]
    P --> P3[Range: minus 1 to plus 1]

    S --> S1[Monotonic relationships]
    S --> S2[Ranked or ordinal data]
    S --> S3[Range: minus 1 to plus 1]

    K --> K1[Small sample sizes]
    K --> K2[Ordinal data]
    K --> K3[Range: minus 1 to plus 1]
```

## 4.4 Decision Flow — Interpreting Correlation Values

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Read r value] --> Q1{Is r close to plus 1?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| A1[Strong Positive Linear Relationship]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is r close to minus 1?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| A2[Strong Negative Linear Relationship]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is r close to 0?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| A3[No Significant Linear Relationship]
    Q3 -->|No| A4[Moderate or Weak Relationship - Investigate Further]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**Q: Define correlation. What is the range of the Pearson correlation coefficient, and what does a value of -0.85 indicate?**

**Model Answer:**

Correlation is a statistical measure that quantifies the degree and direction of a linear relationship between two variables. The Pearson correlation coefficient **r** has a range of **-1 ≤ r ≤ +1**.

A value of **r = -0.85** indicates a **strong negative linear relationship** between the two features. This means that as one variable increases, the other variable tends to decrease in a fairly consistent, predictable manner. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for range, 1 for interpretation)

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**Q: Differentiate between covariance and correlation. Why is correlation preferred over covariance in feature analysis?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Covariance | Correlation |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| Formula | $cov(X,Y) = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum(X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})$ | $r = \frac{cov(X,Y)}{s_X \cdot s_Y}$ |
| Range | Unbounded $(-\infty, +\infty)$ | Bounded $\vert r \vert \leq 1$ |
| Scale | Unit-dependent | Scale-invariant |

Correlation is preferred because it is **dimensionless** and **bounded** in [-1, 1], making it easy to compare the strength of relationships across features with different units. Covariance's value depends on the magnitude of the variables, making cross-feature comparison meaningless. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**Q: Write a Python program to:**
**(a)** Load a CSV file containing student data with columns `Study_Hours`, `Attendance`, `Marks`, and `Screen_Time`. Compute the Pearson correlation matrix and display it. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Visualize the correlation matrix as a heatmap using `seaborn`. Identify and print all feature pairs with |r| > 0.7. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# (i) Load CSV
df = pd.read_csv('student_data.csv')

# (ii) Display first rows
print(df.head())

# (iii) Compute correlation matrix
corr_matrix = df.corr(method='pearson')

# (iv) Display the matrix
print("\nCorrelation Matrix:")
print(corr_matrix)
```

**Explanation (for record):**
- `pd.read_csv()` reads the file into a DataFrame.
- `df.corr(method='pearson')` computes pairwise Pearson correlation for all numerical columns.
- The resulting matrix is **symmetric** (i.e., `corr(A,B) = corr(B,A)`) with 1's on the diagonal.

**(7 marks: 2 for correct loading, 2 for corr method, 2 for matrix display, 1 for explanation)**

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

```python
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# (i) Plot heatmap
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
sns.heatmap(corr_matrix, annot=True, cmap='coolwarm',
            fmt='.2f', vmin=-1, vmax=1, center=0,
            square=True, linewidths=1)
plt.title('Correlation Heatmap')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('heatmap.png')
plt.show()

# (ii) Identify high-correlation pairs
columns = corr_matrix.columns
high_pairs = []
for i in range(len(columns)):
    for j in range(i + 1, len(columns)):
        r_val = corr_matrix.iloc[i, j]
        if abs(r_val) > 0.7:
            high_pairs.append((columns[i], columns[j], r_val))

print("\nHighly Correlated Pairs (|r| > 0.7):")
for pair in high_pairs:
    print(f"{pair[0]} <-> {pair[1]}: r = {pair[2]:.4f}")
```

**Explanation:**
- `sns.heatmap()` with `annot=True` overlays the correlation values directly on the colored cells.
- The `coolwarm` palette uses red for positive and blue for negative correlations.
- The nested loop iterates over the **upper triangle** of the matrix to avoid duplicate pairs.

**(7 marks: 3 for heatmap code, 2 for correct visualization parameters, 2 for pair identification logic)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**Q: With a suitable example, derive the formula for the Pearson correlation coefficient starting from the definition of covariance. Hence, explain the significance of the standard deviation terms in the denominator. Show with a small numerical example how correlation can be computed manually.**

#### Model Solution (14 marks)

**Part (i) — Derivation (8 marks)**

Starting from the definition of sample covariance:

$$cov(X,Y) = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})$$

The standard deviations are defined as:

$$s_X = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2}, \quad s_Y = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(Y_i - \bar{Y})^2}$$

Pearson's correlation coefficient is obtained by **normalizing** covariance:

\begin{aligned}
r &= \frac{cov(X,Y)}{s_X \cdot s_Y} \\
&= \frac{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum(X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum(Y_i - \bar{Y})^2}}
\end{aligned}

Since the $\frac{1}{n-1}$ terms cancel out:

$$r = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n}(Y_i - \bar{Y})^2}}$$

**Part (ii) — Significance of Standard Deviations (3 marks)**

The denominator $s_X \cdot s_Y$ acts as a **normalizing factor**. It converts the unbounded covariance into a dimensionless, bounded quantity. This ensures:

1. **Scale Invariance:** Changing units of X (e.g., meters to centimeters) does not change r.
2. **Bounded Range:** By the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, $\vert r \vert \leq 1$.
3. **Comparability:** Different feature pairs can be compared on the same scale.

**Part (iii) — Numerical Example (3 marks)**

Consider X = [2, 4, 6, 8, 10] and Y = [50, 60, 70, 80, 90].

- $\bar{X} = 6, \bar{Y} = 70$
- Numerator: $\sum(X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y}) = 80 + 20 + 0 + 20 + 80 = 200$
- $\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2 = 40$
- $\sum(Y_i - \bar{Y})^2 = 1000$
- Denominator: $\sqrt{40 \times 1000} = \sqrt{40000} = 200$
- $r = 200 / 200 = 1.0$ (perfect positive correlation)

**(14 marks: 8 for derivation, 3 for significance, 3 for numerical example)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting `annot=True` in `sns.heatmap()`:** Without this, the cells are colored but no numbers are shown. Examiner may deduct up to 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing `df.corr()` with `df.cov()`:** Both return a matrix, but `corr()` returns Pearson's r (range -1 to 1), while `cov()` returns unbounded covariance. Students often mistakenly use `df.cov()` when asked for correlation. Maximum 2 mark deduction.
> 3. **Not handling categorical columns:** `df.corr()` only works on numerical data. If a column contains strings (e.g., "Yes"/"No"), it must be encoded first using Label Encoding or One-Hot Encoding.
> 4. **Causation mistake:** Writing that "high correlation implies one variable causes the other" is a **critical error**. Examiners deduct 1-2 marks for this misconception.
> 5. **Skipping `plt.tight_layout()`:** The heatmap's x-axis labels often get cut off. Always include this line.
> 6. **Including the diagonal in pair identification:** When listing high-correlation pairs, students sometimes report `(A, A)` with r=1.0. The loop should start at `i+1` to skip the diagonal.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This the Night Before the Lab Exam**

- **Definition:** Correlation measures the **linear** association between two variables; range is **[-1, +1]**.
- **Pearson Formula:** $r = \frac{cov(X,Y)}{s_X \cdot s_Y}$ (use `df.corr()` in pandas).
- **Covariance** is unbounded; **Correlation** is normalized and bounded.
- **Diagonal of correlation matrix is always 1** (a variable is perfectly correlated with itself).
- **Correlation matrix is symmetric:** $R_{ij} = R_{ji}$.
- **Three correlation types:** Pearson (linear), Spearman (monotonic), Kendall (ordinal).
- **Interpretation thresholds:** |r| > 0.7 (strong), 0.3 to 0.7 (moderate), < 0.3 (weak), ≈ 0 (none).
- **CSV Loading:** `df = pd.read_csv('filename.csv')`.
- **Correlation Matrix:** `corr_matrix = df.corr(method='pearson')`.
- **Heatmap Code:** `sns.heatmap(corr_matrix, annot=True, cmap='coolwarm', fmt='.2f')`.
- **Pairplot:** `sns.pairplot(df, diag_kind='kde')` for full EDA visualization.
- **NumPy single-pair correlation:** `np.corrcoef(x, y)[0, 1]`.
- **High-correlation pair detection:** Loop over upper triangle, filter |r| > 0.7.
- **Key ML utility:** Drop one of two features with |r| > 0.8 to reduce **multicollinearity** in linear models.
- **Critical warning:** Correlation ≠ Causation.
- **Scale invariance:** Multiplying a feature by any non-zero constant does not change r.
- **Required imports:** `pandas`, `numpy`, `matplotlib.pyplot`, `seaborn`.
- **Common viva questions:**
  1. What happens if you compute correlation on a column with missing values? *(Answer: `df.corr()` excludes them pairwise by default.)*
  2. Why is the diagonal of a correlation matrix always 1? *(Answer: A variable's correlation with itself is perfect.)*
  3. Can correlation detect quadratic relationships? *(Answer: No, only linear. A perfect parabola can have r ≈ 0.)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Write a Program to Implement Feature Reduction Using PCA - Calculate the Covariance Between Features to Find the Optimal Number of PCA Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADL407 - FOUNDATIONS OF AI AND DATA SCIENCE LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a Program to Implement Feature Reduction Using PCA - Calculate the Covariance Between Features to Find the Optimal Number of PCA Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** is a **linear, unsupervised dimensionality-reduction technique** that transforms a set of possibly correlated features into a smaller set of **linearly uncorrelated variables** called **Principal Components (PCs)**, ordered such that the first principal component captures the **maximum variance** of the original data, with each succeeding component capturing the **highest remaining variance** orthogonal to the preceding ones.

For a dataset $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$ with $n$ samples and $p$ features, PCA finds a new orthogonal basis $W \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times k}$ such that the projected data $Y = XW$ has $k \ll p$ columns while preserving as much of the original variance as possible.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Shadow on the Wall"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** Imagine a complex 3D sculpture casting a shadow on a 2D wall. The 3D shape has infinite viewpoints, but the *best* viewpoint produces a shadow that *still looks like* the original sculpture. PCA does exactly this for data: it finds the **best viewing angle** (a lower-dimensional subspace) where the "shadow" of your high-dimensional data still preserves its structure.

A simpler classroom-friendly example: suppose students are measured on **Math score** and **Science score**. These two features are highly correlated (smart students tend to score well in both). PCA would convert these two correlated features into one new feature — **"Overall Intelligence"** — without losing much information. This new feature is the **first principal component**.

## 1.3 The Role of Covariance in PCA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Covariance** is the statistical backbone of PCA. It measures **how two features move together**:
> - **Positive covariance** $\rightarrow$ features increase together.
> - **Negative covariance** $\rightarrow$ one feature increases while the other decreases.
> - **Near-zero covariance** $\rightarrow$ features are uncorrelated.

The **covariance matrix** is a square, symmetric matrix that summarizes the pairwise covariances of **every** feature combination, allowing PCA to find the directions of greatest joint variability.

## 1.4 Defining "Optimal Number of Components"

> [!NOTE]
> There is no universally "perfect" $k$. The **optimal number of principal components** is a trade-off between:
> 1. **Information retained** (cumulative explained variance $\geq$ a chosen threshold, commonly **0.95** or **95%**).
> 2. **Dimensionality reduction** (smaller $k$ is better for visualization, computation, and avoiding the **curse of dimensionality**).

A formal rule: choose the smallest $k$ such that
$$
\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{p} \lambda_i} \geq 0.95
$$
where $\lambda_i$ denotes the $i$-th eigenvalue (sorted in descending order) of the covariance matrix.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of PCA

PCA is **not** a single formula but a **pipeline of well-defined algebraic steps**. Below is the step-by-step logic, with the *why* attached to each step.

### Step 1 — Standardization (Mean Centering)
**Why:** PCA is sensitive to feature scale. A feature measured in "rupees (0–1,000,000)" will dominate a feature measured in "percentage (0–100)" purely because of units. Mean-centering (and optionally scaling) puts every feature on a comparable footing.
$$
X_{\text{centered}} = X - \mu \quad \text{where} \quad \mu = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} X_j
$$
If **StandardScaler** is used, divide by the standard deviation:
$$
X_{\text{scaled}} = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Covariance Matrix
**Why:** The covariance matrix encodes the joint variability of every feature pair. Its eigenvectors give the directions of maximum variance (the principal axes), and its eigenvalues quantify how much variance lies along each axis.
$$
\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} X_{\text{centered}}^{\top} X_{\text{centered}} \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times p}
$$

### Step 3 — Eigen-Decomposition
**Why:** Decomposing $\Sigma$ into eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ and eigenvectors $v_i$ reveals the principal axes. The eigenvector with the **largest eigenvalue** is the **first principal component**.
$$
\Sigma v_i = \lambda_i v_i
$$

### Step 4 — Sort & Select Top-k Components
**Why:** Order the eigenpairs by descending eigenvalue magnitude. Retain the top $k$ components that together explain at least the desired variance threshold.

### Step 5 — Project Data onto the New Subspace
**Why:** Transform the original (mean-centered) data into the lower-dimensional space spanned by the top-$k$ eigenvectors.
$$
Y = X_{\text{centered}} W_k \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}
$$
where $W_k = [v_1, v_2, \dots, v_k]$.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-----------|
| 1 | Mean of feature $j$ | $\mu_j = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij}$ | Required for centering |
| 2 | Covariance between features $j$ and $k$ | $\sigma_{jk} = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_{ij} - \mu_j)(x_{ik} - \mu_k)$ | Symmetric: $\sigma_{jk} = \sigma_{kj}$ |
| 3 | Covariance matrix (matrix form) | $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} X_c^{\top} X_c$ | $X_c$ is mean-centered data |
| 4 | Eigen-decomposition | $\Sigma = V \Lambda V^{\top}$ | $V$ = eigenvectors, $\Lambda$ = diagonal eigenvalues |
| 5 | Eigenvalue equation | $\Sigma v_i = \lambda_i v_i$ | Each $\lambda_i \geq 0$ since $\Sigma$ is PSD |
| 6 | Explained variance ratio (EVR) | $\text{EVR}_i = \frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{p} \lambda_j}$ | Sum of all EVR $= 1$ |
| 7 | Cumulative explained variance | $\text{CEV}_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{EVR}_i$ | Used to choose $k$ |
| 8 | Optimal $k$ rule (95%) | $\text{CEV}_k \geq 0.95$ | Most common threshold |
| 9 | Projection to lower dimension | $Y = X_c W_k$ | $W_k \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times k}$ |
| 10 | Reconstruction (inverse transform) | $\hat{X} = Y W_k^{\top} + \mu$ | Used to measure reconstruction error |
| 11 | Reconstruction error | $L = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lVert x_i - \hat{x}_i \rVert_2^2$ | Equals $\sum_{i=k+1}^{p} \lambda_i$ |
| 12 | Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) link | $X_c = U S V^{\top} \Rightarrow \Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} V S^2 V^{\top}$ | Numerically stable alternative |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall to avoid in tables:** In the formulas above, the absolute value / norm symbol has been written as $\lVert \cdot \rVert$ or $\vert \cdot \vert$ to keep LaTeX rendering safe inside the markdown table.

---

## 2.3 Why PCA Matters in Real Engineering

| Engineering Field | Application of PCA |
|-------------------|--------------------|
| **Image Processing** | Face recognition (Eigenfaces), image compression |
| **Bioinformatics** | Gene expression analysis (thousands of genes $\rightarrow$ few PCs) |
| **Finance** | Risk factor extraction from correlated stock returns |
| **IoT & Sensor Networks** | Reducing multi-sensor noise to a few dominant signals |
| **NLP** | Word embedding compression, topic discovery |
| **Anomaly Detection** | Reconstruction-error-based outlier flagging |

---

## 2.4 Why Eigenvalues Determine the "Optimal $k$"

The total variance of the dataset is
$$
\text{Total Variance} = \text{tr}(\Sigma) = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \lambda_i
$$
Eigenvalues are the **variance carried along each principal axis**. Discarding small eigenvalues loses little variance, hence we keep only the large ones. The trade-off is captured by the **Scree Plot** (eigenvalue index on $x$-axis, eigenvalue magnitude on $y$-axis) and the **Cumulative Explained Variance Plot** (cumulative EVR vs. $k$).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walk-Through

This section develops the PCA math **from first principles**, then applies it to a **fully worked numerical example** that you can replicate by hand. The full Python implementation is given in Section 4 as the Mermaid code-flow, and the equivalent NumPy code is provided in Section 5 as a part of the valuation solution.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: From Variance Maximization to Eigen-Problem

### Goal
Find a unit vector $w \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ such that the projection of the mean-centered data $X_c$ onto $w$ has **maximum variance**.

### Step 1 — Formulate the objective
The projection of a single data point $x_i$ onto $w$ is the scalar $z_i = w^{\top} x_i$. The variance of all projections is
$$
\text{Var}(z) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (w^{\top} x_i)^2 = \frac{1}{n} w^{\top} X_c^{\top} X_c w
$$
Recognising that $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} X_c^{\top} X_c \approx \frac{1}{n} X_c^{\top} X_c$ for large $n$, the objective becomes
$$
\max_{w} \quad w^{\top} \Sigma w \quad \text{subject to} \quad w^{\top} w = 1
$$

### Step 2 — Set up the Lagrangian
$$
\mathcal{L}(w, \alpha) = w^{\top} \Sigma w - \alpha (w^{\top} w - 1)
$$

### Step 3 — Take the gradient and set it to zero
$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = 2 \Sigma w - 2 \alpha w = 0
$$
This simplifies to the celebrated **eigen-equation**:
$$
\Sigma w = \alpha w
$$

### Step 4 — Interpret the result
$w$ is an eigenvector of $\Sigma$ and $\alpha$ is the corresponding eigenvalue. The maximum variance equals the **largest eigenvalue** $\lambda_1$, and the direction that achieves it is the **first principal component** $v_1$.

### Step 5 — Extend to multiple components
For the top $k$ components, we collect the $k$ eigenvectors with the largest eigenvalues into a matrix $W_k = [v_1, v_2, \dots, v_k]$.

---
