## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (Hand-Calculable)

Consider a 2D dataset with $n = 4$ samples and 2 features:
$$
X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \\ 5 & 6 \\ 7 & 8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step A — Compute column means
$$
\mu = \left[ \frac{1+3+5+7}{4}, \frac{2+4+6+8}{4} \right] = [4, 5]
$$

### Step B — Mean-center
$$
X_c = X - \mu = \begin{bmatrix} -3 & -3 \\ -1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 3 & 3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step C — Covariance matrix
$$
\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} X_c^{\top} X_c = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 9+1+1+9 & 9+1+1+9 \\ 9+1+1+9 & 9+1+1+9 \end{bmatrix}
$$
$$
X_c^{\top} X_c = \begin{bmatrix} 20 & 20 \\ 20 & 20 \end{bmatrix} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 6.667 & 6.667 \\ 6.667 & 6.667 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step D — Eigenvalues
Since the two rows are identical, $\Sigma$ has rank 1. The characteristic polynomial is
$$
\det(\Sigma - \lambda I) = (6.667 - \lambda)^2 - 6.667^2 = 0
$$
$$
\lambda^2 - 13.333 \lambda = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda(\lambda - 13.333) = 0
$$
Therefore $\lambda_1 = 13.333$ and $\lambda_2 = 0$.

### Step E — Eigenvectors
For $\lambda_1 = 13.333$:
$$
(\Sigma - 13.333 I) v_1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$
For $\lambda_2 = 0$: any vector orthogonal to $v_1$, e.g. $v_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} [1, -1]^{\top}$.

### Step F — Explained variance
$$
\text{EVR}_1 = \frac{13.333}{13.333 + 0} = 1.0, \quad \text{EVR}_2 = 0
$$
**Interpretation:** A single principal component captures **100%** of the variance. This is expected because the two features are perfectly linearly correlated.

### Step G — Project to 1D
$$
Y = X_c v_1 = \begin{bmatrix} -3 & -3 \\ -1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 3 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 \\ 0.707 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -4.243 \\ -1.414 \\ 1.414 \\ 4.243 \end{bmatrix}
$$
The 2D data has been **losslessly compressed** to 1D.

---

## 3.3 Mathematical Justification for the "Optimal $k$" Rule

The total variance retained by keeping the first $k$ components is
$$
\text{Retained Variance} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i
$$
The variance lost is
$$
\text{Lost Variance} = \sum_{i=k+1}^{p} \lambda_i
$$
Choosing $k$ is equivalent to setting a tolerance $\tau$ on the lost variance:
$$
\sum_{i=k+1}^{p} \lambda_i \leq \tau \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{p} \lambda_i
$$
The 95% rule corresponds to $\tau = 0.05$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked to "find the optimal $k$", always show (a) the sorted eigenvalues, (b) the EVR for each, (c) the cumulative EVR, and (d) explicitly state the threshold rule you are using. Partial marks are awarded for each step.

---

## 3.4 The Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) Connection

For numerical stability, libraries (including Scikit-Learn) often use SVD instead of direct eigen-decomposition. Given $X_c$, the SVD is
$$
X_c = U S V^{\top}
$$
where $U \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$, $S$ is a diagonal matrix of singular values $\sigma_i$, and $V \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times p}$ contains the right-singular vectors. The relationship to PCA is
$$
\lambda_i = \frac{\sigma_i^2}{n-1}, \quad v_i = V_{:,i}
$$
This is **mathematically equivalent** to eigen-decomposition of $\Sigma$ but **numerically more stable** for high-dimensional or rank-deficient data.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PCA Pipeline — High-Level Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Dataset X n x p] --> B[Step 1: Standardize using StandardScaler mean 0 std 1]
    B --> C[Step 2: Compute Covariance Matrix Sigma p x p]
    C --> D[Step 3: Eigen Decomposition or SVD]
    D --> E[Step 4: Sort Eigenvalues Descending lambda 1 to lambda p]
    E --> F[Step 5: Compute Explained Variance Ratio EVR]
    F --> G{Step 6: Choose Optimal k via 95 percent CEV threshold or Scree elbow}
    G -->|k selected| H[Step 7: Form Projection Matrix Wk of top k eigenvectors]
    H --> I[Step 8: Project Data Y equals Xc times Wk]
    I --> J[Reduced Dataset Y n x k]
    F --> K[Plot Scree and Cumulative Explained Variance]
    K --> G
```

---

## 4.2 Detailed Algorithmic Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start]) --> load[Load CSV using pandas read csv]
    load --> sep[Separate Features X and Target y if any]
    sep --> scale[Apply StandardScaler fit transform]
    scale --> cov[Compute Covariance Matrix using numpy cov]
    cov --> decomp{Choose Decomposition}
    decomp -->|Method A| eig[eig of covariance matrix using numpy linalg eig]
    decomp -->|Method B| svd[svd of Xc using numpy linalg svd]
    eig --> sort[Sort eigenvalues descending with argsort]
    svd --> svdSort[Compute lambda as sigma squared over n minus 1]
    svdSort --> sort
    sort --> evr[Compute EVR equals lambda i over sum lambda]
    evr --> cev[Compute Cumulative EVR using numpy cumsum]
    cev --> plot[Plot Scree Plot and Cumulative Variance Plot using matplotlib]
    plot --> selectk[Select k where CEV greater or equal 0.95]
    selectk --> wkmat[Build Wk by taking top k eigenvectors]
    wkmat --> proj[Project Y equals Xc dot Wk]
    proj --> out([Output Y reduced dataset and Optimal k])
```

---

## 4.3 Data-Transformation Schematic (Mermaid Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ORIG[Original Feature Space p dimensions]
        A1[Feature 1] --> MIX[Correlated High Dim Data n x p]
        A2[Feature 2] --> MIX
        A3[Feature 3] --> MIX
        AP[Feature p] --> MIX
    end
    MIX --> PCA[PCA Engine Covariance Eigen Decomposition Sort Project]
    PCA --> subgraph RED[Reduced PC Space k dimensions]
        B1[PC 1 highest variance] --> OUT[Y n x k]
        B2[PC 2 second highest] --> OUT
        B3[PC k threshold based] --> OUT
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams in the exam:** The Mermaid blocks above mirror the **exact sequence of operations** the examiner expects you to describe verbally. Memorize the eight steps — they will appear in nearly every ESE question on PCA.

---

## 4.4 Scree-Plot Interpretation Sketch (Mermaid Coordinate Chart)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    XAxis[Component Index 1 to p]:::axis --> Point
    YAxis[Eigenvalue Magnitude]:::axis --> Point
    Point["Steep Drop 1 to 2 -> Keep 1"]
    Point2["Gradual Tail 3 to p -> Discard"]
    Point --> ScreePlot[Scree Plot shows elbow at k]
    Point2 --> ScreePlot
    classDef axis fill:#f9f,stroke:#333
```

**Reading rule:** Find the **"elbow"** of the scree plot — the point where the steep drop transitions into a gradual slope. Components to the **left of the elbow** are retained; those to the **right** are discarded.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the role of the covariance matrix in Principal Component Analysis? How are eigenvalues and eigenvectors of this matrix used to determine the principal components?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The covariance matrix $\Sigma$ summarizes the joint variability between every pair of features. PCA performs an **eigen-decomposition** of $\Sigma$:
$$
\Sigma v_i = \lambda_i v_i
$$
The **eigenvectors** $v_i$ give the directions (principal axes) of the new feature space, and the **eigenvalues** $\lambda_i$ quantify the variance captured along each direction. Principal components are formed by ordering eigenpairs in **descending order of $\lambda$**, so the first PC corresponds to the largest variance direction.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (–1 mark):** Many students confuse *eigenvectors* with *eigenvalues*. Remember: **eigenvector = direction (axis)**, **eigenvalue = magnitude of variance along that axis**.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State any three differences between PCA and feature selection. Why is PCA classified as a feature *extraction* technique?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | PCA (Feature Extraction) | Feature Selection |
|---|--------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Creates **new** features (linear combinations of originals) | Keeps a **subset** of original features |
| 2 | Output features lose original physical meaning | Retained features remain interpretable |
| 3 | Based on **variance** maximization | Based on statistical tests / model performance |
| 4 | All original features contribute to every PC | Selected features are discrete (kept or dropped) |

PCA is **feature extraction** because the new principal components are mathematically constructed as linear combinations of the original features — the original columns are *not* preserved in the output.

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Derive the mathematical formulation of Principal Component Analysis starting from the variance-maximization objective. Show that the solution reduces to an eigen-value problem of the covariance matrix. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Define the objective (1 mark):** We want a unit vector $w$ that maximizes the variance of the projections $z_i = w^{\top} x_i$ of mean-centered data $x_i$.

$$
\max_{w} \; \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (w^{\top} x_i)^2 \;\;=\;\; \max_{w} \; w^{\top} \Sigma w
$$
subject to $w^{\top} w = 1$.

**Step 2 — Lagrangian formulation (2 marks):**
$$
\mathcal{L}(w, \alpha) = w^{\top} \Sigma w - \alpha (w^{\top} w - 1)
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate and set to zero (2 marks):**
$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = 2 \Sigma w - 2 \alpha w = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Eigen-equation (1 mark):**
$$
\Sigma w = \alpha w
$$
This is the **eigen-value problem**: $w$ is the eigenvector of $\Sigma$ and $\alpha$ the corresponding eigenvalue. The maximum variance equals the largest eigenvalue $\lambda_{\max}$.

**Step 5 — Multi-component extension (1 mark):** The top $k$ principal components correspond to the $k$ eigenvectors of $\Sigma$ with the largest eigenvalues, collected as columns of $W_k = [v_1, v_2, \dots, v_k]$.

---

**(b) Write a complete Python program to implement PCA on the Iris dataset. The program must compute the covariance matrix, perform eigen-decomposition, plot the scree plot, identify the optimal number of components using the 95% cumulative-variance rule, and project the data to the reduced space. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks) — Code:**

```python
# -------------------------------------------------
# PCA Implementation on the Iris Dataset
# Course: PCADL407 - Foundations of AI & DS Lab
# KTU 2024 Scheme - Module Experiment 2
# -------------------------------------------------
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

# ---------- Step 1: Load the dataset ----------
iris = load_iris()
X = iris.data              # shape (150, 4)
y = iris.target            # shape (150,)
feature_names = iris.feature_names

# ---------- Step 2: Standardize the features ----------
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

# ---------- Step 3: Compute the covariance matrix ----------
cov_matrix = np.cov(X_scaled, rowvar=False)
print("Covariance Matrix (4 x 4):")
print(np.round(cov_matrix, 4))

# ---------- Step 4: Eigen-decomposition ----------
eigenvalues, eigenvectors = np.linalg.eig(cov_matrix)
print("\nEigenvalues:", np.round(eigenvalues, 4))

# ---------- Step 5: Sort eigenpairs in descending order ----------
sorted_idx = np.argsort(eigenvalues)[::-1]
eigenvalues = eigenvalues[sorted_idx]
eigenvectors = eigenvectors[:, sorted_idx]

# ---------- Step 6: Explained Variance Ratio ----------
total_variance = np.sum(eigenvalues)
explained_variance_ratio = eigenvalues / total_variance
cumulative_variance = np.cumsum(explained_variance_ratio)

print("\nExplained Variance Ratio per PC:")
print(np.round(explained_variance_ratio, 4))
print("\nCumulative Explained Variance:")
print(np.round(cumulative_variance, 4))

# ---------- Step 7: Find optimal k (95% threshold) ----------
threshold = 0.95
optimal_k = np.argmax(cumulative_variance >= threshold) + 1
print(f"\nOptimal number of components (95% rule): k = {optimal_k}")

# ---------- Step 8: Scree Plot ----------
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))

axes[0].plot(range(1, len(eigenvalues) + 1), eigenvalues, 'o-', color='blue')
axes[0].set_title('Scree Plot')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Principal Component Index')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Eigenvalue (Variance)')
axes[0].grid(True)

# ---------- Step 9: Cumulative Explained Variance Plot ----------
axes[1].plot(range(1, len(cumulative_variance) + 1),
             cumulative_variance, 'o-', color='green')
axes[1].axhline(y=threshold, color='red', linestyle='--', label=f'{threshold*100:.0f}% threshold')
axes[1].axvline(x=optimal_k, color='purple', linestyle='--', label=f'k = {optimal_k}')
axes[1].set_title('Cumulative Explained Variance')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Number of Components')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Cumulative Variance Ratio')
axes[1].legend()
axes[1].grid(True)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# ---------- Step 10: Project data to optimal k dimensions ----------
W_k = eigenvectors[:, :optimal_k]              # shape (4, k)
X_reduced = X_scaled @ W_k                     # shape (150, k)
print(f"\nReduced dataset shape: {X_reduced.shape}")
print("First 5 reduced samples:")
print(np.round(X_reduced[:5], 4))
```

**Expected Output (key values):**
- Eigenvalues (approx): $[2.918, 0.914, 0.147, 0.020]$
- Explained Variance Ratio: $[0.7296, 0.2285, 0.0369, 0.0051]$
- Cumulative Variance: $[0.7296, 0.9581, 0.9949, 1.0000]$
- **Optimal $k = 2$** (since $0.9581 \geq 0.95$)
- Reduced dataset shape: $(150, 2)$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain, with mathematical justification, how the optimal number of principal components is determined using the cumulative explained variance (CEV) rule and the scree-plot elbow method. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Mathematical Foundation (3 marks):**
The total variance of the dataset equals the trace of the covariance matrix:
$$
\text{Total Var} = \text{tr}(\Sigma) = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \lambda_i
$$
The fraction of variance retained by the first $k$ PCs is
$$
\text{CEV}_k = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{p} \lambda_i}
$$
The optimal $k$ is the smallest integer satisfying $\text{CEV}_k \geq 1 - \tau$, where $\tau$ is the acceptable information loss (typically $0.05$).

**The 95% Threshold Rule (2 marks):** Choose the smallest $k$ such that $\text{CEV}_k \geq 0.95$. This is a **quantitative** rule — easy to automate in code.

**The Scree-Plot Elbow Method (2 marks):** Plot eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ vs. component index $i$. Identify the **"elbow"** — the point where the rate of decrease sharply changes. Retain all components up to (and including) the elbow. This is a **qualitative/visual** rule, useful when no threshold is pre-specified. The method was popularized by **Cattell (1966)**.

**Comparison Summary (included for completeness):**

| Aspect | 95% CEV Rule | Scree Elbow |
|--------|--------------|-------------|
| Type | Quantitative | Visual / Heuristic |
| Subjectivity | None | Moderate |
| Best for | Automated pipelines | Exploratory analysis |
| Limitation | Arbitrary threshold | Ambiguous for gentle slopes |

---

**(b) Consider the following 2D dataset:**
$$
X = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 4 & 3 \\ 6 & 5 \\ 8 & 7 \end{bmatrix}
$$
**Hand-compute the covariance matrix, its eigenvalues and eigenvectors, the explained variance ratio, and determine the optimal number of components. Write a Python program that reproduces your hand calculation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Hand Calculation (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Mean-center**
$$
\mu = [5, 4], \quad X_c = \begin{bmatrix} -3 & -3 \\ -1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 3 & 3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Covariance matrix**
$$
\Sigma = \frac{1}{3} X_c^{\top} X_c = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 20 & 20 \\ 20 & 20 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6.667 & 6.667 \\ 6.667 & 6.667 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Eigenvalues**
$$
\lambda_1 = 13.333, \quad \lambda_2 = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Eigenvectors**
$$
v_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad v_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — EVR and CEV**
$$
\text{EVR} = [1.0, 0.0], \quad \text{CEV} = [1.0, 1.0]
$$
**Optimal $k = 1$** (since $\text{CEV}_1 = 1.0 \geq 0.95$).

**Python Code (3 marks):**

```python
import numpy as np

X = np.array([[2, 1], [4, 3], [6, 5], [8, 7]], dtype=float)

# Step 1: Mean-centering
X_c = X - X.mean(axis=0)
print("Mean-centered data:\n", X_c)

# Step 2: Covariance matrix
cov = np.cov(X_c, rowvar=False)
print("\nCovariance matrix:\n", np.round(cov, 4))

# Step 3: Eigen-decomposition
eigvals, eigvecs = np.linalg.eig(cov)
print("\nEigenvalues:", np.round(eigvals, 4))
print("Eigenvectors:\n", np.round(eigvecs, 4))

# Step 4: Sort descending
idx = np.argsort(eigvals)[::-1]
eigvals, eigvecs = eigvals[idx], eigvecs[:, idx]

# Step 5: Explained & cumulative variance
evr = eigvals / eigvals.sum()
cev = np.cumsum(evr)
print("\nExplained Variance Ratio:", np.round(evr, 4))
print("Cumulative Variance:    ", np.round(cev, 4))

# Step 6: Optimal k via 95% rule
k = np.argmax(cev >= 0.95) + 1
print(f"\nOptimal k = {k}")

# Step 7: Projection onto k=1
W = eigvecs[:, :k]
Y = X_c @ W
print("Projected data (n x k):\n", np.round(Y, 4))
```

**Expected Output:**
- Covariance matrix: `[[6.6667, 6.6667], [6.6667, 6.6667]]`
- Eigenvalues: `[13.3333, 0.]`
- EVR: `[1., 0.]`
- **Optimal $k = 1$**
- Projected: `[[-4.2426], [-1.4142], [1.4142], [4.2426]]`

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (–1 to –3 marks each):**
> 1. **Forgetting to standardize** → PCA is dominated by the largest-scale feature. –2 marks.
> 2. **Sorting eigenvalues in ascending order** by mistake. –1 mark.
> 3. **Reporting eigenvectors but not normalizing them** (must be unit vectors). –1 mark.
> 4. **Writing $\text{tr}(\Sigma) = \lambda$** instead of $\text{tr}(\Sigma) = \sum \lambda_i$. –1 mark.
> 5. **Not stating the threshold used** (95%, 99%, etc.) when computing optimal $k$. –1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing PCA with LDA** — PCA is unsupervised (no labels), LDA is supervised. –2 marks.
> 7. **Computing covariance on raw (uncentered) data** — the matrix is then invalid. –2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must memorize for the lab exam):**

- **PCA = linear, unsupervised, variance-maximizing dimensionality reduction.**
- **Pipeline (in order):** Standardize $\rightarrow$ Covariance Matrix $\rightarrow$ Eigen-decompose $\rightarrow$ Sort by $\lambda$ $\rightarrow$ Compute EVR & CEV $\rightarrow$ Choose $k$ $\rightarrow$ Build $W_k$ $\rightarrow$ Project $Y = X_c W_k$.
- **Covariance formula:** $\Sigma_{jk} = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_{ij} - \mu_j)(x_{ik} - \mu_k)$.
- **Covariance matrix is symmetric, positive semi-definite** $\Rightarrow$ all $\lambda_i \geq 0$.
- **Total variance preserved:** $\sum_{i=1}^{p} \lambda_i = \text{tr}(\Sigma)$.
- **Eigenvector = direction (axis) of PC; Eigenvalue = variance along that axis.**
- **Explained Variance Ratio:** $\text{EVR}_i = \frac{\lambda_i}{\sum \lambda_j}$.
- **Cumulative EVR rule (95%):** smallest $k$ with $\text{CEV}_k \geq 0.95$.
- **Scree-plot elbow rule:** keep components before the bend; discard the gentle tail.
- **Always standardize first** — PCA is scale-sensitive.
- **Projection formula:** $Y = X_{\text{centered}} W_k$ where $W_k = [v_1, \dots, v_k]$.
- **Reconstruction:** $\hat{X} = Y W_k^{\top} + \mu$.
- **SVD alternative:** $X_c = U S V^{\top}$ gives $\lambda_i = \sigma_i^2 / (n-1)$ and $V$ = eigenvectors.
- **Scikit-Learn shortcut:** `from sklearn.decomposition import PCA` then `PCA(n_components=k).fit_transform(X_scaled)`.
- **Limitation:** PCA is **linear** — for nonlinear manifolds use t-SNE, UMAP, or Kernel PCA.
- **Lab Viva Quick-Fire:** *"What does a zero eigenvalue mean?"* $\Rightarrow$ A redundant (linearly dependent) feature. *"Why is $n-1$ used and not $n$?"* $\Rightarrow$ Bessel's correction for unbiased sample variance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implement Apriori Algorithm in Python to Find Relationships Among Numerous Items in a Dataset
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADL407 - FOUNDATIONS OF AI AND DATA SCIENCE LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implement Apriori Algorithm in Python to Find Relationships Among Numerous Items in a Dataset

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Apriori Algorithm** is a classical unsupervised machine learning algorithm introduced by *Rakesh Agrawal and Ramakrishnan Srikant in 1994* for **Association Rule Mining (ARM)** over transactional databases. It operates on the principle of *anti-monotonicity* of the **Support metric**, iteratively generating candidate itemsets of increasing size, pruning those that fail to meet a user-defined **minimum support threshold (min_sup)**, and finally extracting **Association Rules** of the form $X \Rightarrow Y$ that satisfy both **minimum support** and **minimum confidence (min_conf)** constraints.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PCADL407 - Foundations of AI and Data Science Lab), this experiment demonstrates how raw transactional data (e.g., market-basket data) can be transformed into actionable, rule-based intelligence describing the *co-occurrence* and *conditional dependency* between items.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** "Implement Apriori Algorithm in Python to find relationships among numerous items in a dataset." Students are expected to (i) preprocess transactions, (ii) apply the Apriori algorithm using a standard Python library, (iii) interpret the generated rules using **Support**, **Confidence**, and **Lift**, and (iv) write a viva-ready lab record with valid engineering conclusions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **supermarket's point-of-sale (POS) data** spanning one year. The manager observes that customers who buy **bread** and **butter** very frequently also buy **jam**. Is this a coincidence, or is it a real cross-selling pattern? The Apriori algorithm answers this question rigorously.

Think of Apriori as a **"Bottom-Up Detective"**:
- It first hunts for **pairs of items** that appear together often enough (e.g., `{Bread, Butter}`).
- Then it asks: *"Are there trios that occur even more often? — for example, `{Bread, Butter, Jam}`."*
- It refuses to even *consider* a large group of items unless *every single smaller subset* of that group has already proven itself popular.

This "no short-cuts" rule is the **Apriori Property** — if a set is rare, none of its supersets can be frequent. It is the same logic as saying: *"If you do not have a high school diploma, you cannot have a college degree."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics used in KTU Lab Viva:**
> - **Support** → *How often does the itemset appear?* (Popularity)
> - **Confidence** → *How strong is the rule?* (Reliability)
> - **Lift** → *How much better than random?* (Usefulness)

### 1.3 What is a Transactional Dataset?

A **transactional dataset** is a collection of records (transactions) where each record contains an unordered set of items purchased/observed together. Formally:

$$D = \{T_1, T_2, T_3, \ldots, T_n\}$$

where each transaction $T_i$ is a non-empty subset of the universal itemset $I = \{i_1, i_2, \ldots, i_m\}$. In the Python ecosystem, this is most commonly stored as a **list of lists** (e.g., `[['Milk','Bread'], ['Bread','Butter'], ...]`).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Core Metrics — Bulleted Logic Steps

The Apriori Algorithm is fundamentally driven by **three metrics**. Each metric answers a different *business* and *statistical* question.

#### A. Support — *The Popularity Quotient*

1. **Purpose:** Measures the proportion of transactions in the database that contain a given itemset.
2. **Intuition:** Out of 10,000 customer bills, if 1,000 contain `{Bread, Butter}`, then Support = **10 %**.
3. **Range:** $0 \le \text{Support}(A) \le 1$ (or expressed as 0 % – 100 %).
4. **Engineering Use:** Filters out statistically insignificant patterns; small support values typically mean the rule is *noise*.

#### B. Confidence — *The Rule's Reliability*

1. **Purpose:** Measures the conditional probability $P(Y \mid X)$ — i.e., the likelihood that itemset $Y$ is purchased *given* that itemset $X$ is already in the cart.
2. **Intuition:** If 80 % of customers who buy `{Diaper}` also buy `{Beer}`, the rule `{Diaper} \Rightarrow {Beer}` has Confidence = **80 %**.
3. **Range:** $0 \le \text{Confidence}(X \Rightarrow Y) \le 1$.
4. **Engineering Use:** Confidence is *directional* — $\text{Conf}(X \Rightarrow Y) \neq \text{Conf}(Y \Rightarrow X)$.

#### C. Lift — *The Statistical Usefulness*

1. **Purpose:** Compares the observed co-occurrence against what would be expected *if items were statistically independent*.
2. **Intuition:** Lift = 1.0 means items are independent; Lift > 1.0 means *positive correlation* (good for cross-selling); Lift < 1.0 means *negative correlation* (substitute products).
3. **Range:** $\text{Lift} \in [0, \infty)$.
4. **Engineering Use:** Lift corrects Confidence for the *base popularity* of the consequent.

### 2.2 The Apriori Property (Anti-Monotonicity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Apriori Property (Downward Closure):** *If an itemset $I$ is infrequent (i.e., its support is below min_sup), then any superset of $I$ is guaranteed to be infrequent.*

- **Why it works:** A superset occurs *at most* as often as any of its subsets. Formally, $\text{Support}(A \cup B) \le \text{Support}(A)$.
- **Engineering Benefit:** This property enables **massive pruning** of the search space. The candidate generation cost is exponentially reduced.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Metric / Formula | Symbolic Expression | Range / Units | Engineering Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Support of Itemset A** | $\text{Support}(A) = \dfrac{\vert \{T \in D \mid A \subseteq T\} \vert}{\vert D \vert}$ | $[0, 1]$ or $[\,0\,\%, 100\,\%\,]$ | Popularity threshold |
| 2 | **Support of Rule** $A \Rightarrow B$ | $\text{Support}(A \Rightarrow B) = \text{Support}(A \cup B)$ | $[0, 1]$ | Joint probability |
| 3 | **Confidence of Rule** $A \Rightarrow B$ | $\text{Confidence}(A \Rightarrow B) = \dfrac{\text{Support}(A \cup B)}{\text{Support}(A)}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Conditional probability |
| 4 | **Lift of Rule** $A \Rightarrow B$ | $\text{Lift}(A \Rightarrow B) = \dfrac{\text{Confidence}(A \Rightarrow B)}{\text{Support}(B)} = \dfrac{\text{Support}(A \cup B)}{\text{Support}(A) \cdot \text{Support}(B)}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Correlation strength |
| 5 | **Leverage** | $\text{Leverage}(A \Rightarrow B) = \text{Support}(A \cup B) - \text{Support}(A) \cdot \text{Support}(B)$ | $[-1, 1]$ | Difference from independence |
| 6 | **Conviction** | $\text{Conviction}(A \Rightarrow B) = \dfrac{1 - \text{Support}(B)}{1 - \text{Confidence}(A \Rightarrow B)}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Directional reliability |
| 7 | **Apriori Pruning Rule** | If $\text{Support}(S) < \text{min\_sup} \Rightarrow \forall S' \supset S,\; \text{Support}(S') < \text{min\_sup}$ | Logical implication | Pruning condition |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember:** For KTU lab evaluations, students *must* show all three of **Support, Confidence, and Lift** in their output table. Markers explicitly look for the **Lift > 1** filter to declare a "useful" rule.

### 2.4 Step-Wise Operational Algorithm

The Apriori algorithm proceeds in two alternating phases — the **Join** step and the **Prune** step — for each value of $k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ :

1. **Initialization:** $k = 1$. Generate $C_1$ = set of all 1-itemsets.
2. **Support Counting:** Compute the support of each candidate in $C_k$ by scanning the database.
3. **Frequent Itemset Generation:** $L_k = \{ c \in C_k \mid \text{Support}(c) \ge \text{min\_sup} \}$.
4. **Termination Check:** If $L_k = \emptyset$, stop. The final frequent itemsets are $\bigcup_{j=1}^{k-1} L_j$.
5. **Join Step:** Generate $C_{k+1}$ as the *self-join* $L_k \bowtie L_k$ (candidates of size $k+1$).
6. **Prune Step:** Discard any $(k+1)$-candidate that has at least one $k$-subset not in $L_k$.
7. **Rule Generation (Post-Step):** From each frequent itemset $F$, generate all non-empty subsets $s \subset F$ and form rules $s \Rightarrow (F - s)$, keeping those satisfying $\text{Confidence} \ge \text{min\_conf}$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Retail / E-Commerce** | Market-basket analysis, cross-selling, product placement |
| **Banking & FinTech** | Fraud-pattern detection in transaction logs |
| **Healthcare** | Co-prescription pattern mining, disease-symptom correlation |
| **Telecommunications** | Bundled-service recommendation (calling + SMS + data) |
| **Web Mining** | Clickstream pattern discovery, UX personalization |
| **Cybersecurity** | Intrusion rule mining from network event logs |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example (Hand-Worked — Board Exam Standard)

> **Dataset (D) of 5 transactions:**
> - $T_1 = \{ \text{Bread, Butter, Jam} \}$
> - $T_2 = \{ \text{Bread, Butter} \}$
> - $T_3 = \{ \text{Butter, Jam, Milk} \}$
> - $T_4 = \{ \text{Bread, Jam, Milk} \}$
> - $T_5 = \{ \text{Bread, Butter, Jam} \}$

**Parameters:** $\text{min\_sup} = 40\,\%$, $\text{min\_conf} = 60\,\%$.

#### Step 1 — Count 1-Itemset Support

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Support}(\text{Bread}) &= \frac{\vert \{T_1, T_2, T_4, T_5\} \vert}{5} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.80 \\[4pt]
\text{Support}(\text{Butter}) &= \frac{\vert \{T_1, T_2, T_3, T_5\} \vert}{5} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.80 \\[4pt]
\text{Support}(\text{Jam}) &= \frac{\vert \{T_1, T_3, T_4, T_5\} \vert}{5} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.80 \\[4pt]
\text{Support}(\text{Milk}) &= \frac{\vert \{T_3, T_4\} \vert}{5} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Frequent 1-itemsets** $L_1 = \{ \{\text{Bread}\}, \{\text{Butter}\}, \{\text{Jam}\}, \{\text{Milk}\} \}$ — all four pass the 40 % threshold.

#### Step 2 — Generate Candidate 2-Itemsets $C_2$

$$
C_2 = \big\{ \{\text{Bread, Butter}\},\; \{\text{Bread, Jam}\},\; \{\text{Bread, Milk}\},\; \{\text{Butter, Jam}\},\; \{\text{Butter, Milk}\},\; \{\text{Jam, Milk}\} \big\}
$$

#### Step 3 — Count 2-Itemset Support

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Support}(\text{Bread, Butter}) &= \frac{\vert \{T_1, T_2, T_5\} \vert}{5} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.60 \\[4pt]
\text{Support}(\text{Bread, Jam}) &= \frac{\vert \{T_1, T_4, T_5\} \vert}{5} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.60 \\[4pt]
\text{Support}(\text{Bread, Milk}) &= \frac{\vert \{T_4\} \vert}{5} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.20 \\[4pt]
\text{Support}(\text{Butter, Jam}) &= \frac{\vert \{T_1, T_3, T_5\} \vert}{5} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.60 \\[4pt]
\text{Support}(\text{Butter, Milk}) &= \frac{\vert \{T_3\} \vert}{5} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.20 \\[4pt]
\text{Support}(\text{Jam, Milk}) &= \frac{\vert \{T_3, T_4\} \vert}{5} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Prune:** $\{\text{Bread, Milk}\}$ and $\{\text{Butter, Milk}\}$ are removed (Support = 0.20 < 0.40).

$$
L_2 = \big\{ \{\text{Bread, Butter}\},\; \{\text{Bread, Jam}\},\; \{\text{Butter, Jam}\},\; \{\text{Jam, Milk}\} \big\}
$$

#### Step 4 — Generate Candidate 3-Itemsets $C_3$ (Join $L_2 \bowtie L_2$)

$$
C_3 = \big\{ \{\text{Bread, Butter, Jam}\},\; \{\text{Bread, Jam, Milk}\},\; \{\text{Butter, Jam, Milk}\} \big\}
$$

#### Step 5 — Prune via Apriori Property

For candidate $\{\text{Butter, Jam, Milk}\}$, its 2-subsets are:
$\{\text{Butter, Jam}\} \in L_2$ ✓, $\{\text{Butter, Milk}\} \notin L_2$ ✗ → **Prune**.
Similarly, $\{\text{Bread, Jam, Milk}\}$ contains $\{\text{Bread, Milk}\} \notin L_2$ → **Prune**.

$$
C_3^{\text{after prune}} = \big\{ \{\text{Bread, Butter, Jam}\} \big\}
$$

#### Step 6 — Count 3-Itemset Support

$$
\text{Support}(\text{Bread, Butter, Jam}) = \frac{\vert \{T_1, T_5\} \vert}{5} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.40
$$

$$
L_3 = \big\{ \{\text{Bread, Butter, Jam}\} \big\}
$$

#### Step 7 — Rule Generation from $L_3$

For itemset $F = \{\text{Bread, Butter, Jam}\}$, generate all non-empty proper subsets $s$ and form $s \Rightarrow (F - s)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{R}_1: \{\text{Bread, Butter}\} &\Rightarrow \{\text{Jam}\} \\[4pt]
\text{Confidence}(\text{R}_1) &= \frac{\text{Support}(\text{Bread, Butter, Jam})}{\text{Support}(\text{Bread, Butter})} = \frac{0.40}{0.60} = 0.667 \;(66.7\,\%) \\[8pt]
\text{R}_2: \{\text{Bread, Jam}\} &\Rightarrow \{\text{Butter}\} \\[4pt]
\text{Confidence}(\text{R}_2) &= \frac{0.40}{0.60} = 0.667 \;(66.7\,\%) \\[8pt]
\text{R}_3: \{\text{Butter, Jam}\} &\Rightarrow \{\text{Bread}\} \\[4pt]
\text{Confidence}(\text{R}_3) &= \frac{0.40}{0.60} = 0.667 \;(66.7\,\%) \\[8pt]
\text{R}_4: \{\text{Bread}\} &\Rightarrow \{\text{Butter, Jam}\} \\[4pt]
\text{Confidence}(\text{R}_4) &= \frac{0.40}{0.80} = 0.500 \;(50.0\,\%) \;\;\textcolor{red}{<\,0.60 \;\Rightarrow \text{REJECTED}} \\[8pt]
\text{R}_5: \{\text{Butter}\} &\Rightarrow \{\text{Bread, Jam}\} \\[4pt]
\text{Confidence}(\text{R}_5) &= \frac{0.40}{0.80} = 0.500 \;\;\textcolor{red}{<\,0.60 \;\Rightarrow \text{REJECTED}} \\[8pt]
\text{R}_6: \{\text{Jam}\} &\Rightarrow \{\text{Bread, Butter}\} \\[4pt]
\text{Confidence}(\text{R}_6) &= \frac{0.40}{0.80} = 0.500 \;\;\textcolor{red}{<\,0.60 \;\Rightarrow \text{REJECTED}}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 8 — Lift Calculation for the Three Accepted Rules

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lift}(\text{R}_1) &= \frac{\text{Support}(\text{Bread, Butter, Jam})}{\text{Support}(\text{Bread, Butter}) \cdot \text{Support}(\text{Jam})} = \frac{0.40}{0.60 \times 0.80} = 0.833 \\[4pt]
\text{Lift}(\text{R}_2) &= \frac{0.40}{0.60 \times 0.80} = 0.833 \\[4pt]
\text{Lift}(\text{R}_3) &= \frac{0.40}{0.60 \times 0.80} = 0.833
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Inference:** Lift = 0.833 < 1.0 means the consequent itemset is *slightly negatively correlated* with the antecedent. The items are bought together *less often* than random chance would predict, even though they frequently co-occur in absolute terms. This is a classic illustration of why **Lift is essential** — Confidence alone would have falsely declared these as "strong" rules.

### 3.2 Reference Python Implementation (Standard Library: `apyori` / `mlxtend`)

```python
# Implementation 1: Using the 'apyori' library (classic KTU lab approach)
# pip install apyori

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from apyori import apriori

# Step 1: Load the dataset
store_data = pd.read_csv('store_data.csv', header=None)

# Step 2: Convert DataFrame to list-of-lists
#   Each inner list is a transaction (row) with non-null items only.
transactions = []
for i in range(len(store_data)):
    transactions.append([str(store_data.values[i, j])
                         for j in range(store_data.shape[1])
                         if str(store_data.values[i, j]) != 'nan'])

# Step 3: Train the Apriori model on the transactions
rules = apriori(transactions=transactions,
                min_support=0.003,
                min_confidence=0.2,
                min_lift=3,
                min_length=2,
                max_length=2)

# Step 4: Visualise the rules
results = list(rules)
print("Number of extracted rules:", len(results))

for rule in results:
    pair = rule[0]
    items = [x for x in pair]
    print("Rule: " + items[0] + " -> " + items[1])
    print("Support: " + str(round(rule[1], 4)))
    print("Confidence: " + str(round(rule[2][0][2], 4)))
    print("Lift: " + str(round(rule[2][0][3], 4)))
    print("=====================================")
```

```python
# Implementation 2: Using 'mlxtend' (more readable, modern, KTU-recommended)
# pip install mlxtend

import pandas as pd
from mlxtend.preprocessing import TransactionEncoder
from mlxtend.frequent_patterns import apriori, association_rules

te = TransactionEncoder()
te_ary = te.fit(transactions).transform(transactions)
df = pd.DataFrame(te_ary, columns=te.columns_)

frequent_itemsets = apriori(df, min_support=0.05, use_colnames=True)
rules = association_rules(frequent_itemsets, metric="lift", min_threshold=1.2)

print(frequent_itemsets.head())
print(rules[['antecedents','consequents','support','confidence','lift']].head(10))
```

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Selection Guide

| min_support | Dataset Size | Behaviour | When to Use |
|:---:|:---:|---|---|
| 0.001 – 0.005 | Very Large (> 100 k) | Many rules | Web clickstream mining |
| 0.01 – 0.05 | Large (10 k – 100 k) | Moderate rules | Retail market-basket |
| 0.10 – 0.30 | Small (< 1 k) | Few rules | Lab experiments, teaching |
| > 0.50 | Toy data | Almost no rules | Sanity checks only |

| min_confidence | Typical Interpretation | Risk |
|:---:|---|---|
| < 0.30 | Weak, exploratory | Spurious correlations |
| 0.40 – 0.60 | Reasonable for retail | May contain redundant rules |
| 0.70 – 0.90 | Strong rules | Misses long-tail patterns |
| = 1.0 | Logical implication | Extremely rare in real data |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Apriori Algorithm — Iterative Block-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start Apriori Procedure]) --> A1[Load Transactional Database D]
    A1 --> A2[Set min_sup and min_conf thresholds]
    A2 --> A3[Initialise k equals 1]
    A3 --> A4[Generate Ck candidates of size k]
    A4 --> A5[Scan D to count Support of every candidate in Ck]
    A5 --> A6[Filter Lk equals set of candidates with Support greater than or equal to min_sup]
    A6 --> A7{Is Lk empty?}
    A7 -- Yes --> A8[Stop. Final Frequent Itemsets are union of all L1 to Lk minus 1]
    A7 -- No --> A9[Increment k equals k plus 1]
    A9 --> A10[Join Lk minus 1 with itself to form Ck]
    A10 --> A11[Prune any candidate with a subset not in Lk minus 1]
    A11 --> A4
    A8 --> A12[Rule Generation Phase: for every frequent itemset F]
    A12 --> A13[Generate all non empty proper subsets s of F]
    A13 --> A14[Compute Confidence of rule s implies F minus s]
    A14 --> A15{Confidence greater than or equal to min_conf?}
    A15 -- Yes --> A16[Accept rule. Compute Lift]
    A15 -- No --> A17[Reject rule]
    A16 --> A18[Output Final Association Rules Table]
    A17 --> A18
    A18 --> A19([End of Apriori Procedure])
```

### 4.2 Apriori Pruning Architecture (Anti-Monotonicity Visualisation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[All possible itemsets in D] --> B1{Check Support}
    B1 -- Support less than min_sup --> B2[Mark as INFREQUENT]
    B1 -- Support greater than or equal to min_sup --> B3[Mark as FREQUENT]
    B2 --> B4[Apriori Property applies]
    B4 --> B5[All SUPERSETS of this infrequent itemset are automatically pruned]
    B5 --> B6[Search space drastically reduced]
    B3 --> B7[Proceed to generate LARGER candidate itemsets]
    B7 --> B1
    B6 --> B8[Frequent Itemsets L final]
    B7 --> B8
```

### 4.3 End-to-End Lab Experiment Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C0[Raw CSV of Transactions] --> C1[Data Preprocessing in Pandas]
    C1 --> C2[Remove null values and headers]
    C2 --> C3[Convert to list of lists]
    C3 --> C4[Apply apriori function]
    C4 --> C5[Frequent Itemsets DataFrame]
    C5 --> C6[Generate Association Rules]
    C6 --> C7[Filter by min_conf and min_lift]
    C7 --> C8[Final Rules Table]
    C8 --> C9[Engineering Interpretation and Viva Answer]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Lab Architecture Insight:** Block C4 corresponds to the **Join + Prune + Count** core of the Apriori algorithm. Block C9 is the *intellectual value-add* — students must explain in their lab record *why* a given rule is significant (e.g., "Lift > 1.5 indicates that buying **Diapers** strongly increases the likelihood of buying **Beer**, justifying bundle promotions in the baby-care aisle.").
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

---

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Q1.** Define the **Apriori property** in association rule mining. Why is it important for computational efficiency?

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Apriori property (or *anti-monotonicity of support*) states that *if an itemset is infrequent, then all of its supersets are also infrequent*. Formally,
> $$\text{If } \text{Support}(S) < \text{min\_sup} \Rightarrow \forall\, S' \supset S,\; \text{Support}(S') < \text{min\_sup}.$$
> It is critical because it allows the algorithm to prune exponential portions of the candidate-generation search space without computing the support of every possible superset. **(1 mark** definition, **1 mark** formal statement, **1 mark** explanation of pruning benefit**.)**

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Q2.** Differentiate between **Support**, **Confidence**, and **Lift** with one-line definitions.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Support:** The fraction of transactions in $D$ that contain a given itemset. *(1 mark)*
> 2. **Confidence:** The conditional probability of the consequent given the antecedent; it measures rule reliability. *(1 mark)*
> 3. **Lift:** The ratio of observed support to expected support under statistical independence; it measures rule usefulness and corrects Confidence for base popularity. *(1 mark)*

---

### Part B — 14-Mark ESE Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]*

**(a)** Explain the **Apriori algorithm** with a clear block diagram. Discuss its **join** and **prune** steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following transaction database. Apply the Apriori algorithm with $\text{min\_sup} = 50\,\%$ and $\text{min\_conf} = 75\,\%$. List all frequent itemsets and the strong association rules. Compute the **Lift** of each rule. **(7 marks)**

| TID | Items |
|:---:|:---|
| 100 | A, B, C |
| 200 | A, C |
| 300 | A, D |
| 400 | B, C, D |
| 500 | A, B, C |
| 600 | B, C |

---

> **Model Solution:**

**(a) Algorithm Explanation (7 marks):**

The Apriori algorithm (Agrawal & Srikant, 1994) is a level-wise, breadth-first algorithm for mining frequent itemsets and association rules. It operates on the **downward-closure property** of support.

* **Procedure:**
  1. Initialise $k = 1$ and generate $C_1$ = all 1-itemsets.
  2. Scan $D$ to compute support of each candidate in $C_k$.
  3. $L_k = \{ c \in C_k \mid \text{Support}(c) \ge \text{min\_sup} \}$.
  4. If $L_k = \emptyset$, stop.
  5. **Join step:** $C_{k+1} = L_k \bowtie L_k$ — combine pairs in $L_k$ sharing the first $k - 1$ items to form $(k+1)$-candidates.
  6. **Prune step:** Remove any $(k+1)$-candidate that has a $k$-subset not in $L_k$ (applies Apriori property).
  7. Set $k \leftarrow k + 1$ and go to step 2.
  8. After mining $L = \bigcup L_k$, generate rules from each $F \in L$ by enumerating all non-empty subsets $s \subset F$, keeping those with $\text{Confidence}(s \Rightarrow F - s) \ge \text{min\_conf}$.

* **Complexity:** Worst-case $O(2^{m} \cdot n)$ where $m$ = number of unique items, $n$ = number of transactions. Apriori pruning reduces the *constant* in this exponential dramatically.

* **Block diagram:** Refer to the Mermaid flowcharts in Section 4 of these notes.

---

**(b) Worked Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — 1-Itemset Support** (threshold = 0.50, so frequency ≥ 3):

| Item | Count | Support |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A | 4 | 0.67 |
| B | 4 | 0.67 |
| C | 5 | 0.83 |
| D | 2 | 0.33 |

$$
L_1 = \{ \{A\},\; \{B\},\; \{C\} \} \quad (\text{D is pruned})
$$

**Step 2 — 2-Itemset Candidates and Support:**

| Pair | Count | Support |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A, B | 2 | 0.33 |
| A, C | 3 | 0.50 |
| B, C | 4 | 0.67 |

$$
L_2 = \{ \{A,C\},\; \{B,C\} \}
$$

**Step 3 — 3-Itemset Candidates and Pruning:**

$C_3 = \{A, B, C\}$. Subsets: $\{A,B\} \notin L_2 \Rightarrow$ **Prune**.

$$
L_3 = \emptyset \quad \Rightarrow \quad L_{\text{final}} = L_1 \cup L_2
$$

**Step 4 — Rule Generation from $L_2$** (min_conf = 0.75):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{R}_1:\; A &\Rightarrow C,\; \text{Conf} = \frac{0.50}{0.67} = 0.75 \;\checkmark \\
\text{R}_2:\; C &\Rightarrow A,\; \text{Conf} = \frac{0.50}{0.83} = 0.60 \;\times \\
\text{R}_3:\; B &\Rightarrow C,\; \text{Conf} = \frac{0.50}{0.67} = 0.75 \;\checkmark \\
\text{R}_4:\; C &\Rightarrow B,\; \text{Conf} = \frac{0.67}{0.83} = 0.80 \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Lift Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lift}(A \Rightarrow C) &= \frac{0.50}{0.67 \times 0.83} \approx 0.90 \\[2pt]
\text{Lift}(B \Rightarrow C) &= \frac{0.50}{0.67 \times 0.83} \approx 0.90 \\[2pt]
\text{Lift}(C \Rightarrow B) &= \frac{0.67}{0.83 \times 0.67} \approx 1.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Strong Rules:**

| Antecedent | Consequent | Support | Confidence | Lift |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A | C | 0.50 | 0.75 | 0.90 |
| B | C | 0.50 | 0.75 | 0.90 |
| C | B | 0.67 | 0.80 | 1.20 |

> **Interpretation:** The rule $C \Rightarrow B$ has Lift = 1.20 > 1.0, indicating a *positive correlation* — buying **C** increases the likelihood of buying **B** by 20 % above random chance. This is the only *statistically useful* rule. **(7 marks split: 2 marks for $L_1$ / $L_2$, 2 marks for rule generation, 2 marks for Lift, 1 mark for engineering interpretation.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* — *[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **steps of Association Rule Mining**. Compare **Support** versus **Confidence** versus **Lift** using a small numerical example of your own. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A retail store has 6 transactions as shown. Using $\text{min\_sup} = 33\,\%$ and $\text{min\_conf} = 70\,\%$, generate all **strong association rules** using the Apriori algorithm. Justify each rejection with a numerical reason. **(7 marks)**

| TID | Items Bought |
|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Shoes, Socks, Cap |
| 2 | Shoes, Socks |
| 3 | Cap, Socks |
| 4 | Shoes, Cap |
| 5 | Shoes, Socks, Cap |
| 6 | Cap, Socks |

---

> **Model Solution:**

**(a) ARM Pipeline + Comparison (7 marks):**

The three-stage pipeline is:
1. **Frequent Itemset Generation** (using min_sup).
2. **Rule Generation** (using min_conf).
3. **Rule Evaluation / Pruning** (using Lift, Leverage, Conviction).

*Comparison via a 5-transaction toy example:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Support}(A) &= 3/5 = 0.60,\quad \text{Support}(B) = 3/5 = 0.60,\quad \text{Support}(A,B) = 2/5 = 0.40 \\[4pt]
\text{Confidence}(A \Rightarrow B) &= 0.40/0.60 = 0.67 \\[4pt]
\text{Lift}(A \Rightarrow B) &= 0.40/(0.60 \times 0.60) = 1.11
\end{aligned}
$$

* Support answers *popularity*, Confidence answers *reliability*, Lift answers *usefulness*. **(7 marks: 3 for diagram/stages, 2 for example, 2 for comparison discussion.)**

**(b) Worked Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

$\text{min\_sup} = 33\,\% \Rightarrow \text{count} \ge 2$ for 6 transactions.

**Step 1 — $L_1$:** Each item appears in 4, 4, 5, 4 transactions (Shoes, Socks, Cap, varying). All pass.

**Step 2 — $L_2$:** Compute all 2-pairs (3 candidates).

| Pair | Count | Support |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Shoes, Socks | 3 | 0.50 ✓ |
| Shoes, Cap | 3 | 0.50 ✓ |
| Socks, Cap | 4 | 0.67 ✓ |

**Step 3 — $L_3$ Candidate:** $\{ \text{Shoes, Socks, Cap} \}$ count = 2 → Support = 0.33 ≥ 0.33 ✓ ⇒ **Keep**.

**Step 4 — Rule Generation** (min_conf = 0.70):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\{\text{Shoes, Socks}\} &\Rightarrow \{\text{Cap}\},\; \text{Conf} = \tfrac{2}{3} = 0.67 \;\;\textcolor{red}{<\,0.70 \Rightarrow \text{Reject}} \\[4pt]
\{\text{Shoes, Cap}\} &\Rightarrow \{\text{Socks}\},\; \text{Conf} = \tfrac{2}{3} = 0.67 \;\;\textcolor{red}{<\,0.70 \Rightarrow \text{Reject}} \\[4pt]
\{\text{Socks, Cap}\} &\Rightarrow \{\text{Shoes}\},\; \text{Conf} = \tfrac{2}{4} = 0.50 \;\;\textcolor{red}{<\,0.70 \Rightarrow \text{Reject}} \\[4pt]
\{\text{Shoes}\} &\Rightarrow \{\text{Socks, Cap}\},\; \text{Conf} = \tfrac{2}{4} = 0.50 \;\;\textcolor{red}{\text{Reject}} \\[4pt]
\{\text{Socks}\} &\Rightarrow \{\text{Shoes, Cap}\},\; \text{Conf} = \tfrac{2}{4} = 0.50 \;\;\textcolor{red}{\text{Reject}} \\[4pt]
\{\text{Cap}\} &\Rightarrow \{\text{Shoes, Socks}\},\; \text{Conf} = \tfrac{2}{5} = 0.40 \;\;\textcolor{red}{\text{Reject}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Strong Rules:** **None** survive. **(7 marks split: 2 for $L_1$/$L_2$, 2 for $L_3$, 2 for rule generation, 1 for final answer.)**

**Interpretation:** Lowering $\text{min\_sup}$ to 0.30 or $\text{min\_conf}$ to 0.60 would be required to extract meaningful rules from this dataset. This demonstrates the **sensitivity of the Apriori algorithm to threshold selection**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Apriori prune step:** Many students generate $C_k$ but skip pruning subsets that are not in $L_{k-1}$. This results in wasted computation and *incorrect* candidate sets. **−2 marks.**
> 2. **Reporting only Confidence:** Confidence ≥ min_conf does NOT imply a useful rule. You *must* show **Lift**. If Lift < 1, the rule is misleading even if confidence is high. **−2 marks.**
> 3. **Confusing Support of a rule with Support of an itemset:** They are not the same; $\text{Support}(A \Rightarrow B) = \text{Support}(A \cup B)$. **−1 mark.**
> 4. **Not showing intermediate $C_k$ and $L_k$ tables:** Examiners allocate marks for *each* level. Skipping a level costs ~2 marks.
> 5. **Hard-coding thresholds without justification:** Always state the *business reason* for the chosen min_sup and min_conf in your lab record.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] Apriori mines **frequent itemsets** and **association rules** from transactional data.
- [x] Built on the **Apriori property (anti-monotonicity):** infrequent ⇒ all supersets are infrequent.
- [x] Iterative: alternates between **Join** (candidate generation) and **Prune** (anti-monotone elimination).
- [x] **Support = Popularity** of itemset; threshold filters noise.
- [x] **Confidence = Reliability** of a rule; threshold filters weak rules.
- [x] **Lift > 1 ⇒ useful positive correlation**; Lift = 1 ⇒ independence; Lift < 1 ⇒ negative correlation.
- [x] **Lift corrects Confidence** for the base popularity of the consequent — always report it.
- [x] Algorithm terminates when $L_k = \emptyset$ (no new frequent itemsets).
- [x] In Python: `apyori.apriori(...)` and `mlxtend.frequent_patterns.apriori(...)` are the two standard implementations.
- [x] **Data preprocessing** is mandatory: convert DataFrame → list-of-lists → boolean-encoded matrix (for `mlxtend`).
- [x] Threshold sensitivity: very low min_sup → combinatorial explosion; very high min_sup → no rules.
- [x] **Real-world use:** market-basket, recommendation engines, fraud detection, bioinformatics, web usage mining.
- [x] **Always include an Engineering Interpretation** in the lab conclusion — *why* does the rule matter?
- [x] **Default starting thresholds for KTU lab:** `min_support = 0.003`, `min_confidence = 0.2`, `min_lift = 3` (when using the classic `store_data.csv`).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implement FP-Growth Algorithm in Python to Find Frequent Patterns in Large Database Efficiently
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADL407 - FOUNDATIONS OF AI AND DATA SCIENCE LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implement FP-Growth Algorithm in Python to Find Frequent Patterns in Large Database Efficiently

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**FP-Growth (Frequent Pattern Growth)** is a highly efficient association rule mining algorithm introduced by *Han, Pei, and Yin (2000)* that discovers frequent itemsets from large transactional databases without generating candidate itemsets explicitly. Unlike the traditional Apriori algorithm which relies on repeated database scans and candidate generation, FP-Growth uses a compact tree-based data structure known as the **FP-Tree (Frequent Pattern Tree)** and a divide-and-conquer strategy known as **pattern fragment growth**.

The algorithm works in two main phases:
1. **Phase 1 — Build FP-Tree:** Compress the input transaction database into a highly compact FP-Tree by mapping each transaction onto a path in the tree, sharing common prefixes to achieve maximum compression.
2. **Phase 2 — Mine Frequent Patterns:** Recursively mine frequent patterns by constructing **conditional pattern bases**, then **conditional FP-Trees**, and finally extracting patterns of length ≥ 1 from each conditional tree.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PCADL407 Module 2):** Students must implement FP-Growth in Python, demonstrate its working on a small transactional dataset, and verify the result against a manually computed expected output. Comparison of execution time with the Apriori algorithm is also expected.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **supermarket billing system** that wants to find which products are frequently bought together. The naive approach (Apriori) would be like writing down *every possible combination* of products on paper and checking each one against all customer bills — a *huge* waste of paper and time.

**FP-Growth** is smarter. It works like a **filing clerk who keeps bills in shared folders**:
- All bills that start with `{Bread, Butter}` are filed in the same folder.
- Bills that start with `{Bread, Milk}` go into another shared sub-folder.
- Instead of checking all combinations, the clerk just looks at how often each "shared prefix" appears in the folders.

This *shared folder* idea is exactly what the **FP-Tree** does — common prefixes are stored once, and the algorithm only walks through the tree to find patterns. The result: **massively reduced memory usage and only two database scans** (one for counting, one for tree building).

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Itemset** | A collection of one or more items. |
| **Support count σ(X)** | Number of transactions containing itemset $X$. |
| **Support s(X)** | $\frac{\sigma(X)}{N}$, where $N$ is the total number of transactions. |
| **Frequent Itemset** | An itemset whose support ≥ user-defined **min_support** threshold. |
| **FP-Tree** | A compact prefix-tree representation of the transaction database. |
| **Header Table** | Linked list structure mapping each frequent item to its nodes in the tree. |
| **Conditional Pattern Base** | The set of prefix paths leading to a particular item (suffix). |
| **Conditional FP-Tree** | FP-Tree built from the conditional pattern base of an item. |
| **Pattern Fragment Growth** | Recursive mining of patterns from conditional trees. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — FP-Tree (Frequent Pattern Tree):**
> A rooted tree where:
> - The root is labeled `null`.
> - Each internal node represents a frequent 1-itemset.
> - Each node stores an item name and a count.
> - Branches represent the itemsets in transactions, with shared prefixes appearing as shared tree paths.
> - A **node-link** pointer connects all nodes bearing the same item to facilitate traversal.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation

Association rule mining is governed by three foundational metrics:

$$
\text{Support}(X) = \frac{\sigma(X)}{N}
$$

$$
\text{Confidence}(A \Rightarrow B) = \frac{\sigma(A \cup B)}{\sigma(A)}
$$

$$
\text{Lift}(A \Rightarrow B) = \frac{\text{Confidence}(A \Rightarrow B)}{\text{Support}(B)} = \frac{\sigma(A \cup B)}{\sigma(A) \cdot \sigma(B)}
$$

where:
- $X$ = an itemset,
- $\sigma(X)$ = **support count** of $X$ (number of transactions containing $X$),
- $N$ = total number of transactions in database $D$.

A pattern $X$ is called **frequent** if:

$$
\text{Support}(X) \geq \text{min\_support}
$$

## 2.2 FP-Growth Algorithm — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The algorithm proceeds in **two main phases**, each composed of structured sub-steps:

### Phase 1 — Construct the FP-Tree

1. **First Database Scan:** Compute the support count of every 1-itemset in $D$.
2. **Filter:** Discard items whose support count < $\text{min\_support} \times N$.
3. **Sort Frequent Items:** Order the remaining frequent items in **descending order of support count** (ties broken lexicographically). This sorted list forms the **Header Table $L$**.
4. **Second Database Scan:** For each transaction, keep only the frequent items, sort them according to $L$, and insert the resulting sequence into the FP-Tree. Shared prefixes are merged; new branches are created when prefixes diverge.
5. **Node-Link Maintenance:** For each item, a linked list is maintained from the header table to all tree nodes carrying that item, enabling fast traversal during mining.

### Phase 2 — Mine Frequent Patterns (Recursive)

For each item $a_i$ in the Header Table (typically processed in **ascending order of support** to keep recursion shallow):

1. Start a **single-path pattern** $\beta = \{(a_i : \sigma)\}$.
2. Construct the **Conditional Pattern Base (CPB)** of $a_i$: collect all prefix paths ending at $a_i$, with their respective counts.
3. Compute the support count of each item in the CPB.
4. Build the **Conditional FP-Tree** of $a_i$ using only items from CPB whose support ≥ $\text{min\_support}$.
5. If the conditional FP-Tree is **not empty**:
   - If it is a **single path** $P$, generate all combinations of nodes in $P \cup \{a_i\}$.
   - If it is a **multi-path**, recursively call `mine_tree()` on this conditional tree.
6. Concatenate generated patterns with $\beta$ to form the final frequent patterns.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Support** | $s(X) = \frac{\sigma(X)}{N}$ | Fraction of transactions containing $X$ |
| **Confidence** | $c(A \Rightarrow B) = \frac{\sigma(A \cup B)}{\sigma(A)}$ | Strength of implication $A \rightarrow B$ |
| **Lift** | $\text{Lift}(A \Rightarrow B) = \frac{\sigma(A \cup B)}{\sigma(A)\,\sigma(B)}$ | $1$ = independent, $>1$ = positively correlated |
| **Frequent condition** | $\sigma(X) \geq \lceil \text{min\_support} \times N \rceil$ | $X$ qualifies as frequent |
| **Tree compression ratio** | $\text{CompRatio} = 1 - \frac{\text{Tree size}}{\text{Database size}}$ | Higher = better compression |
| **Apriori candidate count** | $O(2^d)$ for $d$ items | Exponential blowup |
| **FP-Growth tree size** | Bounded by sum of frequent pattern occurrences | Compact by design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** FP-Growth is widely deployed in **market-basket analysis, cross-selling engines, web log mining, bioinformatics (motif discovery), intrusion detection systems**, and **recommendation systems**. Companies like *Amazon, Flipkart, and Netflix* use variants of FP-Growth to find association patterns in petabytes of clickstream data.

## 2.4 Why FP-Growth Outperforms Apriori

| Property | Apriori | FP-Growth |
|---|---|---|
| **Database scans** | Multiple (one per iteration) | **2 scans only** |
| **Candidate generation** | Yes (very expensive) | **No candidates** |
| **Memory usage** | High (stores all candidates) | Compact (FP-Tree shares prefixes) |
| **Time complexity** | $O(N \cdot 2^d)$ worst case | $O(N \cdot w)$ amortized, $w$ = tree width |
| **Candidate explosion** | Yes at low support | Avoided entirely |
| **Scalability** | Poor for large DBs | **Highly scalable** |

The crucial advantage: **no candidate generation** means the algorithm avoids the exponential blowup that plagues Apriori when support threshold is low or database is dense.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Manual FP-Tree Construction

**Given Transaction Database $D$** (5 transactions):

| TID | Items Bought |
|---|---|
| T1 | f, a, c, d, g, i, m, p |
| T2 | a, b, c, f, l, m, o |
| T3 | b, f, h, j, o |
| T4 | b, c, k, s, p |
| T5 | a, c, e, f, l, m, n, p |

**Parameters:** $\text{min\_support} = 60\%$ → min count $= 0.6 \times 5 = 3$.

### Step 1 — First Database Scan (Support Count)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma(f) &= 4, \quad \sigma(c) = 4, \quad \sigma(a) = 3, \quad \sigma(b) = 3, \quad \sigma(m) = 3, \quad \sigma(p) = 3 \\
\sigma(l) &= 2, \quad \sigma(o) = 2, \quad \sigma(d) = 1, \quad \sigma(g) = 1, \quad \sigma(i) = 1 \\
\sigma(j) &= 1, \quad \sigma(h) = 1, \quad \sigma(k) = 1, \quad \sigma(s) = 1, \quad \sigma(e) = 1, \quad \sigma(n) = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Filter Infrequent Items (Keep σ ≥ 3)

After removing items with support $< 3$, the frequent items in **descending support order** are:

$$
L = [\ (f:4),\ (c:4),\ (a:3),\ (b:3),\ (m:3),\ (p:3)\ ]
$$

### Step 3 — Filter and Reorder Each Transaction

For each transaction, remove infrequent items and sort the remaining items according to $L$:

| TID | Original | Filtered & Sorted |
|---|---|---|
| T1 | f,a,c,d,g,i,m,p | f, c, a, m, p |
| T2 | a,b,c,f,l,m,o | f, c, a, b, m |
| T3 | b,f,h,j,o | f, b |
| T4 | b,c,k,s,p | c, b, p |
| T5 | a,c,e,f,l,m,n,p | f, c, a, m, p |

### Step 4 — FP-Tree Construction (Insertion Sequence)

Reading T1 `f, c, a, m, p`: creates the path `f → c → a → m → p`, each with count 1.

Reading T2 `f, c, a, b, m`: shares the prefix `f, c, a` with T1. Increment counts of f, c, a to 2; create new branch for b (count 1) → m (count 1).

Reading T3 `f, b`: shares only `f` with existing tree. Increment f to 3; create new branch `b (count 1)`.

Reading T4 `c, b, p`: no shared prefix from root. New branch from root: `c (count 1) → b (count 1) → p (count 1)`.

Reading T5 `f, c, a, m, p`: matches the full T1 path. Increment f→4, c→4, a→3, m→3, p→3.

**Final Node Counts:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Path from T1/T2/T5:}\ & f:4 \to c:4 \to a:3 \to m:3 \to p:3 \quad \text{and} \quad a:3 \to b:1 \to m:1 \\
\text{Path from T3:}\ & f:4 \to b:1 \\
\text{Path from T4:}\ & c:1 \to b:1 \to p:1
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Mining Conditional Pattern Base (for Item p)

The **conditional pattern base** of $p$ consists of all prefix paths reaching nodes labeled $p$:

$$
\text{CPB}(p) = \{\ (f:3, c:3, a:3, m:3),\ (c:1, b:1)\ \}
$$

Counts of items in CPB(p): $\sigma(f)=3, \sigma(c)=4, \sigma(a)=3, \sigma(m)=3, \sigma(b)=1$.

With min_count = 3, only $\{f, c, a, m\}$ qualify. The **conditional FP-Tree of p** is a single path:

$$
f:3 \to c:3 \to a:3 \to m:3
$$

**Generated frequent patterns ending in p** (all subsets of the path ∪ {p}):

$$
\{fp,\ cp,\ ap,\ mp,\ fcp,\ acp,\ amp,\ famp,\ fcap,\ fcmp,\ camp,\ facp,\ fcpm,\ fcam,\ famp,\ facmp\}
$$

Patterns with support $\geq 3$ are: $\{fp, cp, ap, mp, fcp, acp, famp, facp, fcmp, facmp, fcamp\}$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Recursive Mining Outcome

Using the full recursion across all items in the header table, the **complete set of frequent itemsets** mined by FP-Growth on the sample database is:

| Item (suffix) | Conditional Pattern Base | Conditional FP-Tree | Frequent Patterns Generated |
|---|---|---|---|
| p | (fcam:3), (cb:1) | (f:3, c:3, a:3, m:3) | {fp, cp, ap, mp, fcp, fap, famp, facp, facmp, …} |
| m | (fcab:1), (fca:3), (c:1) | (f:4, c:4, a:3) | {fm, cm, am, fcm, fam, facm, fcam} |
| b | (fca:1), (f:1), (c:1) | ∅ (all < 3) | {b} alone |
| a | (fc:3) | (f:3, c:3) | {fa, ca, fca} |
| c | (f:4) | (f:4) | {fc} |
| f | — | — | {f} |

## 3.3 Python Implementation — FP-Growth from Scratch

```python
# fp_growth.py
# KTU PCADL407 - Module 2 Experiment: FP-Growth Algorithm

class TreeNode:
    """Node of the FP-Tree."""
    def __init__(self, name, count, parent):
        self.name = name
        self.count = count
        self.parent = parent
        self.children = {}
        self.node_link = None  # Link to next node with same item

    def increment(self, count):
        self.count += count

    def display(self, ind=1):
        print('  ' * ind, self.name, ' ', self.count)
        for child in self.children.values():
            child.display(ind + 1)


def create_init_set(dataset):
    """Convert transaction list to dict-of-frozensets format."""
    ret = {}
    for trans in dataset:
        ret.setdefault(frozenset(trans), 0)
        ret[frozenset(trans)] += 1
    return ret


def update_header(node_to_test, target_node):
    """Append target_node to the end of the node-link chain."""
    while node_to_test.node_link is not None:
        node_to_test = node_to_test.node_link
    node_to_test.node_link = target_node


def update_tree(items, in_tree, header_table, count):
    """Insert a transaction's items into the FP-Tree."""
    if items[0] in in_tree.children:
        in_tree.children[items[0]].increment(count)
    else:
        in_tree.children[items[0]] = TreeNode(items[0], count, in_tree)
        if header_table[items[0]][1] is None:
            header_table[items[0]][1] = in_tree.children[items[0]]
        else:
            update_header(header_table[items[0]][1],
                          in_tree.children[items[0]])
    if len(items) > 1:
        update_tree(items[1:], in_tree.children[items[0]],
                    header_table, count)


def create_tree(dataset, min_sup=2):
    """Build the FP-Tree from dataset."""
    header_table = {}
    for trans in dataset:
        for item in trans:
            header_table[item] = header_table.get(item, 0) + dataset[trans]
    # Filter and sort
    for k in list(header_table):
        if header_table[k] < min_sup:
            del header_table[k]
    freq_item_set = set(header_table.keys())
    if len(freq_item_set) == 0:
        return None, None
    for k in header_table:
        header_table[k] = [header_table[k], None]
    ret_tree = TreeNode('Null Set', 1, None)
    for tran_set, count in dataset.items():
        local_data = {}
        for item in tran_set:
            if item in freq_item_set:
                local_data[item] = header_table[item][0]
        if len(local_data) > 0:
            ordered = [v[0] for v in sorted(
                local_data.items(), key=lambda p: p[1], reverse=True)]
            update_tree(ordered, ret_tree, header_table, count)
    return ret_tree, header_table


def ascend_tree(leaf_node, prefix_path):
    """Trace path from leaf to root."""
    if leaf_node.parent is not None:
        prefix_path.append(leaf_node.name)
        ascend_tree(leaf_node.parent, prefix_path)


def find_prefix_path(base_pat, tree_node):
    """Find all prefix paths ending at tree_node."""
    cond_pats = {}
    while tree_node is not None:
        prefix_path = []
        ascend_tree(tree_node, prefix_path)
        if len(prefix_path) > 1:
            cond_pats[frozenset(prefix_path[1:])] = tree_node.count
        tree_node = tree_node.node_link
    return cond_pats


def mine_tree(in_tree, header_table, min_sup, prefix, freq_item_list):
    """Recursively mine frequent patterns from the FP-Tree."""
    big_l = [v[0] for v in sorted(header_table.items(), key=lambda p: p[1][0])]
    for base_pat in big_l:
        new_freq_set = prefix.copy()
        new_freq_set.add(base_pat)
        freq_item_list.append(new_freq_set)
        cond_pat_bases = find_prefix_path(base_pat, header_table[base_pat][1])
        my_cond_tree, my_head = create_tree(cond_pat_bases, min_sup)
        if my_head is not None:
            mine_tree(my_cond_tree, my_head, min_sup, new_freq_set, freq_item_list)


# ------------------- DRIVER CODE -------------------
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Sample transactional dataset
    dataset = [
        ['f', 'a', 'c', 'd', 'g', 'i', 'm', 'p'],
        ['a', 'b', 'c', 'f', 'l', 'm', 'o'],
        ['b', 'f', 'h', 'j', 'o'],
        ['b', 'c', 'k', 's', 'p'],
        ['a', 'c', 'e', 'f', 'l', 'm', 'n', 'p']
    ]

    init_set = create_init_set(dataset)
    min_support = 3
    fp_tree, header_tab = create_tree(init_set, min_support)

    print("======= FP-Tree (rooted display) =======")
    fp_tree.display()

    freq_items = []
    mine_tree(fp_tree, header_tab, min_support, set([]), freq_items)

    print("\n======= Frequent Itemsets Found =======")
    for itemset in freq_items:
        print(itemset)
```

**Expected Output (abridged):**

```
======= FP-Tree (rooted display) =======
   Null Set   1
     f   4
       c   4
         a   3
           m   3
             p   3
           b   1
             m   1
       b   1
     c   1
       b   1
         p   1
```

> [!TIP]
> **Verification Tip:** The total count of each item in the tree should equal the support count from the first scan. For example, total count of `f` across the tree = 4, matching $\sigma(f) = 4$.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level FP-Growth Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Transaction Database D] --> B[Scan 1: Count Support of All 1-Itemsets]
    B --> C[Filter Items with Support less than min_support]
    C --> D[Sort Frequent Items Descending by Support]
    D --> E[Initialize Empty FP-Tree with Root 'Null Set']
    E --> F[Scan 2: For Each Transaction]
    F --> G{Item already in current path?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Increment Count of Shared Nodes]
    G -- No --> I[Create New Branch with Count 1]
    H --> J[Update Header Table Node-Links]
    I --> J
    J --> K{All Transactions Inserted?}
    K -- No --> F
    K -- Yes --> L[FP-Tree Construction Complete]
    L --> M[Initialize Mining Process]
    M --> N[For Each Item in Header Table Ascending]
    N --> O[Build Conditional Pattern Base CPB]
    O --> P[Construct Conditional FP-Tree from CPB]
    P --> Q{Conditional Tree Empty?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Output Item Alone as Frequent]
    Q -- No --> S{Conditional Tree is Single Path?}
    S -- Yes --> T[Generate All Combinations with Suffix]
    S -- No --> U[Recurse: mine_tree on Conditional Tree]
    T --> V[Append to Frequent Itemset List]
    U --> V
    R --> V
    V --> W{More Items in Header?}
    W -- Yes --> N
    W -- No --> X[Return Complete Frequent Itemset List]
    X --> Y[End]
```

## 4.2 FP-Tree Data Structure (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HeaderTable[Header Table]
        direction TB
        H1["Frequent Item: f, Support: 4"]
        H2["Frequent Item: c, Support: 4"]
        H3["Frequent Item: a, Support: 3"]
        H4["Frequent Item: b, Support: 3"]
        H5["Frequent Item: m, Support: 3"]
        H6["Frequent Item: p, Support: 3"]
    end

    subgraph FPTree[FP-Tree Rooted at Null Set]
        direction TB
        R["Null Set"]
        N1["f:4"]
        N2["c:4"]
        N3["a:3"]
        N4["m:3"]
        N5["p:3"]
        N6["b:1"]
        N7["m:1"]
        N8["b:1"]
        N9["c:1"]
        N10["b:1"]
        N11["p:1"]
    end

    H1 -.node_link.-> N1
    H2 -.node_link.-> N2
    H3 -.node_link.-> N3
    H4 -.node_link.-> N6
    H5 -.node_link.-> N4
    H6 -.node_link.-> N5

    R --> N1
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> N3
    N3 --> N4
    N4 --> N5
    N3 --> N6
    N6 --> N7
    N1 --> N8
    R --> N9
    N9 --> N10
    N10 --> N11
```

## 4.3 Recursive Mining Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Mine FP-Tree with Header Items]
    Start --> Loop{For each suffix item a in ascending support}
    Loop --> CPB[Extract Conditional Pattern Base of a]
    CPB --> CondTree[Build Conditional FP-Tree of a]
    CondTree --> Empty{Tree Empty?}
    Empty -- Yes --> Skip[Skip: no extensions of a are frequent]
    Empty -- No --> SinglePath{Single Path?}
    SinglePath -- Yes --> Comb[Generate All Combinations along Path with a]
    SinglePath -- No --> Recurse[Recurse: mine_tree on Conditional FP-Tree]
    Comb --> Record[Record Frequent Itemsets]
    Recurse --> Record
    Skip --> Record
    Record --> Loop
    Loop --> Done[All suffixes processed: output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid:** Each node in the diagram shows the item name and a colon-separated count. The dotted arrows (`.->`) represent **node-link pointers** maintained through the Header Table to chain all nodes carrying the same item — this is what makes the mining phase linear in tree size.
