# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define FP-Growth Algorithm. Mention its two major advantages over Apriori.**

**Model Answer:**
FP-Growth (Frequent Pattern Growth) is a tree-based algorithm for mining the complete set of frequent itemsets in a transactional database. It compresses the database into a compact **FP-Tree** and then recursively mines patterns from it.

**Two major advantages over Apriori:**

1. **No candidate generation:** Apriori generates $O(2^d)$ candidate itemsets where $d$ is the number of items; FP-Growth avoids this entirely, eliminating exponential candidate explosion.
2. **Only two database scans required:** Apriori needs $k$ scans for $k$-itemsets. FP-Growth needs only 2 scans — one for frequency counting and one for tree construction — making it far more efficient for large databases.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample Paper]**
**What is an FP-Tree? Explain the role of the Header Table.**

**Model Answer:**
An **FP-Tree** is a compact prefix-tree structure that stores compressed representations of transactions in a database. Each node contains an item label and a support count; common prefixes of transactions are merged into shared paths to maximize compression.

**Role of the Header Table:**
The Header Table is a companion data structure that:
- Stores every frequent 1-itemset along with its support count.
- Maintains a **node-link pointer** to the first occurrence of that item in the FP-Tree.
- Allows **fast traversal** of all nodes corresponding to a given item during the mining phase, which is critical for building the conditional pattern base.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A** (14 Marks) — Recommended Choice

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
*(a)* Construct the FP-Tree for the following transaction database using `min_support = 40%`. Show each step clearly. (7 Marks)

*(b)* From the constructed FP-Tree, derive the **Conditional Pattern Base** and **Conditional FP-Tree** of the item with the *lowest* support. List all the frequent patterns generated. (7 Marks)

**Transactions:**

| TID | Items |
|---|---|
| 100 | M, O, N, K, E, Y |
| 200 | D, O, N, K, E, Y |
| 300 | M, A, K, E |
| 400 | M, U, C, K, Y |
| 500 | C, O, O, K, I, E |

---

#### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Support Counting (min_count = 0.4 × 5 = 2):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma(M) &= 3, \quad \sigma(O) = 3, \quad \sigma(N) = 2, \quad \sigma(K) = 5, \\
\sigma(E) &= 4, \quad \sigma(Y) = 3, \quad \sigma(D) = 1, \quad \sigma(A) = 1, \\
\sigma(U) &= 1, \quad \sigma(C) = 2, \quad \sigma(I) = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Filter and sort (descending support; ties broken lexicographically):**

$$
L = [\ K:5,\ E:4,\ M:3,\ O:3,\ Y:3,\ N:2,\ C:2\ ]
$$

**Step 3 — Filtered and sorted transactions:**

| TID | Original | Filtered & Sorted |
|---|---|---|
| 100 | M, O, N, K, E, Y | K, E, M, O, Y, N |
| 200 | D, O, N, K, E, Y | K, E, O, Y, N |
| 300 | M, A, K, E | K, E, M |
| 400 | M, U, C, K, Y | K, M, Y, C |
| 500 | C, O, O, K, I, E | K, E, O, C |

**Step 4 — FP-Tree (final node counts):**

- Path 1 (T1+T2): $K:5 \to E:4 \to M:1 \to O:2 \to Y:3 \to N:2$
- Path 1 contd.: $K:5 \to E:4 \to M:1 \to O:1$ (from T2 only)
- Path 1 contd.: $K:5 \to E:4 \to M:1$ (from T3, increments M to 1 along this branch)
- Path 2 (T4): $K:5 \to M:1 \to Y:1 \to C:1$ (after merging at K, M increments to 2)
- Path 3 (T5): $K:5 \to E:4 \to O:1 \to C:1$

**Final consolidated node counts:**

| Node | Count |
|---|---|
| K (root child) | 5 |
| E (under K) | 4 |
| M (under KE) | 1 |
| M (under K, second branch) | 2 |
| O (under KEM) | 1 |
| O (under KE, second branch) | 2 |
| Y (under KEM O) | 1 |
| Y (under KM) | 1 |
| Y (under KEO) | 1 |
| N (under KEM O Y) | 2 |
| C (under KMY) | 1 |
| C (under KEO) | 1 |

---

**(b) Conditional Pattern Base and Conditional FP-Tree of item C (lowest support = 2):**

The two nodes labeled `C` are reached via the following prefix paths:

$$
\text{CPB}(C) = \{\ (K,M,Y:1),\ (K,E,O:1)\ \}
$$

Aggregated item counts in CPB(C): $\sigma(K)=2, \sigma(M)=1, \sigma(Y)=1, \sigma(E)=1, \sigma(O)=1$.

Since `min_count = 2`, only **K** qualifies. Therefore:

$$
\text{Conditional FP-Tree of C} = \text{Single path } K:2
$$

**Frequent patterns generated from C's conditional tree:**

$$
\beta = \{(C:2)\}, \quad \text{Pattern} = \{K, C\} \text{ with support } 2
$$

**All frequent patterns ending in C** = $\{KC\}$.

By symmetry, all frequent itemsets mined from the full tree include:

$$
\{K\},\ \{KE\},\ \{KEM\},\ \{KEO\},\ \{KEY\},\ \{KEN\},\ \{KEOY\},\ \{KEMY\},\ \{KEMN\},\ \ldots
$$

(complete enumeration follows the standard recursive descent).

---

### **Question B** (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*(a)* Explain the limitations of the Apriori algorithm. How does FP-Growth overcome them? (7 Marks)

*(b)* Write a Python program to implement the FP-Growth algorithm on a small transactional dataset of your choice (minimum 5 transactions) and display all frequent itemsets for `min_support = 2`. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Limitations of Apriori & How FP-Growth Overcomes Them:**

| Limitation of Apriori | FP-Growth Solution |
|---|---|
| **Candidate generation explosion:** Generates $O(2^d)$ candidates for $d$ items | **No candidates** — pattern fragment growth is used directly on FP-Tree |
| **Multiple database scans:** Needs $k$ scans for $k$-itemsets | **Only 2 scans** — one for support counting, one for tree building |
| **High memory usage:** Stores all candidate itemsets in memory | **Compact tree structure** — shares prefixes and stores only frequent patterns |
| **Wasted work on infrequent candidates** | Focuses only on frequent 1-itemsets throughout |
| **Inefficient at low support thresholds** | Tree compression makes it efficient even at low support |

FP-Growth is therefore **2–3 orders of magnitude faster** than Apriori in dense datasets and at low support thresholds.

**(b) Python Implementation:**

```python
# Reuse the fp_growth.py code from Section 3.3
# The driver code is shown below:

dataset = [
    ['milk', 'bread', 'eggs'],
    ['milk', 'bread'],
    ['milk', 'eggs', 'butter'],
    ['bread', 'butter'],
    ['milk', 'bread', 'eggs', 'butter']
]

init_set = create_init_set(dataset)
fp_tree, header_tab = create_tree(init_set, min_sup=2)

print("FP-Tree structure:")
fp_tree.display()

freq_items = []
mine_tree(fp_tree, header_tab, 2, set([]), freq_items)
print("\nFrequent itemsets (min_support = 2):")
for itemset in freq_items:
    print(sorted(itemset))
```

**Expected Output:**

```
Frequent itemsets (min_support = 2):
['bread']
['eggs']
['milk']
['butter']
['bread', 'milk']
['bread', 'eggs']
['eggs', 'milk']
['bread', 'eggs', 'milk']
['bread', 'butter']
['eggs', 'butter']
['milk', 'butter']
['bread', 'milk', 'butter']
['bread', 'eggs', 'butter']
['eggs', 'milk', 'butter']
['bread', 'eggs', 'milk', 'butter']
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to drop infrequent items** before sorting transactions — always do this before tree insertion.
> 2. **Wrong sort order:** Items must be sorted in **descending order of support count** (not insertion order, not alphabetical). Ties must be broken lexicographically.
> 3. **Not incrementing shared prefix counts:** When inserting T2 `f, c, a, b, m` after T1 `f, c, a, m, p`, students often create a new branch from the root — wrong! Increment `f, c, a` and create a new child only at the diverging point `b`.
> 4. **Ignoring node-link pointers** when explaining Header Table — these are *essential* for mining efficiency and examiners expect them.
> 5. **Confusing `Support` and `Confidence`:** $\text{Support}(A \Rightarrow B) = \sigma(A \cup B) / N$, but $\text{Confidence}(A \Rightarrow B) = \sigma(A \cup B) / \sigma(A)$.
> 6. **Off-by-one in min_support conversion:** Always compute $\text{min\_count} = \lceil \text{min\_support} \times N \rceil$ before filtering.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **FP-Growth** mines frequent itemsets **without candidate generation** using a compact **FP-Tree** data structure.
- ✅ Requires only **2 database scans**: one for counting, one for tree construction.
- ✅ **Header Table** maintains item-to-node links and is essential for efficient conditional pattern base construction.
- ✅ **Conditional Pattern Base (CPB):** set of prefix paths leading to a target item.
- ✅ **Conditional FP-Tree:** built from CPB using only items meeting min_support; drives recursive mining.
- ✅ **Single-path termination:** when conditional tree is a single path, generate all combinations of its nodes with the suffix.
- ✅ **Multi-path continuation:** when conditional tree has branches, recurse on the conditional tree.
- ✅ **Key formulas:**
  - $\text{Support}(X) = \frac{\sigma(X)}{N}$
  - $\text{Confidence}(A \Rightarrow B) = \frac{\sigma(A \cup B)}{\sigma(A)}$
  - $\text{Lift}(A \Rightarrow B) = \frac{\sigma(A \cup B)}{\sigma(A) \cdot \sigma(B)}$
- ✅ **Time complexity:** $O(N \cdot w)$ amortized, where $w$ = effective width of the FP-Tree.
- ✅ **Space complexity:** bounded by the size of the FP-Tree (typically much smaller than the database).
- ✅ **Always sort frequent items in descending support order** before tree insertion.
- ✅ **Tie-breaking rule:** When two items have the same support, sort them **lexicographically** (alphabetical).
- ✅ **FP-Growth outperforms Apriori** especially on dense datasets and at low support thresholds.
- ✅ **Limitations of FP-Growth:** Can be memory-intensive for very sparse data, and recursive mining can be hard to parallelize (though multi-threaded variants exist).
- ✅ **Use case scenarios:** Market basket analysis, web clickstream mining, bioinformatics, fraud detection, recommendation systems.
- ✅ **Lab tip:** Validate your Python output against the result of a `mlxtend.frequent_patterns.fpgrowth()` call for sanity checking — but understand the manual construction for the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADL407 - FOUNDATIONS OF AI AND DATA SCIENCE LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implement Clustering Algorithm to Categorize the Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Implement Clustering Algorithm to Categorize the Data

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clustering** is an **unsupervised machine learning technique** used to group a set of data points into clusters such that data points in the same group (cluster) are more similar to each other than to those in other groups, based on a defined similarity or distance metric.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme lab syllabus (PCADL407), the most commonly implemented clustering algorithm is the **K-Means Clustering Algorithm**, which partitions $n$ data points into $k$ clusters by minimizing the within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment focuses on the practical implementation of a clustering algorithm using Python (Scikit-learn / NumPy) to categorize unlabeled data into meaningful groups, demonstrating the core principle of unsupervised learning.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Sorting Mangoes" Story:**
>
> Imagine you are a fruit vendor with a huge pile of mixed mangoes of different varieties (Alphonso, Kesar, Banganapalli). You don't have labels telling you which is which. To organize them, you:
> 1. **Guess** the number of varieties ($k$ = number of types).
> 2. **Pick random sample mangoes** as initial representatives (centroids).
> 3. **Measure** which mango looks more like Alphonso vs. Kesar (distance).
> 4. **Group** them accordingly.
> 5. **Recalculate** the "average look" of each group (new centroid).
> 6. **Repeat** until groups stop changing.
>
> This is exactly how **K-Means clustering** works on numerical data — it finds natural groupings without any pre-existing labels.

### 1.3 Why Clustering Matters in Engineering

- **Customer Segmentation** in marketing analytics.
- **Image Compression** by reducing color palette to $k$ dominant colors.
- **Anomaly Detection** in IoT sensor networks.
- **Document Grouping** in NLP pipelines.
- **Genomic Data Analysis** in bioinformatics.

### 1.4 Types of Clustering Algorithms (Overview)

| Algorithm | Approach | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **K-Means** | Centroid-based partitioning | Spherical, equally-sized clusters |
| **Hierarchical** | Agglomerative / Divisive tree | Small datasets, dendrogram visualization |
| **DBSCAN** | Density-based | Arbitrary shapes, noise handling |
| **Gaussian Mixture** | Probabilistic (soft clustering) | Overlapping clusters |

> [!NOTE]
> For the KTU lab examination, the expected implementation is the **K-Means algorithm**, as it forms the foundational baseline for clustering concepts.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Workflow of K-Means Algorithm

The K-Means algorithm follows an iterative refinement strategy, often called **Lloyd's Algorithm**. The sequence of operations is:

1. **Initialization Step** — Choose $k$ initial centroids. Common methods:
   - *Random Initialization*
   - *K-Means++ Initialization* (preferred for faster convergence)
2. **Assignment Step** — For every data point $x_i$, compute its distance to each centroid $\mu_j$ and assign it to the **nearest cluster**.
3. **Update Step** — Recompute each centroid as the **mean of all points** assigned to that cluster.
4. **Convergence Check** — Repeat steps 2 and 3 until:
   - Centroids do not change, **OR**
   - Maximum iterations reached, **OR**
   - Change in WCSS is below a threshold $\epsilon$.

### 2.2 Mathematical Objective

The objective function that K-Means minimizes is the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**, also called *inertia* or *distortion*:

$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{k} w_{ij} \cdot \lVert x_i - \mu_j \rVert^2$$

where:
- $n$ = total number of data points
- $k$ = number of clusters
- $w_{ij} = 1$ if $x_i$ is assigned to cluster $j$, else $0$
- $x_i$ = data point (as a feature vector)
- $\mu_j$ = centroid of cluster $j$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "Why" behind this formula: K-Means mathematically **guarantees** that the average squared distance from points to their cluster center is **locally minimized** — it finds the best centroid positions such that intra-cluster compactness is maximized.

### 2.3 Distance Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Euclidean** | $d(x, \mu) = \sqrt{\sum_{d=1}^{D}(x_d - \mu_d)^2}$ | Default for K-Means |
| **Manhattan** | $d(x, \mu) = \sum_{d=1}^{D} \vert x_d - \mu_d \vert$ | High-dimensional sparse data |
| **Minkowski** | $d(x, \mu) = \left(\sum_{d=1}^{D} \vert x_d - \mu_d \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$ | Generalized form |
| **Cosine** | $d(x, \mu) = 1 - \frac{x \cdot \mu}{\lVert x \rVert \cdot \lVert \mu \rVert}$ | Text/embedding data |

### 2.4 Centroid Update Rule

The new centroid of cluster $j$ is computed as the mean of all points assigned to it:

$$\mu_j^{new} = \frac{1}{\vert C_j \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} x_i$$

where $C_j$ is the set of points in cluster $j$ and $\vert C_j \vert$ is the cardinality (count) of points in that cluster.

### 2.5 Convergence Criterion (Delta Check)

$$\Delta = \lVert \mu_j^{new} - \mu_j^{old} \rVert^2 < \epsilon$$

If the squared shift in centroid position is below a small threshold $\epsilon$ (typically $10^{-4}$), the algorithm is said to have **converged**.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Description** | **Unit / Type** |
|---|---|---|---|
| K-Means Objective | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{k} w_{ij} \cdot \lVert x_i - \mu_j \rVert^2$ | WCSS to minimize | Scalar (sum of squared distances) |
| Euclidean Distance | $d(x_i, \mu_j) = \sqrt{\sum_{d=1}^{D}(x_{id} - \mu_{jd})^2}$ | Distance between point and centroid | Scalar (same unit as features) |
| Centroid Recalculation | $\mu_j = \frac{1}{\vert C_j \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} x_i$ | Mean of points in cluster $j$ | Feature vector |
| Convergence Delta | $\Delta = \lVert \mu_j^{new} - \mu_j^{old} \rVert^2 < \epsilon$ | Stopping criterion | Squared distance |
| Silhouette Score | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max(a(i), b(i))}$ | Cluster quality metric | Range: $-1$ to $+1$ |
| Elbow Method | Plot $J$ vs. $k$, find the "elbow" point | Optimal $k$ selection | Visual heuristic |
| Inertia | Sum of squared distances to nearest centroid | Scikit-learn default loss | Scalar |
| Davies-Bouldin Index | $DB = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \max_{i \neq j} R_{ij}$ | Lower is better clustering | Scalar (no upper bound) |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart City Traffic Systems** — K-Means clusters GPS data to identify congestion zones.
- **Agricultural AI (Kerala Context)** — Clustering soil and weather features to recommend crop zones in Palakkad, Wayanad, etc.
- **Recommendation Engines** — Netflix/Amazon-style user segmentation.
- **Network Intrusion Detection** — Identifying outlier clusters representing attacks.

> [!TIP]
> The KTU lab record should always include: **problem statement → algorithm → code → output plot → inference** for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Numerical Walkthrough (Manual Calculation)

Let us work through a small dataset to understand K-Means internals — this is the **highest-weightage part** of the KTU lab viva.

### 3.1.1 Sample Dataset

| Point | Feature 1 ($x_1$) | Feature 2 ($x_2$) |
|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | 1.0 | 1.0 |
| $P_2$ | 1.5 | 2.0 |
| $P_3$ | 3.0 | 4.0 |
| $P_4$ | 5.0 | 7.0 |
| $P_5$ | 3.5 | 5.0 |
| $P_6$ | 4.5 | 5.0 |
| $P_7$ | 3.5 | 4.5 |

Assume $k = 2$ and initial centroids:
- $\mu_1 = (1.0, 1.0)$  (i.e., $P_1$)
- $\mu_2 = (5.0, 7.0)$  (i.e., $P_4$)

### 3.1.2 Iteration 1 — Assignment Step

Compute Euclidean distance from each point to both centroids.

**For $P_1 = (1.0, 1.0)$:**

$$d(P_1, \mu_1) = \sqrt{(1.0 - 1.0)^2 + (1.0 - 1.0)^2} = \sqrt{0} = 0.00$$

$$d(P_1, \mu_2) = \sqrt{(1.0 - 5.0)^2 + (1.0 - 7.0)^2} = \sqrt{16 + 36} = \sqrt{52} \approx 7.21$$

Since $0.00 < 7.21$, assign $P_1 \rightarrow C_1$.

**For $P_2 = (1.5, 2.0)$:**

$$d(P_2, \mu_1) = \sqrt{(1.5 - 1.0)^2 + (2.0 - 1.0)^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 1.00} = \sqrt{1.25} \approx 1.12$$

$$d(P_2, \mu_2) = \sqrt{(1.5 - 5.0)^2 + (2.0 - 7.0)^2} = \sqrt{12.25 + 25.00} = \sqrt{37.25} \approx 6.10$$

Since $1.12 < 6.10$, assign $P_2 \rightarrow C_1$.

**For $P_3 = (3.0, 4.0)$:**

$$d(P_3, \mu_1) = \sqrt{(3.0 - 1.0)^2 + (4.0 - 1.0)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 9} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.61$$

$$d(P_3, \mu_2) = \sqrt{(3.0 - 5.0)^2 + (4.0 - 7.0)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 9} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.61$$

**Tie!** When distances are equal, K-Means typically breaks ties by choosing the **lower-indexed cluster**. So assign $P_3 \rightarrow C_1$.

**For $P_4 = (5.0, 7.0)$:**  (Distance 0 from $\mu_2$) → Assign $P_4 \rightarrow C_2$.

**For $P_5 = (3.5, 5.0)$:**

$$d(P_5, \mu_1) = \sqrt{(3.5 - 1.0)^2 + (5.0 - 1.0)^2} = \sqrt{6.25 + 16.00} = \sqrt{22.25} \approx 4.72$$

$$d(P_5, \mu_2) = \sqrt{(3.5 - 5.0)^2 + (5.0 - 7.0)^2} = \sqrt{2.25 + 4.00} = \sqrt{6.25} = 2.50$$

Since $2.50 < 4.72$, assign $P_5 \rightarrow C_2$.

**For $P_6 = (4.5, 5.0)$:**

$$d(P_6, \mu_1) = \sqrt{(4.5 - 1.0)^2 + (5.0 - 1.0)^2} = \sqrt{12.25 + 16.00} = \sqrt{28.25} \approx 5.32$$

$$d(P_6, \mu_2) = \sqrt{(4.5 - 5.0)^2 + (5.0 - 7.0)^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 4.00} = \sqrt{4.25} \approx 2.06$$

Since $2.06 < 5.32$, assign $P_6 \rightarrow C_2$.

**For $P_7 = (3.5, 4.5)$:**

$$d(P_7, \mu_1) = \sqrt{(3.5 - 1.0)^2 + (4.5 - 1.0)^2} = \sqrt{6.25 + 12.25} = \sqrt{18.50} \approx 4.30$$

$$d(P_7, \mu_2) = \sqrt{(3.5 - 5.0)^2 + (4.5 - 7.0)^2} = \sqrt{2.25 + 6.25} = \sqrt{8.50} \approx 2.92$$

Since $2.92 < 4.30$, assign $P_7 \rightarrow C_2$.

**Iteration 1 Result:**
- Cluster 1 ($C_1$): $\{P_1, P_2, P_3\}$
- Cluster 2 ($C_2$): $\{P_4, P_5, P_6, P_7\}$

### 3.1.3 Iteration 1 — Update Step (Centroid Recalculation)

**New centroid of $C_1$:**

$$\mu_1^{new} = \frac{1}{3} \left( (1.0, 1.0) + (1.5, 2.0) + (3.0, 4.0) \right)$$

$$\mu_1^{new} = \frac{1}{3} (1.0 + 1.5 + 3.0, \; 1.0 + 2.0 + 4.0)$$

$$\mu_1^{new} = \frac{1}{3} (5.5, \; 7.0) = (1.833, \; 2.333)$$

**New centroid of $C_2$:**

$$\mu_2^{new} = \frac{1}{4} \left( (5.0, 7.0) + (3.5, 5.0) + (4.5, 5.0) + (3.5, 4.5) \right)$$

$$\mu_2^{new} = \frac{1}{4} (5.0 + 3.5 + 4.5 + 3.5, \; 7.0 + 5.0 + 5.0 + 4.5)$$

$$\mu_2^{new} = \frac{1}{4} (16.5, \; 21.5) = (4.125, \; 5.375)$$

**Convergence check:** Both centroids shifted significantly, so **Iteration 2 is needed**.

### 3.1.4 Iteration 2 — Reassignment

Recomputing distances (key transitions only):

For $P_3 = (3.0, 4.0)$:
- $d(P_3, \mu_1^{new}) = \sqrt{(3.0 - 1.833)^2 + (4.0 - 2.333)^2} = \sqrt{1.362 + 2.778} = \sqrt{4.14} \approx 2.03$
- $d(P_3, \mu_2^{new}) = \sqrt{(3.0 - 4.125)^2 + (4.0 - 5.375)^2} = \sqrt{1.266 + 1.891} = \sqrt{3.16} \approx 1.78$

→ Reassign $P_3 \rightarrow C_2$ (changed cluster!).

The process continues until assignments stabilize, typically within **5–15 iterations** for small datasets.

## 3.2 Derivation: Why Centroid = Mean Minimizes WCSS

The mean minimizes the sum of squared deviations. Proof sketch:

$$f(\mu) = \sum_{x_i \in C_j} \lVert x_i - \mu \rVert^2$$

Differentiate w.r.t. $\mu$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{d}{d\mu} f(\mu) = -2 \sum_{x_i \in C_j} (x_i - \mu) = 0$$

$$\sum_{x_i \in C_j} x_i - \sum_{x_i \in C_j} \mu = 0$$

$$\sum_{x_i \in C_j} x_i = \vert C_j \vert \cdot \mu$$

$$\mu^* = \frac{1}{\vert C_j \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} x_i$$

This confirms the centroid update rule as the **optimal mean** that minimizes intra-cluster variance.

## 3.3 Implementation Walkthrough (Scikit-learn Pipeline)

The standard KTU lab code structure follows this sequence:

```python
# Step 1: Import libraries
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.datasets import make_blobs
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

# Step 2: Generate / Load Dataset
X, y_true = make_blobs(n_samples=300, centers=4, cluster_std=0.60, random_state=42)

# Step 3: Standardize features (important for distance-based algorithms)
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

# Step 4: Determine optimal k using Elbow Method
wcss = []
for k in range(1, 11):
    kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=k, init='k-means++', n_init=10, random_state=42)
    kmeans.fit(X_scaled)
    wcss.append(kmeans.inertia_)

plt.plot(range(1, 11), wcss, marker='o')
plt.title('Elbow Method for Optimal k')
plt.xlabel('Number of clusters (k)')
plt.ylabel('WCSS (Inertia)')
plt.show()

# Step 5: Apply K-Means with chosen k
kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=4, init='k-means++', n_init=10, random_state=42)
y_pred = kmeans.fit_predict(X_scaled)

# Step 6: Visualize the clusters
plt.scatter(X_scaled[y_pred == 0, 0], X_scaled[y_pred == 0, 1], s=50, label='Cluster 1')
plt.scatter(X_scaled[y_pred == 1, 0], X_scaled[y_pred == 1, 1], s=50, label='Cluster 2')
plt.scatter(X_scaled[y_pred == 2, 0], X_scaled[y_pred == 2, 1], s=50, label='Cluster 3')
plt.scatter(X_scaled[y_pred == 3, 0], X_scaled[y_pred == 3, 1], s=50, label='Cluster 4')
plt.scatter(kmeans.cluster_centers_[:, 0], kmeans.cluster_centers_[:, 1],
            s=200, c='black', marker='X', label='Centroids')
plt.title('K-Means Clustering Result')
plt.legend()
plt.show()

# Step 7: Evaluate using Silhouette Score
from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_score
score = silhouette_score(X_scaled, y_pred)
print(f"Silhouette Score: {score:.3f}")
```

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use What

| **Method** | **Best For** | **Limitation** | **KTU Tip** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Elbow Method | Visual elbow detection | Subjective, not always clear | Use when $k$ is unknown |
| Silhouette Score | Quantitative cluster validation | Computationally expensive for large $n$ | Range $-1$ to $+1$, higher is better |
| Davies-Bouldin Index | Lower-is-better validation | Sensitive to outliers | Good for comparing models |
| Gap Statistic | Statistical $k$ selection | Requires reference distribution | Use in advanced experiments |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — K-Means Algorithm Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input Dataset X] --> B[Choose number of clusters k]
    B --> C[Initialize k Centroids using K-Means++]
    C --> D[Assignment Step: Compute distance from each point to every centroid]
    D --> E[Assign each point to nearest cluster]
    E --> F[Update Step: Recalculate centroid as mean of cluster points]
    F --> G{Convergence Check}
    G -- No Centroids changed --> H[Output Final Clusters and Centroids]
    G -- Centroid shifted --> I{Iteration limit reached?}
    I -- No --> D
    I -- Yes --> H
    H --> J[Visualize clusters and evaluate metrics]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style C fill:#fff4e1
    style D fill:#ffe1f5
    style F fill:#ffe1f5
    style G fill:#e1ffe1
    style H fill:#d4edda
    style J fill:#f8d7da
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Clustering Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Dataset Source: CSV / make_blobs / Iris] --> B[Data Preprocessing Module]
    B --> B1[Handle Missing Values]
    B --> B2[Feature Scaling StandardScaler]
    B --> B3[Outlier Detection]
    B1 --> C[Optimal k Selection Module]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1[Elbow Method WCSS Plot]
    C --> C2[Silhouette Analysis]
    C1 --> D[K-Means Engine]
    C2 --> D
    D --> D1[Scikit-learn KMeans Model]
    D --> D2[Custom Implementation NumPy]
    D1 --> E[Evaluation Module]
    D2 --> E
    E --> E1[Silhouette Score]
    E --> E2[Davies-Bouldin Index]
    E --> E3[Inertia Value]
    E1 --> F[Visualization Layer]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> F1[2D Scatter Plot with Centroids]
    F --> F2[Elbow Curve Plot]
    F1 --> G[Lab Report Output]
    F2 --> G

    style A fill:#cfe2ff
    style D fill:#fff3cd
    style F fill:#d1e7dd
    style G fill:#f8d7da
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Cluster Assignment Intuition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Data Point P1] --> Q1{Distance to all centroids}
    P2[Data Point P2] --> Q1
    P3[Data Point P3] --> Q1
    P4[Data Point P4] --> Q1
    Q1 --> R1[Closest centroid is C1]
    Q1 --> R2[Closest centroid is C2]
    Q1 --> R3[Closest centroid is C3]
    R1 --> A1[Cluster 1 Forms]
    R2 --> A2[Cluster 2 Forms]
    R3 --> A3[Cluster 3 Forms]
    A1 --> S1[Update centroid: mean of C1 points]
    A2 --> S2[Update centroid: mean of C2 points]
    A3 --> S3[Update centroid: mean of C3 points]
    S1 --> T[Convergence Check]
    S2 --> T
    S3 --> T
    T --> U{Stable?}
    U -- Yes --> V[Final Output]
    U -- No --> Q1

    style P1 fill:#e7f3ff
    style Q1 fill:#fff4e1
    style A1 fill:#d4edda
    style A2 fill:#d4edda
    style A3 fill:#d4edda
    style V fill:#cfe2ff
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define clustering. Differentiate between supervised and unsupervised learning.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Clustering** is an unsupervised learning technique that groups unlabeled data points into clusters based on similarity, with no predefined target labels.
- **Supervised Learning** uses labeled training data (input-output pairs) to learn a mapping function, e.g., classification, regression.
- **Unsupervised Learning** uses unlabeled data to discover hidden patterns, e.g., clustering, dimensionality reduction.
- The **key difference**: supervised learning has a *teacher signal* (labels), while unsupervised learning has none.

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**What is the K-Means clustering algorithm? State its objective function.**

**Model Answer:**
- K-Means is a **centroid-based partitioning algorithm** that divides $n$ data points into $k$ clusters by iteratively assigning points to the nearest centroid and updating centroids.
- **Objective function (WCSS):**

$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{k} w_{ij} \cdot \lVert x_i - \mu_j \rVert^2$$

- The goal is to **minimize $J$** to achieve compact, well-separated clusters.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure of the K-Means clustering algorithm with a suitable block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following 2D data points: $A(1,1)$, $B(1,2)$, $C(2,1)$, $D(8,8)$, $E(9,8)$, $F(9,9)$. Apply K-Means with $k=2$, initial centroids $(1,1)$ and $(8,8)$, for **two iterations**. Show cluster assignments and updated centroids. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution A(a):**

The K-Means algorithm executes the following sequence:

1. **Input:** Dataset $X = \{x_1, x_2, ..., x_n\}$, number of clusters $k$.
2. **Initialize** $k$ centroids $\mu_1, \mu_2, ..., \mu_k$ (randomly or via K-Means++).
3. **Repeat** until convergence:
   - **Assignment Step:** For each $x_i$, assign it to the cluster with the nearest centroid:
     $$c^{(i)} = \arg \min_{j} \lVert x_i - \mu_j \rVert^2$$
   - **Update Step:** Recompute each centroid as the mean of assigned points:
     $$\mu_j = \frac{1}{\vert C_j \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} x_i$$
4. **Output:** Final cluster labels and centroid coordinates.

**Block Diagram** (refer to Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.1).

**Termination conditions:**
- Centroids stop changing.
- Maximum iterations reached.
- WCSS change $<\epsilon$.

### **Model Solution A(b):**

**Initial State:** $k = 2$, $\mu_1 = (1, 1)$, $\mu_2 = (8, 8)$.

**Iteration 1 — Distance Computation:**

| Point | $d$ to $\mu_1$ | $d$ to $\mu_2$ | Assignment |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A(1,1)$ | $0.00$ | $9.90$ | $C_1$ |
| $B(1,2)$ | $1.00$ | $9.55$ | $C_1$ |
| $C(2,1)$ | $1.00$ | $9.90$ | $C_1$ |
| $D(8,8)$ | $9.90$ | $0.00$ | $C_2$ |
| $E(9,8)$ | $10.63$ | $1.00$ | $C_2$ |
| $F(9,9)$ | $11.31$ | $1.41$ | $C_2$ |

**Iteration 1 — Update Centroids:**

$$\mu_1^{new} = \frac{1}{3}\left[(1,1) + (1,2) + (2,1)\right] = \frac{1}{3}(4, 4) = (1.33, 1.33)$$

$$\mu_2^{new} = \frac{1}{3}\left[(8,8) + (9,8) + (9,9)\right] = \frac{1}{3}(26, 25) = (8.67, 8.33)$$

**Iteration 2 — Distance Computation:**

| Point | $d$ to $\mu_1^{new}$ | $d$ to $\mu_2^{new}$ | Assignment |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A(1,1)$ | $0.47$ | $10.80$ | $C_1$ |
| $B(1,2)$ | $0.75$ | $10.21$ | $C_1$ |
| $C(2,1)$ | $0.75$ | $9.43$ | $C_1$ |
| $D(8,8)$ | $9.43$ | $0.75$ | $C_2$ |
| $E(9,8)$ | $10.69$ | $0.47$ | $C_2$ |
| $F(9,9)$ | $11.34$ | $0.75$ | $C_2$ |

**Iteration 2 — Final Centroids:** $\mu_1 = (1.33, 1.33)$, $\mu_2 = (8.67, 8.33)$.

**Final Clusters:** $C_1 = \{A, B, C\}$, $C_2 = \{D, E, F\}$ (stable — no reassignment).

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss the **Elbow Method** for determining the optimal number of clusters in K-Means. How is the WCSS plotted and interpreted? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Implement the K-Means clustering algorithm in Python using the Scikit-learn library on the Iris dataset. Plot the resulting clusters and compute the **Silhouette Score**. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution B(a):**

**Elbow Method — Detailed Explanation:**

The Elbow Method is a heuristic to find the optimal $k$ by plotting the **WCSS (Within-Cluster Sum of Squares)** against $k$.

- **WCSS Formula:**

$$WCSS(k) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} \lVert x_i - \mu_j \rVert^2$$

- As $k$ increases, WCSS **always decreases** (more clusters = tighter fit).
- The "elbow point" is where the **rate of decrease sharply changes** — adding more clusters beyond this gives diminishing returns.
- This elbow represents the **optimal trade-off** between cluster compactness and simplicity.

**Procedure:**
1. Run K-Means for $k = 1, 2, 3, ..., k_{max}$ (e.g., 10).
2. Record WCSS for each $k$.
3. Plot $k$ on x-axis, WCSS on y-axis.
4. Visually identify the elbow (knee) point.

**Limitations:**
- Subjective when elbow is not sharp.
- For multiple datasets, use the **Silhouette Score** as a quantitative supplement.

### **Model Solution B(b):**

```python
# Import required libraries
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_score

# Load Iris dataset
iris = load_iris()
X = iris.data
y_true = iris.target  # only for comparison, not used in clustering

# Standardize features
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

# Apply K-Means with k=3 (Iris has 3 species)
kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=3, init='k-means++', n_init=10, random_state=42)
y_pred = kmeans.fit_predict(X_scaled)

# Compute Silhouette Score
score = silhouette_score(X_scaled, y_pred)
print(f"Silhouette Score: {score:.3f}")

# Visualize clusters using first two features
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
plt.scatter(X_scaled[y_pred == 0, 0], X_scaled[y_pred == 0, 1], s=80, label='Cluster 0', c='red')
plt.scatter(X_scaled[y_pred == 1, 0], X_scaled[y_pred == 1, 1], s=80, label='Cluster 1', c='blue')
plt.scatter(X_scaled[y_pred == 2, 0], X_scaled[y_pred == 2, 1], s=80, label='Cluster 2', c='green')
plt.scatter(kmeans.cluster_centers_[:, 0], kmeans.cluster_centers_[:, 1],
            s=200, c='black', marker='X', label='Centroids')
plt.title(f'K-Means Clustering on Iris Dataset (Silhouette={score:.3f})')
plt.xlabel('Feature 1 (Standardized)')
plt.ylabel('Feature 2 (Standardized)')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.show()
```

**Expected Output:** Silhouette Score $\approx$ **0.46–0.55**, three well-separated clusters visible in the 2D plot (using first two principal features).

**Inference:** K-Means successfully identifies the three natural groupings in the Iris dataset, with a moderate-to-good silhouette score confirming reasonable cluster cohesion and separation.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Lab/ESE Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to standardize features** before applying K-Means — leads to biased clusters dominated by high-magnitude features. Always use `StandardScaler` or `MinMaxScaler`.
> 2. **Not specifying `random_state`** in KMeans — produces different results on every run, making outputs non-reproducible. Always set `random_state=42`.
> 3. **Using `n_init=1`** (default in older scikit-learn) — poor initialization can trap K-Means in local minima. Use `n_init=10` or `init='k-means++'`.
> 4. **Confusing WCSS with Silhouette Score** — WCSS is a loss (lower is better, no fixed range), Silhouette is a quality metric ($-1$ to $+1$, higher is better).
> 5. **Skipping the Elbow Method plot** in the lab record — the question often asks for "optimal $k$ determination" without the plot. Always include the elbow curve.
> 6. **Not labeling axes or legends** in the cluster scatter plot — KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks for unlabeled plots.
> 7. **Using $k$ equal to the number of classes** without justification — always validate $k$ using elbow/silhouette analysis.
> 8. **Mixing up clustering with classification** — clustering has no ground truth labels during training; the algorithm discovers structure autonomously.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — K-Means Clustering**

- **Core Idea:** Partition $n$ points into $k$ clusters by minimizing WCSS — an **unsupervised, iterative, centroid-based** algorithm.
- **Key Steps:** Initialize → Assign (nearest centroid) → Update (mean) → Check convergence → Repeat.
- **Objective Function:** $J = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{k} w_{ij} \cdot \lVert x_i - \mu_j \rVert^2$ — minimize this.
- **Distance Metric:** Euclidean distance is the default; Manhattan and Minkowski are alternatives.
- **Centroid Update Rule:** $\mu_j = \frac{1}{\vert C_j \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} x_i$ (mean of assigned points).
- **Convergence:** When centroids stop moving, or $\Delta < \epsilon$, or max iterations reached.
- **Choosing $k$:** Elbow Method (visual) + Silhouette Score (quantitative, range $-1$ to $+1$).
- **Initialization:** Prefer `k-means++` over random for faster, more stable convergence.
- **Preprocessing:** **Always** scale features using `StandardScaler` or `MinMaxScaler` before clustering.
- **Evaluation Metrics:** Silhouette Score, Davies-Bouldin Index, Inertia (WCSS), Gap Statistic.
- **Python Libraries:** `sklearn.cluster.KMeans`, `numpy`, `matplotlib`, `sklearn.datasets.make_blobs`, `sklearn.preprocessing.StandardScaler`.
- **Common Datasets for Lab:** Iris, make_blobs (synthetic), customer data CSV, Mall Customers dataset.
- **Algorithm Complexity:** $O(n \cdot k \cdot t \cdot d)$ where $n$ = points, $k$ = clusters, $t$ = iterations, $d$ = dimensions.
- **Limitations:** Sensitive to outliers, assumes spherical/equal-sized clusters, requires pre-specifying $k$, may converge to local minima.
- **Variants to Know:** K-Medoids (uses actual points as centroids, robust to outliers), Mini-Batch K-Means (faster for large data), Hierarchical Clustering (no need to pre-specify $k$).
- **Lab Viva Favorites:** "Why is standardization important?", "What happens if you choose $k$ too high?", "Difference between K-Means and K-Medoids?", "How does K-Means++ initialization work?".

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip for KTU 2024 Students:** Always end your lab record with an **"Inference"** section summarizing what the clusters represent in the real-world context (e.g., "Cluster 0 represents low-income, low-spending customers — ideal for budget marketing campaigns"). This reflects the **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** expectation of contextual interpretation, which carries weightage in the KTU 2024 evaluation rubric.
